parts.content: Define the term functional group in the context of organic chemistry.
parts.markscheme: An atom or group of atoms that gives an organic molecule its characteristic chemical properties/reactions. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the focus
steps.content: To define a functional group according to the IB standard, we need to address two components: what it **is** (its structure) and what it **does** (its function). This concept is foundational to **Topic S3.2: Functional groups**.

steps.title: Identify the structural aspect
steps.content: First, we look at the physical makeup. In organic chemistry, the backbone is usually a chain of carbon and hydrogen atoms. A functional group is a specific part of that molecule consisting of an **atom** (like a halogen) or a **group of atoms** (like the hydroxyl group, $-OH$).

highlights.text: An atom or group of atoms

steps.title: Identify the functional aspect
steps.content: Next, we explain its role. This specific structural unit is responsible for the **characteristic chemical properties** and **reactivity** of the molecule. For example, all alcohols behave similarly in reactions because they all contain the same hydroxyl functional group.

steps.title: State the final definition
steps.content:
Combining these two aspects gives us the full definition required for the mark:

**A functional group is an atom or group of atoms that gives an organic molecule its characteristic chemical properties and reactions.**

parts.content: Identify the functional group present in alcohols.
parts.markscheme: Hydroxyl group (accept $-\text{OH}$ or $\text{OH}$) <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the homologous series
steps.content: The question asks us to identify the specific functional group that characterizes the **alcohol** homologous series. In organic chemistry, functional groups are specific atoms or groups of atoms within molecules that are responsible for the characteristic chemical reactions of those molecules.

highlights.text: Identify the functional group present in alcohols.

steps.title: Recall the structural feature
steps.content:
From your study of **Topic S3.2: Functional groups**, you should recall that alcohols are organic compounds where a hydrogen atom of a hydrocarbon is replaced by an oxygen and hydrogen pair. 

This is represented chemically as:
$$-OH$$

steps.title: Name the functional group
steps.content:
By direct recall, the name of the $-OH$ group attached to a carbon chain in an alcohol is the **hydroxyl group**. 

It is important not to confuse this with the *hydroxide ion* ($OH^-$), which is found in inorganic bases. In organic molecules, it is a covalently bonded group.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
To earn the mark, you can provide either the name or the chemical formula of the group.

**Final Answer:**
Hydroxyl group (or $-OH$)

steps.title: Identify the compound class
steps.content: The question asks us to identify the specific functional group that characterizes the **alcohol** family of organic compounds.

highlights.text: Identify the functional group present in alcohols.

steps.title: Consult the Data Booklet
steps.content:
To find this, you should refer to **Section 10** of your IB Chemistry Data Booklet, which contains a table titled "Functional groups". 

This section is essential for **Topic 3.2 (Functional groups)**. Look down the "Class" column until you find **Alcohol**.

steps.title: Identify name and structure
steps.content:
In the row for alcohols, the Data Booklet provides the following information:

*   **Functional group name:** Hydroxyl
*   **Structural formula:** $-\text{OH}$

According to the markscheme, you can provide either the name or the formula to earn the mark.

steps.title: Recall a specific alcohol
steps.content:
To identify the functional group of alcohols, let's start with a specific example you likely already know from **Topic 3.2: Functional groups**: ethanol. Ethanol is the alcohol found in drinks and used as a fuel.

Its molecular formula is:
$$C_2H_5OH$$

steps.title: Analyze the structural formula
steps.content:
If we look at the expanded structural formula of ethanol, we can see how the atoms are connected:

$$\begin{aligned} H &\quad H \\ | &\quad | \\ H - C &- C - O - H \\ | &\quad | \\ H &\quad H \end{aligned}$$

Notice that the main chain is a group of carbons and hydrogens (an alkyl group), but there is one specific part that stands out and gives the molecule its alcohol properties.

steps.title: Identify the functional group
steps.content:
In the structure above, the part that differentiates an alcohol from a simple alkane is the oxygen atom bonded to a hydrogen atom. This is the part that reacts and determines the chemical properties of the sample.

By looking at this specific example, we can deduce that the characteristic group for all alcohols is the **$-\text{OH}$** group.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content: According to the IB syllabus and the markscheme, this functional group is named the **hydroxyl group**. You can provide either the name or the chemical symbol to earn the mark.

parts.content: Name the functional group present in carboxylic acids and draw its displayed structure.
parts.markscheme:
Name: Carboxyl (group) <marks>A1</marks>

Displayed structure showing all atoms and all bonds ($\text{C=O}$, $\text{C-O}$ and $\text{O-H}$):

![Carboxyl group displayed structure](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/9a20f3f4-8635-4a77-8cf9-4661715c28f1.jpeg) <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the functional group
steps.content:
To answer this question, we first need to recall the nomenclature for organic families. Carboxylic acids are characterized by a specific group of atoms that defines their chemical behavior. In IB chemistry, this is known as the **carboxyl** group.

This concept is part of **Topic S3.2: Functional groups**. Identifying these groups is a fundamental skill for predicting the reactivity and properties of organic molecules.

steps.title: Recall the displayed structure
steps.content:
The question specifically asks for a **displayed structure**. In IB exams, this is a technical requirement meaning you must show **every single atom** and **every single bond** explicitly. 

You cannot use condensed notation like $-\text{COOH}$ or even $-\text{OH}$ because those hide the bonds between oxygen and hydrogen.

steps.title: Construct the carboxyl group
steps.content:
The carboxyl group consists of a central carbon atom bonded to two different oxygen-containing components:

1. A carbonyl group: A carbon double-bonded to an oxygen ($$\text{C=O}$$).
2. A hydroxyl group: A carbon single-bonded to an oxygen, which is then single-bonded to a hydrogen ($$\text{C-O-H}$$).

When we draw this, we ensure the carbon has four bonds in total (one to the rest of the molecule, two to the carbonyl oxygen, and one to the hydroxyl oxygen).

steps.title: Final Result and Marking
steps.content:
To earn both marks, ensure your final answer looks like this:

**Name:** Carboxyl group

**Structure:**

$$\begin{matrix} \quad \text{O} \\ \quad // \\ \text{R}-\text{C} \\ \quad \backslash \\ \quad \text{O}-\text{H} \end{matrix}$$

*(Note: The 'R' represents the rest of the carbon chain, though for just the functional group, you can leave the bond leading to the left open).* 

The markscheme awards 1 mark for the name and 1 mark for the drawing showing the $$\text{C=O}$$, $$\text{C-O}$$, and $$\text{O-H}$$ bonds clearly.

steps.title: Recall a specific molecule
steps.content:
To identify the functional group, let's start by thinking of a common carboxylic acid you might recognize, such as **ethanoic acid** (found in vinegar). Its molecular formula is $CH_3COOH$.

This falls under **Topic S3.2: Functional groups** in your syllabus. If you can remember the structure of one member of a homologous series, you can always find the functional group!

steps.title: Draw the full structure
steps.content:
Now, let's draw the full displayed structure of ethanoic acid to see how the atoms are connected. A displayed structure must show **every single bond**:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{H} & \quad \text{O} \\ | & \quad // \\ \text{H}-\text{C}&-\text{C} \\ | & \quad \backslash \\ \text{H} & \quad \text{O}-\text{H} \end{aligned}$$

Notice the unique arrangement at the end of the carbon chain.

steps.title: Isolate the functional group
steps.content:
In organic chemistry, the functional group is the specific part of the molecule responsible for its characteristic chemical reactions. 

If we remove the methyl group ($-CH_3$) from ethanoic acid, we are left with the part that makes it an acid. This group is called the **carboxyl group**.

According to the markscheme, the name required for the first mark is:
- **Carboxyl (group)**

steps.title: Draw the final structure
steps.content:
For the second mark, we need to draw the **displayed structure** of just the carboxyl group. 

**Important:** In IB Chemistry, a "displayed structure" means you must show all atoms and **all bonds**, including the bond between the Oxygen and the Hydrogen ($O-H$).

$$\begin{aligned} &\quad \text{O} \\ &\quad // \\ -\text{C}& \\ &\quad \backslash \\ &\quad \text{O}-\text{H} \end{aligned}$$

(The bond on the left of the Carbon indicates where it attaches to the rest of the carbon chain).

highlights.text: Displayed structure showing all atoms and all bonds ( C=O ,  C-O  and  O-H )

parts.content: State the empirical formula and the molecular formula of ethenone.
parts.markscheme:
- Empirical formula: $\text{C}_2\text{H}_2\text{O}$
- Molecular formula: $\text{C}_2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ <marks>1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Analyze the structural diagram
steps.content:
To find the molecular formula, we need to count the total number of atoms for each element shown in the structural diagram of ethenone. 

Let's break it down by element:
- **Carbon (C):** Looking at the central chain, there are 2 carbon atoms.
- **Hydrogen (H):** There are 2 hydrogen atoms bonded to the left-hand carbon.
- **Oxygen (O):** There is 1 oxygen atom double-bonded to the right-hand carbon.

steps.title: Determine the molecular formula
steps.content:
The molecular formula shows the actual number of atoms of each element in a single molecule. Based on our count, we have 2 carbons, 2 hydrogens, and 1 oxygen.

$$\text{Molecular formula: C}_2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

steps.title: Simplify for empirical formula
steps.content:
The empirical formula is the simplest whole-number ratio of the atoms in a compound. 

We look at the subscripts in the molecular formula $\text{C}_2\text{H}_2\text{O}$. The ratio of $\text{C}:\text{H}:\text{O}$ is $2:2:1$. Since the number of oxygen atoms is 1, this ratio cannot be simplified into smaller whole numbers. 

Therefore, the empirical formula is identical to the molecular formula:
$$\text{Empirical formula: C}_2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
Both formulas for ethenone are the same in this case:
- **Empirical formula:** $\text{C}_2\text{H}_2\text{O}$
- **Molecular formula:** $\text{C}_2\text{H}_2\text{O}$

This is a common occurrence in small organic molecules where the ratio is already in its simplest form. This topic is covered in **S1.4.4 Empirical and molecular formulas**.

steps.title: Analyze the condensed formula
steps.content: To find the molecular formula, we start by looking at the condensed formula provided in the question: $\text{CH}_2\text{CO}$. We need to count the total number of atoms for each element present in this string.

steps.title: Sum the individual atoms
steps.content:
Let's sum the atoms from the string $\text{CH}_2\text{CO}$:

- **Carbon (C):** There is $1$ carbon in the $\text{CH}_2$ group and $1$ carbon in the $\text{CO}$ group.
$$\text{Total C} = 1 + 1 = 2$$
- **Hydrogen (H):** There are $2$ hydrogens in the $\text{CH}_2$ group.
$$\text{Total H} = 2$$
- **Oxygen (O):** There is $1$ oxygen in the $\text{CO}$ group.
$$\text{Total O} = 1$$

steps.title: State the molecular formula
steps.content:
The molecular formula represents the actual number of atoms of each element in a molecule. Combining our totals from the previous step, we get:

$$\text{Molecular Formula} = \text{C}_2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

steps.title: Determine the empirical formula
steps.content:
The empirical formula is the simplest whole-number ratio of atoms in a compound. We look at the ratio of $\text{C}:\text{H}:\text{O}$ from the molecular formula:

$$\text{Ratio} = 2 : 2 : 1$$

Since $1$ is the smallest number and cannot be divided further into a whole number, this ratio is already in its simplest form. Therefore, the empirical formula is the same as the molecular formula.

$$\text{Empirical Formula} = \text{C}_2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

parts.content: Identify the types of bonds present between the atoms in ethenone.
parts.markscheme:
- One $\text{C}=\text{C}$ double bond (one $\sigma$ and one $\pi$ bond) <marks>1</marks>
- One $\text{C}=\text{O}$ double bond (one $\sigma$ and one $\pi$ bond) <marks>1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the structural formula
steps.content: To identify the types of bonds, we first look directly at the provided structural formula of ethenone ($CH_2CO$). By observing the lines between the atoms, we can see that there are two double bonds present in the molecule: one between the two carbon atoms ($C=C$) and one between the central carbon and the oxygen atom ($C=O$).

highlights.text: The structure of ethenone (CH2CO) is shown below:

steps.title: Classify the C=C bond
steps.content:
In IB Chemistry, we learn that every double bond is composed of two distinct types of covalent bonds. For the $C=C$ double bond:

- The first bond is a **sigma ($\sigma$) bond**, formed by the head-on overlap of orbitals.
- The second bond is a **pi ($\pi$) bond**, formed by the sideways overlap of p-orbitals.

So, the $C=C$ double bond consists of one $\sigma$ and one $\pi$ bond.

highlights.text: One C=C double bond (one σ and one π bond)

steps.title: Classify the C=O bond
steps.content:
Following the same structural analysis for the $C=O$ double bond, we apply the same rule for multiple bonds:

- The $C=O$ double bond also consists of exactly **one sigma ($\sigma$) bond** and **one pi ($\pi$) bond**.

This concept is a key part of **Topic S2.2: The covalent model**, specifically focusing on how multiple bonds are structured.

highlights.text: One C=O double bond (one σ and one π bond)

steps.title: Final answer summary
steps.content:
To earn the full 2 marks, you must identify both double bonds and correctly state their composition:

- **1 mark**: $C=C$ double bond consists of one $\sigma$ and one $\pi$ bond.
- **1 mark**: $C=O$ double bond consists of one $\sigma$ and one $\pi$ bond.

(Note: While there are also $C-H$ single bonds present, they are simple $\sigma$ bonds and are usually not the primary focus when the markscheme emphasizes the double bond components.)

steps.title: Analyze electron domains
steps.content:
To determine the types of bonds using the hybridization approach, we first count the **electron domains** around each atom in the ethenone molecule ($CH_2=C=O$):

*   **Terminal Carbon ($C_1$):** Bonded to two Hydrogens and one Carbon. It has **3 electron domains**, which corresponds to **$sp^2$ hybridization**.
*   **Central Carbon ($C_2$):** Bonded to one Carbon and one Oxygen. It has **2 electron domains** (both are double bonds), which corresponds to **$sp$ hybridization**.
*   **Oxygen atom ($O$):** Bonded to one Carbon and has two lone pairs. It has **3 electron domains**, which corresponds to **$sp^2$ hybridization**.

This knowledge is found in **Topic S2.2: The covalent model**.

highlights.text: Identify the types of bonds present between the atoms in ethenone.

steps.title: Evaluate the C=C bond
steps.content:
In the $C=C$ double bond, the atoms overlap their hybridized orbitals ($sp^2$ from the terminal carbon and $sp$ from the central carbon) head-on to form a **sigma ($\sigma$) bond**. 

The remaining unhybridized $p$-orbitals on each carbon atom overlap sideways to form a **pi ($\pi$) bond**. 

Therefore, the $C=C$ double bond consists of **one $\sigma$ and one $\pi$ bond**.

highlights.text: One C=C double bond (one σ and one π bond)

steps.title: Evaluate the C=O bond
steps.content:
Similarly, for the $C=O$ double bond, the central carbon ($sp$) and the oxygen ($sp^2$) overlap head-on to create a **sigma ($\sigma$) bond**. 

Because the central carbon is $sp$ hybridized, it has two $p$-orbitals available. One is used for the $\pi$ bond with the terminal carbon, and the second one (oriented at $90^\circ$ to the first) overlaps sideways with a $p$-orbital on the oxygen to form another **pi ($\pi$) bond**.

Thus, the $C=O$ double bond also consists of **one $\sigma$ and one $\pi$ bond**.

highlights.text: One C=O double bond (one σ and one π bond)

steps.title: Final bond identification
steps.content:
To summarize all bonds in the molecule for the full 2 marks:

1.  The **$C=C$ double bond** is composed of one $\sigma$ and one $\pi$ bond.
2.  The **$C=O$ double bond** is composed of one $\sigma$ and one $\pi$ bond.
3.  (Note: The two $C-H$ bonds are single $\sigma$ bonds, though the markscheme focuses on the double bonds).

parts.content:
Ethenone is classified as a compound with two functional groups. 

Identify the two functional groups present in the molecule.
parts.markscheme:
- Alkene ($\text{C}=\text{C}$) <marks>1</marks>
- Carbonyl group ($\text{C}=\text{O}$), specifically part of a ketene functional group <marks>1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the molecular structure
steps.content: To solve this, we will use the **Structural Recognition** approach. This involves looking at the Lewis structure of ethenone and identifying specific bonding patterns that correspond to known functional groups listed in **Topic S3.2: Functional groups**.

highlights.text: Identify the two functional groups present in the molecule.

steps.title: Identify the alkene group
steps.content:
Looking at the left side of the molecule, we can see a double bond between the two carbon atoms. In IB organic chemistry, a carbon-to-carbon double bond is the defining feature of an **alkene**.

$$\text{C}=\text{C}$$

steps.title: Identify the carbonyl group
steps.content:
Now, look at the right side of the molecule. There is a double bond between the central carbon atom and the oxygen atom. This structural feature is known as a **carbonyl group**.

$$\text{C}=\text{O}$$

While this specific arrangement (where a single carbon is double-bonded to both another carbon and an oxygen) makes the molecule a "ketene," for your IB exams, identifying it as a carbonyl group is the standard requirement.

highlights.text: Carbonyl group (\text{C}=\text{O}), specifically part of a ketene functional group

steps.title: Deconstruct the IUPAC name
steps.content:
To identify the functional groups, we can break down the systematic name **ethenone** into its constituent IUPAC parts. Each part of the name tells us something specific about the molecule's structure.

- **eth-**: Indicates a chain of 2 carbon atoms.
- **-en-**: Indicates the presence of a carbon-carbon double bond.
- **-one**: Indicates the presence of a carbonyl group.

steps.title: Identify the alkene group
steps.content:
The suffix **-en-** is characteristic of the **alkene** functional group. In the structure of ethenone ($CH_2CO$), this corresponds to the double bond between the two carbon atoms:

$$C=C$$

In your IB studies, you'll recognize this from **Topic S3.2: Functional groups**.

steps.title: Identify the carbonyl group
steps.content:
The suffix **-one** is used in IUPAC nomenclature to denote a **carbonyl group** ($C=O$), typically associated with ketones. 

Looking at the provided structure, we see the carbon atom double-bonded to an oxygen atom. This confirms the presence of the carbonyl group. In this specific arrangement where the carbonyl is adjacent to an alkene, the molecule is known as a **ketene**.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
Based on the analysis of the name 'ethenone', the two functional groups are:

1.  **Alkene** (from the '-en-' part)
2.  **Carbonyl group** (from the '-one' part)

These two identifications will earn you both marks according to the markscheme.

steps.title: Analyze the molecular structure
steps.content:
To identify the functional groups, we first look at the structural formula of ethenone ($\text{CH}_2\text{CO}$). 

By examining the diagram, we can see two distinct double bonds connected to the central carbon atom:
1. A double bond between two carbon atoms ($\text{C}=\text{C}$).
2. A double bond between a carbon atom and an oxygen atom ($\text{C}=\text{O}$).

steps.title: Reference the Carbon-Carbon bond
steps.content:
Now, we look at the **Identification of functional groups** table in your IB Chemistry Data Booklet. 

We look for the structural motif consisting of a carbon-carbon double bond ($\text{C}=\text{C}$). The table identifies this group and its corresponding class of organic compounds as an **alkene**.

steps.title: Reference the Carbon-Oxygen bond
steps.content:
Next, we search the Data Booklet table for the oxygen-containing motif. 

The $\text{C}=\text{O}$ structural unit is identified as a **carbonyl** group. 

*Note: While this specific arrangement (where the carbonyl is part of a $\text{C}=\text{C}=\text{O}$ system) is known as a ketene, for the IB exam, identifying the primary functional group as a carbonyl group is the standard requirement.*

steps.title: Finalize the two groups
steps.content:
Based on our comparison with the Data Booklet, the two functional groups present in ethenone are:

1. **Alkene** (from the $\text{C}=\text{C}$ bond)
2. **Carbonyl** (from the $\text{C}=\text{O}$ bond)

Each identification earns 1 mark, for a total of 2 marks.

parts.content:
Ethenone is a reactive molecule. 

Predict its polarity based on molecular geometry and electronegativity.
parts.markscheme:
- Ethenone is polar <marks>1</marks>
- The $\text{C}=\text{O}$ bond is highly polar due to oxygen's greater electronegativity <marks>1</marks>
- Molecular shape around the central carbon is linear, but asymmetry from the polar $\text{C}=\text{O}$ bond causes an overall dipole moment

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze bond polarity
steps.content:
To determine if the molecule is polar, we first look at the electronegativity of the atoms involved. Oxygen is significantly more electronegative than carbon. 

This difference creates a strong **bond dipole** in the $C=O$ group, where electron density is pulled toward the oxygen atom, making it partially negative ($\delta-$) and the carbon partially positive ($\delta+$).

$$ 	ext{Electronegativity: } \text{O} > \text{C} > \text{H} $$

steps.title: Determine molecular geometry
steps.content:
Next, we apply **VSEPR theory** to find the shape. 

Looking at the central carbon atom (the one in the middle of the $C=C=O$ chain), it has two electron domains (two double bonds) and no lone pairs. According to VSEPR theory, this results in a **linear** geometry around that carbon with a bond angle of $180^\circ$.

The terminal carbon ($CH_2$) has three electron domains, giving it a trigonal planar geometry.

steps.title: Evaluate dipole cancellation
steps.content:
For a molecule to be non-polar, the bond dipoles must be equal and opposite so that they cancel out. 

In ethenone, the molecule is asymmetric. On one side of the central carbon, we have a very polar $C=O$ bond. On the other side, we have a $C=CH_2$ group. Since the atoms and bond polarities on either side of the central carbon are different, the dipoles **do not cancel out**.

steps.title: Predict final polarity
steps.content:
Because there is a net dipole moment pointing towards the oxygen atom, we can conclude that the molecule is **polar**.

According to the markscheme, you earn one mark for stating it is polar and another for explaining that the $C=O$ bond is highly polar due to oxygen's electronegativity.

steps.title: Analyze bond polarity
steps.content: To determine if ethenone is polar, we first look at the individual bonds. Oxygen is significantly more electronegative than carbon. This difference creates a highly polar $\text{C}=\text{O}$ bond, where the electron density is pulled toward the oxygen atom, creating a bond dipole.

highlights.text: The $\text{C}=\text{O}$ bond is highly polar due to oxygen's greater electronegativity

steps.title: Evaluate molecular symmetry
steps.content:
Next, we examine the molecular geometry using the **Molecular Symmetry Analysis** approach. The molecule consists of a $\text{CH}_2$ group at one end and a terminal oxygen atom at the other. 

While the arrangement around the central carbon is linear (the $\text{C}=\text{C}=\text{O}$ axis), the molecule is **asymmetric** along its main axis because the atoms at either end are different ($H$ and $C$ on one side vs $O$ on the other).

steps.title: Determine net dipole moment
steps.content: In a perfectly symmetrical molecule (like $\text{CO}_2$), individual bond dipoles cancel each other out. However, in ethenone, the presence of the terminal oxygen atom creates an asymmetric distribution of electron density. Because the bond dipoles do not cancel, the molecule has a **permanent dipole moment**.

highlights.text: asymmetry from the polar $\text{C}=\text{O}$ bond causes an overall dipole moment

steps.title: State the final prediction
steps.content:
Since the molecule has an asymmetric shape and a non-zero net dipole moment, we can conclude that **ethenone is polar**. 

This concept is a key part of **Topic S2.2: The covalent model** (specifically S2.2.6 Molecular polarity). If you're stuck on why some molecules are non-polar despite having polar bonds, reviewing VSEPR theory and symmetry is a great next step!

labeledSubSubTopics.title: S3.2.7 Stereoisomers (Higher Level Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: All parts focus on stereochemistry: defining a chiral (asymmetric) carbon, counting stereoisomers with two chiral centers, and optical activity/interaction of enantiomers with plane-polarized light.

parts.content: Explain what is meant by the term chiral carbon.
parts.markscheme: Carbon atom bonded to four different atoms or groups [1]

steps.title: Identify the structural requirement
steps.content: In organic chemistry, specifically within **Topic S3.2.7: Stereoisomers**, we identify a carbon atom's chirality by looking at the four single bonds it forms. For a carbon atom to be considered "chiral," it must have a specific arrangement that allows for non-superimposable mirror images.

steps.title: Define the chiral center
steps.content:
The structural definition of a **chiral carbon** is a carbon atom that is bonded to **four different atoms or groups**. 

If any two groups attached to the carbon are the same (for example, two hydrogen atoms), the carbon is no longer chiral and is instead called "achiral."

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
To earn the mark in an IB exam, your definition must clearly state both the atom involved and the variety of its substituents:

**A carbon atom bonded to four different atoms or groups.**

steps.title: Define the Chiral Carbon
steps.content:
A chiral carbon (often called an asymmetric carbon) is a carbon atom that is bonded to **four different atoms or groups**.

This concept is a key part of **Topic S3.2: Functional groups** (specifically stereoisomerism in HL), where we look at how the spatial arrangement of atoms affects a molecule's properties.

steps.title: Analyze Internal Symmetry
steps.content:
Using the **symmetry approach**, we describe this carbon as an **asymmetric center**. 

Because the carbon is attached to four unique groups, the molecule **lacks an internal plane of symmetry**. This means there is no way to divide the molecule into two identical halves that reflect each other.

steps.title: Explain Non-Superimposability
steps.content:
Because the molecule lacks this internal symmetry, it becomes **non-superimposable** on its mirror image. 

Think of it like your left and right hands: they are mirror images of each other, but no matter how you rotate them, they will never perfectly overlap (superimpose). This unique spatial property is what makes the carbon "chiral."

steps.title: Final Definition for Marks
steps.content:
To earn the mark in an IB exam, you must explicitly state the structural requirement:

**A chiral carbon is a carbon atom bonded to four different atoms or groups.**

parts.content: State the maximum number of stereoisomers for a compound with two chiral centers.
parts.markscheme: $2^2 = 4$ stereoisomers [1]

parts.content: Explain how enantiomers interact with plane-polarized light.
parts.markscheme:
They rotate plane-polarized light in opposite directions [1]
One is dextrorotatory, the other levorotatory [1]

steps.title: Identify Optical Activity
steps.content:
Enantiomers are a type of stereoisomer that are non-superimposable mirror images of each other. This specific property leads to **optical activity**, which is their ability to rotate the plane of plane-polarized light when it passes through a solution of the compound.

This concept is a key part of **Topic S3.2.7: Stereoisomers**, where we study how the 3D arrangement of atoms affects a molecule's properties.

steps.title: Observe Opposite Rotation
steps.content: The most significant way enantiomers interact with plane-polarized light is by rotating it in **opposite directions**. If one enantiomer rotates the light clockwise, its mirror image (the other enantiomer) will rotate it anticlockwise by the exact same number of degrees.

highlights.text: rotate plane-polarized light in opposite directions

steps.title: Define Specific Terms
steps.content:
To distinguish between these two directions, we use specific chemical terms:

- **Dextrorotatory**: The enantiomer that rotates the light to the right (clockwise), often labeled as $(+)$ or $d$.
- **Levorotatory**: The enantiomer that rotates the light to the left (anticlockwise), often labeled as $(-)$ or $l$.

highlights.text: One is dextrorotatory, the other levorotatory

steps.title: Summary for Marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this question, ensure you state both the behavior and the correct terminology:

1. Enantiomers rotate plane-polarized light in **opposite directions**.
2. One enantiomer is **dextrorotatory** and the other is **levorotatory**.

parts.content: Outline what a homologous series is.
parts.markscheme:
- Define the term: A group of compounds with the same functional group and general formula, where each successive member differs by a $\text{CH}_2$ unit. <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Analyze structural commonalities
steps.content: To understand what a homologous series is, we can look at the physical structures of the alcohols provided: methanol, ethanol, and propanol. In **Topic S3.2.3 (Homologous series)**, we learn that these molecules aren't just a random collection; they share specific structural characteristics that group them together.

steps.title: Identify the functional group
steps.content:
The first structural characteristic is that all members of the series possess the **same functional group**. 

Looking at the images:
- Methanol has an $-OH$ group.
- Ethanol has an $-OH$ group.
- Propanol has an $-OH$ group.

Because they share this functional group, they will have very similar chemical properties.

steps.title: Apply a general formula
steps.content:
The second structural characteristic is that they follow a **common general formula**. For the alcohols shown, we can calculate the number of Hydrogen atoms relative to Carbon atoms:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Methanol } (n=1) &: C_1H_{2(1)+1}OH = CH_3OH \\ \text{Ethanol } (n=2) &: C_2H_{2(2)+1}OH = C_2H_5OH \\ \text{Propanol } (n=3) &: C_3H_{2(3)+1}OH = C_3H_7OH \end{aligned}$$

All these molecules fit the general formula **$C_nH_{2n+1}OH$**.

ti84.instructions: Enter 2*1+1, then 2*2+1, then 2*3+1 on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Define a function f(n) = 2n+1 and evaluate for n=1, 2, 3.

calculator.description: Verifying the number of hydrogen atoms in the alkyl group for n=1, 2, and 3 using the 2n+1 rule.

steps.title: Observe the structural increment
steps.content:
Finally, we notice how the chain grows. If we subtract the formula of one member from the next, we see a constant difference:

$$\text{Ethanol } (C_2H_5OH) - \text{Methanol } (CH_3OH) = CH_2$$

In a homologous series, each successive member differs from the previous one by a **$CH_2$ unit**.

gdc.instructions: Subtract the hydrogen count of methanol from ethanol.

ti84.instructions: Enter 5 - 3 on the home screen.

calculator.description: Calculating the difference in Hydrogen atoms between ethanol (5) and methanol (3).

steps.title: Define homologous series
steps.content:
To earn the mark, we combine these structural observations into a single definition:

A **homologous series** is a group of compounds that have the **same functional group** and the **same general formula**, where each successive member differs by a **$CH_2$ unit**.

highlights.text: Define the term: A group of compounds with the same functional group and general formula, where each successive member differs by a CH2 unit.

steps.title: Analyze the molecule formulas
steps.content:
To understand a homologous series, let's look at the chemical formulas of the first two alcohols shown in the image:

*   **Methanol**: $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$ (or $\text{CH}_4\text{O}$)
*   **Ethanol**: $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$ (or $\text{C}_2\text{H}_6\text{O}$)

Both molecules belong to the same family because they share the same functional group ($-\text{OH}$). You can learn more about these classifications in **Topic S3.2: Functional groups**.

steps.title: Calculate the incremental difference
steps.content:
A key feature of a homologous series is the mathematical relationship between successive members. If we subtract the formula of methanol from ethanol, we can find the "incremental step" between them:

$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_6\text{O} - \text{CH}_4\text{O} = \text{CH}_2$$

This means that as the chain length increases, each new member adds exactly one carbon atom and two hydrogen atoms.

gdc.instructions: Calculate the difference in Carbon and Hydrogen atoms.

ti84.instructions: Subtract the number of atoms for each element (Carbon: 2-1, Hydrogen: 6-4).

calculator.description: Verify the difference in atoms between Ethanol (C2H6O) and Methanol (CH4O).

steps.title: Define the homologous series
steps.content:
Using this incremental progression, we can define a **homologous series**. To earn the mark, your definition should include these three core points:

1.  Compounds have the **same functional group**.
2.  They follow the **same general formula** (e.g., $\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+1}\text{OH}$ for alcohols).
3.  Each successive member differs by a **$\text{CH}_2$ unit**.

In an IB exam, mentioning the $\text{CH}_2$ difference is crucial for a complete answer.

highlights.text: successive member differs by a CH2 unit

steps.title: Analyze the provided examples
steps.content:
To understand what a homologous series is, let's look at the three molecules provided: methanol, ethanol, and propanol. 

- **Methanol**: One carbon atom with an $-\text{OH}$ group.
- **Ethanol**: Two carbon atoms with an $-\text{OH}$ group.
- **Propanol**: Three carbon atoms with an $-\text{OH}$ group.

All three share a common structure (the alcohol group), but they vary in the length of their carbon "spine."

highlights.text: three alcohols with increasing carbon chain length: methanol, ethanol and propanol

steps.title: Identify the shared features
steps.content:
Notice that all three molecules have the same **functional group**, which is the $-\text{OH}$ (hydroxyl) group. 

Because they have the same functional group, they also follow a **general formula**. For alcohols, this is $C_n H_{2n+1}OH$. Compounds in a homologous series share these chemical characteristics.

steps.title: Identify the incremental change
steps.content:
Look at the difference between successive members:
- From methanol ($CH_3OH$) to ethanol ($C_2H_5OH$), we add one carbon and two hydrogens.
- From ethanol ($C_2H_5OH$) to propanol ($C_3H_7OH$), we again add one carbon and two hydrogens.

This recurring unit is called a **$\text{CH}_2$ unit**. Each member in the series differs from the next by exactly this amount.

gdc.instructions: Subtract the molar mass of CH4O from C2H6O.

ti84.instructions: Calculate the molar mass of ethanol (C2H6O) and subtract the molar mass of methanol (CH4O).

desmos.instructions: Enter the expression to find the mass difference.

calculator.description: Verification of the mass difference between ethanol and methanol to confirm the CH2 unit.

steps.title: State the formal definition
steps.content:
Based on our observations, we can define a **homologous series** as a group of organic compounds that:
1. Have the same **functional group**.
2. Can be described by the same **general formula**.
3. Have each successive member differing by a **$\text{CH}_2$ unit**.

This concept is a fundamental part of **Topic S3.2: Functional groups** in your syllabus.

parts.content: State the name of the homologous series to which the compounds above belong.
parts.markscheme:
- State the series name: Alcohols. <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Analyze the question text
steps.content:
In IB Chemistry exams, the introductory text often contains the information you need to answer classification questions. This is a great example of where careful reading pays off! 

We are looking for the name of the **homologous series** (a family of compounds with the same functional group and general formula) for the molecules shown.

steps.title: Identify the key term
steps.content:
The introductory sentence of the question specification explicitly identifies the type of molecules being discussed. 

It states: "The image shows three **alcohols** with increasing carbon chain length..."

highlights.text: The image shows three alcohols with increasing carbon chain length: methanol, ethanol and propanol.

steps.title: State the series name
steps.content:
Since the question identifies the compounds as alcohols, the name of the homologous series they belong to is simply the **alcohols**.

In your exam, stating this single word earns you the mark according to the markscheme.

**Final Answer:**
Alcohols

steps.title: Analyze the molecule names
steps.content:
The question provides us with three specific names for the organic compounds shown in the image:

*   **Methanol**
*   **Ethanol**
*   **Propanol**

To identify the homologous series using the **IUPAC Suffix Identification** approach, we need to look closely at the ending (suffix) of these names. This is covered in **Topic S3.2: Functional groups** of your syllabus.

steps.title: Identify the common suffix
steps.content:
When we look at the names, we can see they all share the same ending:

$$\text{methan-} \mathbf{ol}$$
$$\text{ethan-} \mathbf{ol}$$
$$\text{propan-} \mathbf{ol}$$

In IUPAC nomenclature, the suffix **-ol** is reserved for molecules containing the hydroxyl ($-OH$) functional group.

steps.title: Determine the series name
steps.content:
The homologous series characterized by the **-ol** suffix and the presence of a hydroxyl group is the **alcohols**.

According to the markscheme, simply stating the name of the series is enough to earn the mark.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content: The compounds belong to the **alcohols** homologous series.

steps.title: Identify the common feature
steps.content:
To identify the homologous series, we first look at the structural diagrams provided for methanol, ethanol, and propanol. 

Each molecule has a common feature: a oxygen atom bonded to a hydrogen atom, represented as $-\text{OH}$, attached to the carbon chain.

steps.title: Recognize the functional group
steps.content:
In organic chemistry, specific arrangements of atoms are called functional groups. The $-\text{OH}$ group is known as the **hydroxyl group**. 

This group is the defining characteristic of this family of compounds. You can review this classification in **Topic S3.2: Functional groups** of your syllabus.

steps.title: State the homologous series
steps.content:
A homologous series is a family of organic compounds that share the same functional group and general formula. 

Compounds characterized by the presence of a hydroxyl ($-\text{OH}$) functional group belong to the **alcohols** homologous series.

parts.content: Describe the trend in boiling point as the number of carbon atoms increases in this series.
parts.markscheme:
- State the trend: Boiling point increases as the number of carbon atoms (chain length) increases. <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain the trend: This is due to stronger London dispersion forces as the molecular size (number of electrons) increases. <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the trend
steps.content:
In a homologous series like these alcohols, we observe a clear pattern as the molecules get larger. As the number of carbon atoms (the chain length) increases from methanol to ethanol to propanol, the **boiling point increases**.

This is the first mark in the markscheme, noting that boiling point is positively correlated with the number of carbon atoms.

steps.title: Explain using polarizability
steps.content:
To explain why this happens, we look at the electrons. As the carbon chain gets longer, the total number of electrons in the molecule increases. 

A larger number of electrons leads to a larger electron cloud that is more easily distorted. This property is known as **greater polarizability**. When the electron cloud is more polarizable, it creates stronger temporary dipoles, which result in **stronger London dispersion forces** between the molecules.

steps.title: Relate to boiling point
steps.content:
Because the London dispersion forces are stronger in larger molecules (like propanol compared to methanol), more energy is required to overcome these intermolecular forces to change the state from liquid to gas. Therefore, the boiling point is higher.

To summarize for your exam:
1. **Trend:** Boiling point increases as the number of carbons increases.
2. **Reason:** More electrons lead to greater polarizability and stronger London dispersion forces.

steps.title: Identify the trend
steps.content: By looking at the series from methanol to propanol, we can see that the length of the carbon chain is growing. As the number of carbon atoms in the chain increases, the **boiling point increases**.

highlights.text: Describe the trend in boiling point as the number of carbon atoms increases in this series.

steps.title: Analyze surface area
steps.content:
As we add more carbon atoms (increasing from methanol to ethanol to propanol), the molecule becomes physically longer. This increase in chain length results in a **larger molecular surface area**.

This concept is part of **Topic S3.2.4: Physical trends in homologous series**, which explains how physical properties change as we move through a series of similar organic compounds.

steps.title: Explain contact points
steps.content:
A larger surface area is important because it allows for more **contact points** between neighboring molecules. 

With more points of contact, there are more opportunities for **London dispersion forces** (a type of intermolecular force) to form between the molecules. Even though each individual dispersion force is weak, the cumulative effect of having many contact points makes the total attraction much stronger.

steps.title: Relate to boiling point
steps.content:
To boil a liquid, you must provide enough thermal energy to overcome these intermolecular forces and separate the molecules. 

Because the longer chains have more contact points and therefore stronger total London dispersion forces, **more energy is required** to break them apart, resulting in a higher boiling point.

steps.title: Final answer and marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this question, you need to state the trend and provide the explanation:

1.  **Trend:** The boiling point increases as the number of carbon atoms increases. <marks>A1</marks>
2.  **Explanation:** Increasing the chain length increases the molecular surface area, providing more contact points for stronger London dispersion forces. <marks>A1</marks>

highlights.text: Boiling point increases as the number of carbon atoms (chain length) increases.

parts.content:
All three alcohols are soluble in water, but solubility decreases with increasing carbon chain length.

Explain this trend in terms of intermolecular forces.
parts.markscheme:
- Describe the bonding: Alcohols form hydrogen bonds with water molecules due to the presence of the polar $\text{OH}$ group. <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain the decrease: As the non-polar hydrocarbon chain length increases, it disrupts the hydrogen bonding network in water, making the molecules less soluble. <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify hydrogen bonding
steps.content:
To understand why alcohols are soluble, we look at the hydroxyl ($-\text{OH}$) group. This group is highly polar because oxygen is much more electronegative than hydrogen. 

Because of this polarity, alcohols can form **hydrogen bonds** with water molecules. This attractive force allows the alcohol molecules to integrate into the water solvent, which is why methanol, ethanol, and propanol are all soluble.

highlights.text: All three alcohols are soluble in water

steps.title: Analyze the hydrocarbon chain
steps.content:
Alcohols also consist of a hydrocarbon chain (the alkyl group). Unlike the hydroxyl group, this part of the molecule is **non-polar** and cannot form hydrogen bonds. 

As we move from methanol ($1$ carbon) to propanol ($3$ carbons), the length of this non-polar hydrocarbon chain increases. This part of the molecule is considered "hydrophobic" (water-fearing) because it only interacts with water through weak London dispersion forces.

highlights.text: solubility decreases with increasing carbon chain length

steps.title: Explain the solubility trend
steps.content:
The decrease in solubility happens because the longer non-polar chains **disrupt the hydrogen bonding network** of the water molecules. 

As the chain grows, it takes more energy to push the water molecules apart to make room for the large non-polar group than is gained from the weak interactions the chain forms with water. Consequently, the "hydrophilic" (water-loving) effect of the $-\text{OH}$ group is increasingly outweighed by the "hydrophobic" effect of the carbon chain, leading to lower solubility.

parts.content:
Methanol and ethanol are both used as fuels.

State one advantage and one disadvantage of using alcohols as fuels.
parts.markscheme:
- State an advantage: Alcohol fuels are renewable (e.g., produced from biomass) or burn more cleanly (less soot/carbon monoxide). <marks>A1</marks>
- State a disadvantage: They have a lower energy density than hydrocarbons or their production may compete with food crops for land. <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the fuel source
steps.content:
To answer this from an environmental perspective, we first look at where these alcohols come from. Unlike traditional fossil fuels (like gasoline) which are finite, alcohols like ethanol are often produced as **biofuels** through the fermentation of biomass (plant matter). 

This topic is covered in **Reactivity 1.3: Energy from fuels**, specifically focusing on how biofuels offer an alternative to carbon-intensive energy sources.

steps.title: Identify the sustainability advantage
steps.content:
The primary advantage of using alcohols like ethanol as fuel is that they are **renewable**. 

Because they are produced from crops (such as corn or sugarcane) that can be regrown year after year, we are not depleting a limited resource. Furthermore, because plants absorb $CO_2$ during growth, biofuels are considered to have a lower net carbon footprint compared to fossil fuels.

highlights.text: Alcohol fuels are renewable (e.g., produced from biomass)

steps.title: Identify the land-use disadvantage
steps.content:
The main environmental and social disadvantage is the **competition for land**. 

Growing crops for fuel requires vast amounts of agricultural land. This often leads to a "food vs. fuel" conflict, where land that could be used to grow food for people is diverted to energy production, potentially increasing food prices or leading to deforestation to create new planting space.

highlights.text: their production may compete with food crops for land

steps.title: Final Answer Summary
steps.content:
To earn both marks, you should state:

*   **Advantage:** They are **renewable** resources (produced from biomass).
*   **Disadvantage:** Their production can **compete with food crops** for available agricultural land.

steps.title: Analyze fuel composition
steps.content: This question asks us to evaluate the use of alcohols (like methanol and ethanol) as fuels. To understand their performance, we look at their chemical structure. Alcohols contain an oxygen atom ($R-OH$), which distinguishes them from pure hydrocarbons (alkanes) that only contain carbon and hydrogen. This difference significantly impacts how they burn and how much energy they provide, which are key concepts in **Topic R1.3: Energy from fuels**.

highlights.text: Methanol and ethanol are both used as fuels.

steps.title: Evaluate emissions (Advantage)
steps.content: A major chemical advantage of alcohols is that they undergo **cleaner combustion**. Because alcohols already contain oxygen within their molecular structure, they require less atmospheric oxygen to achieve complete combustion. This results in a significant reduction in the production of **soot** (unburnt carbon) and carbon monoxide ($CO$) compared to traditional fossil fuels.

steps.title: Assess energy density (Disadvantage)
steps.content: From a performance perspective, alcohols have a **lower energy density** than hydrocarbons. Because alcohols are already partially oxidized (due to the presence of the $-OH$ group), they release less energy per gram or per liter when burned. This means a vehicle powered by ethanol would require a larger volume of fuel to travel the same distance as one powered by gasoline.

steps.title: Final answer summary
steps.content:
To earn the marks, you must state one clear advantage and one clear disadvantage:

*   **Advantage:** They burn more cleanly with less soot produced.
*   **Disadvantage:** They have a lower energy density compared to hydrocarbons.

parts.content: Draw the full structural formula of propanoic acid.
parts.markscheme:
- Draw the correct full structural formula showing all atoms and all bonds: <marks>A1</marks>

![Propanoic acid full structural formula](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/a71a5fd5-76da-49b8-a0f7-2863a5abdfa8.jpeg)

1 mark total

steps.title: Analyze the prefix
steps.content:
To name or draw organic molecules using the **Systematic IUPAC Analysis** approach, we first look at the prefix. The prefix **prop-** indicates that the longest continuous carbon chain contains **3 carbon atoms**. 

This is a fundamental part of **Topic 3.2.5: IUPAC nomenclature** which you can review if you're ever unsure about chain lengths.

steps.title: Identify the functional group
steps.content:
Next, we look at the suffix **-oic acid**. This suffix identifies the molecule as a carboxylic acid. 

In a carboxylic acid, the terminal carbon (Carbon-1) is part of a carboxyl group. This group consists of a carbon atom double-bonded to an oxygen atom ($C=O$) and single-bonded to a hydroxyl group ($-OH$).

steps.title: Construct the backbone
steps.content:
Now we draw the 3-carbon chain and include the carboxyl group on the end. Carbon always forms **four bonds**. 

1. **Carbon 1**: Double bond to $O$, single bond to $O-H$, and one bond to Carbon 2. Total = 4 bonds.
2. **Carbon 2**: Bonded to Carbon 1 and Carbon 3. It needs two more bonds to reach four, so we add two hydrogen atoms.
3. **Carbon 3**: Bonded only to Carbon 2. It needs three more bonds, so we add three hydrogen atoms.

steps.title: Draw full structural formula
steps.content:
The question asks for the **full structural formula**. This is a specific requirement in IB Chemistry: you must show **every single atom** and **every single bond** explicitly. This includes the bond between the oxygen and the hydrogen in the hydroxyl group ($O-H$).

$$\begin{matrix} & & \text{H} & & \text{H} & & \text{O} \\ & & | & & | & & // \\ \text{H} & - & \text{C} & - & \text{C} & - & \text{C} \\ & & | & & | & & \backslash \\ & & \text{H} & & \text{H} & & \text{O} - \text{H} \end{matrix}$$

If you leave out the bond between $O$ and $H$, or write it just as $-OH$, you may lose the mark for a 'full structural formula'.

highlights.text: Draw the correct full structural formula showing all atoms and all bonds

steps.title: Identify the condensed formula
steps.content:
To draw the full structural formula, we first look at the name and condensed formula for propanoic acid. The prefix "prop-" tells us there are 3 carbons in the chain, and the suffix "-oic acid" indicates it belongs to the carboxylic acid functional group ($-COOH$).

The condensed structural formula is:
$$CH_3CH_2COOH$$

steps.title: Lay out the carbon backbone
steps.content:
Start by drawing the three carbon atoms in a horizontal line, connected by single bonds. 

$$C - C - C$$

steps.title: Expand the alkyl groups
steps.content:
Next, we expand the first two segments of the condensed formula: $CH_3$ and $CH_2$. 

1. The first carbon is bonded to three hydrogen atoms ($CH_3$).
2. The second carbon is bonded to two hydrogen atoms ($CH_2$).

At this stage, your drawing should look like this:
$$\begin{matrix} H & H & \\ | & | & \\ H-C & -C & -C \\ | & | & \\ H & H & \end{matrix}$$

steps.title: Expand the carboxyl group
steps.content:
The final part of the formula is the carboxyl group ($-COOH$). In a **full structural formula**, you must show every single bond, including the double bond to oxygen and the single bond between oxygen and hydrogen.

- The third carbon forms a double bond with one oxygen atom ($C=O$).
- It also forms a single bond with another oxygen atom, which is then bonded to a hydrogen atom ($C-O-H$).

**Note:** Simply writing $-OH$ is often penalized in IB exams when a "full structural formula" is requested. You must show the bond between $O$ and $H$.

highlights.text: Draw the correct full structural formula showing all atoms and all bonds

steps.title: Final full structural formula
steps.content:
Combining all the pieces, we get the complete diagram. Ensure every Carbon has 4 bonds, every Oxygen has 2, and every Hydrogen has 1.

$$\begin{aligned} &\quad H \quad H \quad O \\ &\quad | \quad | \quad // \\ H - &C - C - C \\ &\quad | \quad | \quad \backslash \\ &\quad H \quad H \quad O - H \end{aligned}$$

This drawing earns the **1 mark** by showing all atoms and all bonds clearly.

steps.title: Analyze the IUPAC name
steps.content: To identify the functional group, we look at the name of the compound: **propanoic acid**. In chemistry, the suffix (the ending) of a molecule's name tells us which family of organic compounds it belongs to.

steps.title: Link suffix to group
steps.content:
In IUPAC nomenclature, the suffix **-oic acid** is used specifically for molecules that contain the carboxylic acid functional group. This group consists of a carbon atom double-bonded to an oxygen atom and single-bonded to a hydroxyl group, written as:

$$-\text{COOH}$$

steps.title: State the functional group
steps.content:
Based on the name **propanoic acid**, the functional group is the **carboxyl group**.

According to the markscheme, you can also name this as the **carboxylic acid group** or provide the structural formula **$-\text{COOH}$** to earn the mark.

steps.title: Identify the compound class

steps.title: Consult the Data Booklet
steps.content:
To find the specific name of the functional group, we should refer to **Table 19** in the IB Chemistry Data Booklet. This table lists various classes of organic compounds alongside their corresponding functional group names.

For more background on how these groups are categorized, you can review **Topic S3.2: Functional groups** in your syllabus.

steps.title: Locate the functional group
steps.content: In Table 19, find the row labeled "carboxylic acid" under the "Class" column. Looking across to the "Functional group" column, the name provided for this specific group is the **carboxyl** group.

steps.title: Final identification
steps.content:
The functional group present in propanoic acid is the **carboxyl group**. 

Note that the markscheme also accepts "carboxylic acid group" or the structural formula $-\text{COOH}$, but "carboxyl" is the standard name listed in your data booklet.

parts.content: Draw the fully structural formula of propan-1-ol.
parts.markscheme:
Structure: $\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2OH}$ or full displayed formula showing all bonds

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/39cbfe4b-2ef2-4212-878c-31a6fc98ac26)

<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Analyze the IUPAC name
steps.content:
To draw the formula for **propan-1-ol**, we first break down its IUPAC name into two parts:
- The prefix **"prop-"** indicates a chain of **three carbon atoms**.
- The suffix **"-1-ol"** indicates a **hydroxyl group ($-$OH)** attached to the **first carbon** atom in the chain.

This falls under **Topic S3.2: Functional groups**, where we learn to represent organic molecules.

steps.title: Draw the carbon backbone
steps.content:
Since the prefix is "prop-", we start by drawing a horizontal chain of three carbon atoms connected by single bonds:

$$\text{C} - \text{C} - \text{C}$$

steps.title: Position the hydroxyl group
steps.content:
The name specifies "-1-ol", meaning the alcohol functional group is on the first carbon. In a **full displayed formula**, we must show the bond between the oxygen and the hydrogen atom ($-\text{O}-\text{H}$).

$$\text{C} - \text{C} - \text{C} - \text{O} - \text{H}$$

steps.title: Add hydrogen atoms
steps.content:
Finally, we fill in the remaining bonds for each carbon atom. Carbon must always form **four bonds**. 
- The first carbon (with the $-\text{OH}$ group) needs 2 more hydrogens.
- The middle carbon needs 2 more hydrogens.
- The end carbon needs 3 more hydrogens.

We must draw out every single bond ($-\text{H}$) explicitly to satisfy the requirements for a full displayed formula.

steps.title: Final Displayed Formula
steps.content:
Combining everything, the full displayed formula showing every atom and every bond is:

$$\begin{aligned} & \quad \text{H} \quad \text{H} \quad \text{H} \\ & \quad | \quad \enspace | \quad \enspace | \\ \text{H} - & \text{C} - \text{C} - \text{C} - \text{O} - \text{H} \\ & \quad | \quad \enspace | \quad \enspace | \\ & \quad \text{H} \quad \text{H} \quad \text{H} \end{aligned}$$

This structure earns the **1 mark** allocated in the markscheme by clearly showing the three-carbon chain and the terminal hydroxyl group with all bonds visible.

highlights.text: full displayed formula showing all bonds

steps.title: Analyze the IUPAC name
steps.content:
To represent the molecule, we first break down the name **propan-1-ol** into its components:

*   **Prop-**: This prefix indicates a backbone of three carbon atoms.
*   **-an-**: This indicates that the carbon-carbon bonds are all single bonds (alkane-derived).
*   **-1-ol**: This suffix indicates an alcohol functional group ($-OH$) attached to the first carbon in the chain.

steps.title: Determine the carbon units
steps.content:
Using the **condensed structural formula** approach, we represent each carbon atom and the hydrogen atoms bonded to it as a single unit without drawing individual bond lines. For a three-carbon chain:

1.  The first carbon (at the start of the chain) is a methyl group: $CH_3$.
2.  The second carbon (in the middle) is a methylene group: $CH_2$.
3.  The third carbon is where the functional group is attached.

steps.title: Attach the functional group
steps.content:
Since the name specifies **-1-ol**, we attach the hydroxyl group ($-OH$) to the end of the chain. This changes the final $CH_3$ group of propane into a $CH_2OH$ group.

Following the sequence of the carbon atoms, we arrange these units together.

steps.title: Assemble the final formula
steps.content:
Combining the units in order, we get the condensed structural formula:

$$CH_3CH_2CH_2OH$$

While the question asks for a "fully structural formula," the IB markscheme accepts this condensed version as a correct representation for the mark.

steps.title: Identify the compound class

steps.title: Reference the Data Booklet
steps.content: To identify the correct name, we consult the **Functional groups** table in the IB Chemistry Data Booklet. This table is an essential tool for **Topic S3.2: Functional groups**, as it provides a standardized list of compound classes and their corresponding functional group names.

parts.content: Classify propan-1-ol as a primary, secondary, or tertiary alcohol, and justify your answer based on its structure.
parts.markscheme:
- Primary alcohol
<marks>1 mark</marks>
- Hydroxyl group is attached to a carbon that is bonded to only one other carbon atom
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Visualize the structure
steps.content:
To classify an alcohol using carbon connectivity, we first need to look at the structural formula of propan-1-ol. The "1" in the name tells us the hydroxyl ($-OH$) group is on the first carbon of a three-carbon chain.

$$CH_{3}-CH_{2}-CH_{2}-OH$$

steps.title: Identify the alpha carbon
steps.content:
In carbon connectivity analysis, we focus on the **alpha ($\alpha$) carbon**. This is the specific carbon atom that is directly bonded to the functional group—in this case, the hydroxyl ($-OH$) group.

In propan-1-ol, the $\alpha$-carbon is the one at the end of the chain:

$$CH_{3}-CH_{2}-\mathbf{C}H_{2}-OH$$

steps.title: Analyze carbon connectivity
steps.content:
Now, count how many **other carbon atoms** are directly attached to that $\alpha$-carbon. 

Looking at the structure:
- The $\alpha$-carbon is bonded to **one** other carbon atom (the middle carbon in the chain).
- The other bonds on the $\alpha$-carbon are connected to hydrogen atoms.

steps.title: Determine the classification
steps.content:
We use the following rules for classification based on the $\alpha$-carbon connectivity:
- **Primary ($1^{\circ}$):** Bonded to 1 other carbon.
- **Secondary ($2^{\circ}$):** Bonded to 2 other carbons.
- **Tertiary ($3^{\circ}$):** Bonded to 3 other carbons.

Since the $\alpha$-carbon in propan-1-ol is bonded to only one other carbon, it is a **primary alcohol**.

steps.title: Final answer and justification
steps.content:
Based on the structure and our connectivity analysis, we can state the final answer for the 2 marks:

1.  **Classification:** Primary alcohol.
2.  **Justification:** The hydroxyl group is attached to a carbon atom that is bonded to only one other carbon atom.

steps.title: Visualize the structure
steps.content:
To classify the alcohol, we first look at the structure of **propan-1-ol**. The "prop-" prefix indicates a three-carbon chain, and the "-1-ol" tells us the hydroxyl ($-OH$) group is attached to the first carbon.

$$\text{CH}_3-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{OH}$$

steps.title: Identify the alpha carbon
steps.content:
In organic chemistry, the **alpha carbon** ($\alpha$-carbon) is the carbon atom directly bonded to the functional group. For propan-1-ol, this is the carbon atom attached to the $-OH$ group.

This concept is a key part of **Topic S3.2: Functional groups** in your syllabus.

steps.title: Count the alpha hydrogens
steps.content:
Using the **Alpha-Hydrogen Count** approach, we look at how many hydrogen atoms are attached to that specific $\alpha$-carbon. 

In propan-1-ol ($CH_3-CH_2-\mathbf{C}H_2-OH$), the $\alpha$-carbon is bonded to:
- One $-OH$ group
- One alkyl group ($-CH_2CH_3$)
- **Two** hydrogen atoms

There are 2 alpha hydrogens.

steps.title: Classify and justify
steps.content:
According to the classification rules:
- **Primary ($1^\circ$):** 2 or 3 $\alpha$-hydrogens (carbon is bonded to only 1 other carbon).
- **Secondary ($2^\circ$):** 1 $\alpha$-hydrogen (carbon is bonded to 2 other carbons).
- **Tertiary ($3^\circ$):** 0 $\alpha$-hydrogens (carbon is bonded to 3 other carbons).

Since propan-1-ol has 2 $\alpha$-hydrogens, it is a **primary alcohol**. This means the carbon holding the hydroxyl group is bonded to only one other carbon atom.

highlights.text: Hydroxyl group is attached to a carbon that is bonded to only one other carbon atom

steps.title: Identify the molecule structure
steps.content:
To classify propan-1-ol, we first need to understand its structure. The name tells us everything we need to know: 
- **'prop-'** indicates a chain of 3 carbon atoms.
- **'-1-ol'** indicates that the hydroxyl functional group ($-OH$) is attached to the first carbon in that chain.

This classification falls under **Topic S3.2 Functional groups: Classification of organic compounds** in your IB syllabus.

steps.title: Draw the displayed formula
steps.content:
Let's visualize the molecule by drawing its full displayed formula. This helps us see exactly how many atoms are connected to each other:

$$\begin{array}{ccccccc} 
& H & H & H \\
& | & | & | \\
H - & C_3 & - C_2 & - C_1 & - O - H \\
& | & | & | \\
& H & H & H 
\end{array}$$

Notice that the $-OH$ group is situated on a **terminal carbon atom** (the one at the very end of the chain).

steps.title: Analyze the alpha carbon
steps.content:
To classify an alcohol, we look specifically at the carbon atom attached to the $-OH$ group (often called the alpha carbon). 

In our diagram, this is $C_1$. Let's count how many other **carbon atoms** are directly bonded to $C_1$:
- $C_1$ is bonded to two Hydrogen atoms ($H$).
- $C_1$ is bonded to only **one other carbon atom** ($C_2$).

steps.title: Apply classification rules
steps.content:
According to IB definitions:
- **Primary ($1^{\circ}$):** The $-OH$ carbon is bonded to 0 or 1 other carbon.
- **Secondary ($2^{\circ}$):** The $-OH$ carbon is bonded to 2 other carbons.
- **Tertiary ($3^{\circ}$):** The $-OH$ carbon is bonded to 3 other carbons.

Since our carbon is bonded to only one other carbon, propan-1-ol is a **primary alcohol**.

steps.title: Final justification
steps.content:
To earn full marks, you must state the classification and provide the structural reason.

**Classification:** Primary alcohol
**Justification:** The hydroxyl group is attached to a carbon atom that is bonded to only one other carbon atom.

highlights.text: Hydroxyl group is attached to a carbon that is bonded to only one other carbon atom

parts.content: Between $\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2CH_3}$ (butane) and $\mathrm{(CH_3)_3CH}$ (2-methylpropane), predict which has the higher boiling point and explain your reasoning.
parts.markscheme:
- $\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2CH_3}$ (butane) has the higher boiling point <marks>A1</marks>
- Less branched structure results in greater surface area and stronger London dispersion forces <marks>R1</marks>
2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the primary IMF
steps.content:
First, we look at the type of molecules we are dealing with. Both butane and 2-methylpropane are alkanes, which are non-polar hydrocarbons. Because they lack a permanent dipole, the only intermolecular forces (IMFs) present are **London dispersion forces**. 

This topic is covered in **Topic S2.2: The covalent model**, specifically under intermolecular forces and physical properties.

steps.title: Analyze molecular shapes
steps.content:
The strength of London dispersion forces depends on how easily the electron clouds can interact. We compare the structures:

*   **Butane** ($\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2CH_3}$) is a straight-chain (linear) molecule.
*   **2-methylpropane** ($\mathrm{(CH_3)_3CH}$) is a branched molecule.

Think of butane like a long piece of Velcro and 2-methylpropane like a crumpled ball of Velcro. The long piece has more surface area to stick to others!

steps.title: Link shape to forces
steps.content: Because butane is linear, it has a **greater surface area** and can pack more closely with neighboring molecules. This increased contact area leads to stronger London dispersion forces compared to the branched 2-methylpropane, where branching prevents close packing and reduces surface contact.

highlights.text: Less branched structure results in greater surface area and stronger London dispersion forces

steps.title: Predict the boiling point
steps.content:
Since boiling involves overcoming these intermolecular attractions, the molecule with the stronger forces will require more energy (a higher temperature) to turn into a gas. 

Therefore, **butane** has the higher boiling point.

highlights.text: CH3CH2CH2CH3 (butane) has the higher boiling point

parts.content: Describe how the shape of a molecule affects the London dispersion forces between molecules.
parts.markscheme:
- Linear or less branched molecules pack more closely together <marks>A1</marks>
- Greater contact between molecules increases dispersion forces <marks>A1</marks>
2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze Molecular Packing
steps.content: To understand how shape influences London dispersion forces (LDF), we first look at how molecules fit together. Linear or less branched molecules have an elongated shape, which allows them to align and **pack more closely together** compared to highly branched isomers, which tend to be more spherical.

highlights.text: Linear or less branched molecules pack more closely together

steps.title: Evaluate Surface Contact Area
steps.content: When molecules can pack closely, they have a **greater surface area of contact** with one another. Since London dispersion forces are temporary dipole-induced dipole interactions that occur at the surface, increasing the area where molecules touch directly increases the total strength of these forces.

highlights.text: Greater contact between molecules increases dispersion forces

steps.title: Contrast with Branched Isomers
steps.content:
In contrast, branched isomers (like neopentane) are more spherical. Their shape prevents them from coming into close contact with each other, resulting in a smaller surface area for LDFs to act upon. This explains why branched alkanes typically have lower boiling points than their linear counterparts.

This concept is a key part of **Topic S2.2: The covalent model**, specifically regarding how intermolecular forces influence physical properties.

parts.content: Ethanol and propane have similar molar masses, but very different boiling points. Explain why ethanol boils at a much higher temperature.
parts.markscheme:
- Ethanol has a hydroxyl group ($-\mathrm{OH}$) that forms hydrogen bonds <marks>A1</marks>
- Hydrogen bonding is stronger than the dispersion forces in propane <marks>R1</marks>
2 marks total

steps.title: Identify ethanol's intermolecular forces
steps.content:
First, we look at the structure of ethanol ($CH_3CH_2OH$). It contains a **hydroxyl group** ($-OH$), where a hydrogen atom is covalently bonded to a highly electronegative oxygen atom. This specific arrangement allows ethanol molecules to form **hydrogen bonds** with one another.

This is a key concept from **Topic S2.2: The covalent model**, specifically section **S2.2.8** on intermolecular forces.

highlights.text: hydroxyl group ($-\mathrm{OH}$) that forms hydrogen bonds

steps.title: Identify propane's intermolecular forces
steps.content: Next, we look at propane ($CH_3CH_2CH_3$). Propane is a non-polar hydrocarbon. Because it lacks polar bonds like the $-OH$ group, its molecules are only held together by relatively weak **London (dispersion) forces**, which arise from temporary dipoles.

steps.title: Compare force strengths
steps.content:
To explain the boiling point difference, we compare the strength of these forces. **Hydrogen bonding** is significantly stronger than the **London dispersion forces** found in propane.

Since boiling involves overcoming these attractions to move molecules from the liquid to the gas phase, the stronger forces in ethanol require much more thermal energy (a higher temperature) to break.

highlights.text: Hydrogen bonding is stronger than the dispersion forces in propane

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
Even though they have similar molar masses, the presence of strong hydrogen bonds in ethanol versus the weak dispersion forces in propane results in ethanol having a much higher boiling point.

**Marking Breakdown:**
- **1 Mark:** Identifying that ethanol has a hydroxyl group that forms hydrogen bonds.
- **1 Mark:** Stating that hydrogen bonding is stronger than the dispersion forces in propane.
