parts.content: State what is meant by a homologous series in organic chemistry.
parts.markscheme:
- A series of compounds with the same functional group and general formula, differing by a $-\text{CH}_2-$ unit <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify structural similarities
steps.content: To define a **homologous series** using structural characteristics, we first look at what the molecules have in common. All members of a specific series must share the same **functional group** and follow the same **general formula** (such as $$C_nH_{2n+2}$$ for alkanes). This common structure is why they react in similar ways.

steps.title: Identify the structural difference
steps.content: The second key characteristic is how the molecules change as the chain gets longer. Each successive member in the series differs from the previous one by a constant structural unit: a **$$-\text{CH}_2-$$ unit** (a methylene group).

steps.title: State the final definition
steps.content:
Combining these structural requirements gives us the complete definition required for the mark. 

A **homologous series** is a series of compounds with the **same functional group** and **general formula**, where each member differs from the next by a **$$-\text{CH}_2-$$ unit**.

highlights.text: A series of compounds with the same functional group and general formula, differing by a $-\text{CH}_2-$ unit

parts.content: Identify the homologous series to which ethanoic acid belongs.
parts.markscheme:
- Carboxylic acids <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Analyze the compound name
steps.content:
To identify the homologous series, we first look at the IUPAC name provided in the question: **ethanoic acid**. 

In organic chemistry, the name of a compound is built from different parts that tell us about its structure. The most important part for identifying the family (homologous series) it belongs to is the **suffix** (the ending).

steps.title: Identify the suffix
steps.content:
Using the **Nomenclature Suffix Analysis** approach, we isolate the ending of the name:

$$\text{ethan} + \mathbf{\text{-oic acid}}$$

The suffix **-oic acid** is the specific indicator used in IUPAC nomenclature to represent a particular functional group and its corresponding homologous series.

steps.title: Match suffix to series
steps.content:
In the IB Chemistry syllabus (specifically **Topic S3.2: Functional groups**), we learn that the suffix **-oic acid** is unique to the homologous series where the molecules contain the carboxyl functional group ($-COOH$).

This series is known as the **carboxylic acids**.

steps.title: Final Identification
steps.content:
Since the suffix of ethanoic acid is **-oic acid**, we can conclude that it belongs to the carboxylic acid homologous series.

**Final Answer:**
Carboxylic acids

steps.title: Analyze the chemical name
steps.content:
To identify the homologous series, we first look at the IUPAC name: **ethanoic acid**. 

*   The prefix **"eth-"** tells us there are 2 carbon atoms in the main chain.
*   The suffix **"-oic acid"** is the key indicator for the functional group present in the molecule.

steps.title: Identify the functional group
steps.content:
The suffix "-oic acid" indicates the presence of the **carboxyl group**, which has the formula **$-COOH$**. 

The full structural formula for ethanoic acid is:

$$CH_3COOH$$

In this structure, the carbon atom is double-bonded to an oxygen atom and single-bonded to a hydroxyl ($-OH$) group.

steps.title: Determine the homologous series
steps.content:
Compounds that contain the $-COOH$ (carboxyl) functional group belong to the same family of organic compounds. 

According to the rules of classification covered in **Topic S3.2: Functional groups**, any molecule with this group belongs to the **carboxylic acids** homologous series.

parts.content: Describe two properties that are common to members of the same homologous series.
parts.markscheme:
- Show similar chemical properties (e.g., react in similar ways) <marks>A1</marks>
- Show a gradual trend in physical properties (e.g., boiling point or solubility) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define a homologous series
steps.content: In IB Chemistry, specifically in **Topic S3.2: Functional groups**, a homologous series is defined as a family of organic compounds that share the same general formula and functional group. Because they share these structural features, they exhibit predictable behaviors.

steps.title: Recall chemical properties
steps.content:
The first major characteristic is that members of the same series **show similar chemical properties**. 

This occurs because they all possess the same **functional group**, which is the part of the molecule responsible for its characteristic chemical reactions. For example, all alcohols ($R	ext{—OH}$) will undergo similar oxidation reactions.

highlights.text: Show similar chemical properties (e.g., react in similar ways)

steps.title: Recall physical trends
steps.content:
The second characteristic is that members **show a gradual trend in physical properties**. 

As you move down a series (e.g., from methane to ethane to propane), each successive member differs by a constant $-	ext{CH}_2-$ unit. This increase in molar mass and chain length leads to stronger London dispersion forces, resulting in a predictable increase in boiling points. This is a core concept found in **S3.2.4 Physical trends in homologous series**.

highlights.text: Show a gradual trend in physical properties (e.g., boiling point or solubility)

steps.title: Final answer summary
steps.content:
To earn the full 2 marks, you simply need to state these two distinct features:

1.  **Similar chemical properties** (because they have the same functional group).
2.  **A gradual trend in physical properties** (such as boiling point or solubility).

steps.title: Identify structural similarities
steps.content:
To understand the properties of a homologous series, we first look at their structure. All members of a homologous series share the same **general formula** and possess the same **functional group**. 

For example, in the alcohol series ($C_n H_{2n+1}OH$), every member contains the $-\text{OH}$ hydroxyl group. This structural consistency leads to predictable behavior across the entire family. This concept is covered in **Topic S3.2.3: Homologous series**.

highlights.text: Describe two properties that are common to members of the same homologous series.

steps.title: Analyze chemical reactivity
steps.content:
The first property common to a homologous series is that they show **similar chemical properties**. 

In organic chemistry, the **functional group** is the "reactive part" of the molecule. Because every member of the series has the same functional group, they will undergo the same types of chemical reactions (e.g., all alkanes undergo combustion, and all alkenes undergo addition reactions).

steps.title: Analyze physical trends
steps.content:
The second property is a **gradual trend in physical properties**, such as boiling point or solubility. 

As we move down a series, each successive member differs by a $-\text{CH}_2-$ group. This increase in molecular size and molar mass leads to stronger intermolecular forces (specifically London dispersion forces). Consequently, properties like the boiling point increase predictably as the chain length grows. You can review these patterns in **Topic S3.2.4: Physical trends in homologous series**.

steps.title: Finalize the answer
steps.content:
To secure the 2 marks available, you must identify both the chemical and physical characteristics:

1.  **Similar chemical properties** (or reacting in similar ways).
2.  **A gradual trend in physical properties** (such as boiling point).

highlights.text: Show similar chemical properties (e.g., react in similar ways)
Show a gradual trend in physical properties (e.g., boiling point or solubility)

parts.content: Draw and name two functional group isomers of this compound.
parts.markscheme:
- **Name** and **draw** butanal: $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CHO}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- **Name** and **draw** butan-2-one: $\text{CH}_3\text{COCH}_2\text{CH}_3$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Accept any valid functional group isomers, such as cyclobutanol or but-3-en-1-ol, if drawn and named correctly.</note>

parts.content: Draw the chain isomer of butanal and name it.
parts.markscheme:
- **Name** and **draw** 2-methylpropanal: $(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{CHCHO}$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the starting structure
steps.content:
To find the chain isomer, we first look at the structure of **butanal**. It is a straight-chain aldehyde with four carbons. The carbon atoms are arranged in a single continuous line:

$$\text{CH}_3 - \text{CH}_2 - \text{CH}_2 - \text{CHO}$$

This uses concepts from **Topic S3.2 Functional groups**, where you learn that the "-al" suffix indicates an aldehyde group ($-\text{CHO}$) at the end of the chain.

steps.title: Shorten the parent chain
steps.content:
A **chain isomer** is created by changing the arrangement of the carbon skeleton while keeping the molecular formula ($\text{C}_4\text{H}_8\text{O}$) the same. 

We apply the **Structural Chain Branching** approach by shortening the four-carbon parent chain to a three-carbon chain. This gives us a base structure of **propanal**.

steps.title: Add the branch
steps.content:
Now, we take the carbon atom we removed from the main chain and attach it as a **methyl group** ($-\text{CH}_3$) to the middle carbon of the propanal chain. 

In IUPAC nomenclature for aldehydes, the carbonyl carbon (the one in the $-\text{CHO}$ group) is always **carbon 1**. Therefore, the middle carbon is **carbon 2**.

steps.title: Draw and name the isomer
steps.content:
By adding a methyl group to position 2 of propanal, we get the final isomer. 

**Name:** 2-methylpropanal  
**Condensed structural formula:** $(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{CHCHO}$

To earn the mark, you must provide both the correct name and a clear drawing of the structure.

parts.content: For each compound in Parts 1 and 2, identify the homologous series it belongs to and give its general formula.
parts.markscheme:
- **Identify** butanal as belonging to the aldehyde homologous series with general formula $\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n}\text{O}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- **Identify** butan-2-one as belonging to the ketone homologous series with general formula $\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n}\text{O}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- **Identify** 2-methylpropanal as belonging to the aldehyde homologous series with general formula $\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n}\text{O}$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify isomers from nomenclature
steps.content: To solve this, we first look at the IUPAC names of the isomers derived in the previous parts: **butanal**, **butan-2-one**, and **2-methylpropanal**. These names contain suffixes that tell us exactly which homologous series they belong to, as covered in **Topic S3.2: Functional groups**.

steps.title: Analyze the aldehyde suffix
steps.content:
The suffix **-al** indicates the presence of a carbonyl group at the end of a carbon chain, identifying the compound as an **aldehyde**. 

Looking at our list, both **butanal** and **2-methylpropanal** end in **-al**, so they belong to the aldehyde homologous series.

highlights.text: Identify butanal as belonging to the aldehyde homologous series
Identify 2-methylpropanal as belonging to the aldehyde homologous series

steps.title: Analyze the ketone suffix
steps.content:
The suffix **-one** indicates a carbonyl group located within the carbon chain, identifying the compound as a **ketone**. 

**Butan-2-one** ends in **-one**, meaning it belongs to the ketone homologous series.

highlights.text: Identify butan-2-one as belonging to the ketone homologous series

steps.title: Deduce the general formula
steps.content:
We can deduce the general formula by comparing the number of carbon atoms ($n$) to the number of hydrogen and oxygen atoms in the molecular formula $C_4H_8O$. 

If $n = 4$, then $2n = 8$. This matches the ratio in our formula perfectly. Therefore, for both aldehydes and ketones containing one carbonyl group, the general formula is:

$$C_nH_{2n}O$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 2 * 4 to verify the hydrogen ratio.

ti84.instructions: Calculate 2 times 4 to check the hydrogen count.

calculator.description: Verify the C:H ratio for n=4

steps.title: Final identification
steps.content:
Based on our analysis:

1. **Butanal**: Aldehyde series; General formula $C_nH_{2n}O$.
2. **Butan-2-one**: Ketone series; General formula $C_nH_{2n}O$.
3. **2-methylpropanal**: Aldehyde series; General formula $C_nH_{2n}O$.

steps.title: Identify the Carbonyl Position
steps.content:
To classify these isomers, we focus on the position of the **carbonyl group** ($C=O$). This distinction is a key part of **Topic S3.2: Functional groups**. 

*   If the carbonyl group is at the **end** of a carbon chain, the compound is an **aldehyde**.
*   If the carbonyl group is in the **middle** of a carbon chain (bonded to two other carbons), the compound is a **ketone**.

steps.title: Analyze Butanal
steps.content:
By sketching the structural formula for **butanal**, we see a straight four-carbon chain with the carbonyl group on the first carbon:

$$\text{CH}_3-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{CHO}$$

Because the group is at the end, it belongs to the **aldehyde** homologous series. For a compound with 4 carbons, the formula is $\text{C}_4\text{H}_8\text{O}$, which fits the general formula:

$$\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n}\text{O}$$

highlights.text: Identify butanal as belonging to the aldehyde homologous series with general formula C_nH_{2n}O

steps.title: Analyze Butan-2-one
steps.content:
Next, we sketch **butan-2-one**. Here, the carbonyl group is located on the second carbon atom of the chain:

$$\text{CH}_3-\text{C}(=\text{O})-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_3$$

Since the carbonyl carbon is bonded to two other carbons, it belongs to the **ketone** homologous series. It also shares the general formula:

$$\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n}\text{O}$$

highlights.text: Identify butan-2-one as belonging to the ketone homologous series with general formula C_nH_{2n}O

steps.title: Analyze 2-methylpropanal
steps.content:
Finally, we sketch **2-methylpropanal**. This is a branched isomer where the main chain has three carbons and a methyl branch. The carbonyl group is at the end of the main chain:

$$\text{CH}_3-\text{CH}(\text{CH}_3)-\text{CHO}$$

Despite the branching, the terminal position of the carbonyl group confirms it is an **aldehyde**. Its general formula remains:

$$\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n}\text{O}$$

highlights.text: Identify 2-methylpropanal as belonging to the aldehyde homologous series with general formula C_nH_{2n}O

steps.title: Verify the General Formula
steps.content:
We can verify the general formula using the original molecular formula. All three isomers have the same molecular formula.

For $n=4$ carbons:
$$2n = 2 \times 4 = 8$$

This matches the number of hydrogens in $\text{C}_4\text{H}_8\text{O}$. Thus, for both aldehydes and ketones, the general formula is:

$$\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n}\text{O}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 2 times 4 to find the number of hydrogens.

parts.markscheme:

- Correct displayed (or clearly unambiguous) structure for trans-but-2-ene <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the structural feature that allows cis-trans isomerism.
parts.markscheme:
- Rotation about the $\text{C}=\text{C}$ bond is restricted due to the $\pi$ bond <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Cis/trans isomerism requires a $\text{C}=\text{C}$ double bond (or other restricted-rotation unit) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Each carbon in the double bond is bonded to two different groups (so each end has two different substituents) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- If either carbon has two identical substituents, cis/trans isomerism is not possible <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: State which compound has the higher boiling point: ethanol or ethanoic acid.
parts.markscheme:
- State that ethanoic acid has the higher boiling point <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify functional groups
steps.content:
To compare the boiling points, we first look at the functional groups present in each molecule:

*   **Ethanol** ($CH_3CH_2OH$): Contains a **hydroxyl group** ($-OH$).
*   **Ethanoic acid** ($CH_3COOH$): Contains a **carboxyl group** ($-COOH$), which consists of both a carbonyl ($C=O$) and a hydroxyl ($-OH$) group.

This analysis falls under **Topic S3.2: Functional groups**.

steps.title: Analyze hydrogen bonding potential
steps.content:
Both molecules exhibit **hydrogen bonding**, the strongest type of intermolecular force (IMF) for these small molecules. 

However, the carboxyl group in ethanoic acid is more effective at forming these bonds. Because it contains both a $C=O$ and an $-OH$ group, two ethanoic acid molecules can align to form two hydrogen bonds between them, creating a stable structure called a **dimer**.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Ethanol:} & \quad \text{1 H-bond interaction site} \\ \text{Ethanoic acid:} & \quad \text{2 H-bond interaction sites (Dimerization)} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Relate IMFs to Boiling Point
steps.content:
Boiling point is a measure of the energy required to overcome intermolecular forces. 

Since ethanoic acid can form dimers and has a more polar functional group, its total intermolecular attractions are stronger than those in ethanol. Stronger IMFs require more thermal energy to break, leading to a higher boiling point. This relationship is covered in **Topic S2.2: Intermolecular forces and physical properties**.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content: Based on the comparison of intermolecular forces, **ethanoic acid** has the higher boiling point.

highlights.text: State which compound has the higher boiling point: ethanol or ethanoic acid.

steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content: The question asks us to identify which compound has a higher boiling point: ethanol or ethanoic acid. Since we have access to the IB Chemistry Data Booklet, we can find the exact experimental values to make a direct comparison.

highlights.text: State which compound has the higher boiling point: ethanol or ethanoic acid.

steps.title: Consult the Data Booklet
steps.content:
To find these values, we look at the section for **Physical constants of organic compounds** (often Table 15). 

Looking at the boiling point ($BP$) values:
- **Ethanol** ($C_2H_5OH$): Approximately $78.3^\circ\text{C}$ (or $351\text{ K}$)
- **Ethanoic acid** ($CH_3COOH$): Approximately $117.9^\circ\text{C}$ (or $391\text{ K}$)

steps.title: Compare the values
steps.content:
By comparing the two temperatures:

$$117.9^\circ\text{C} > 78.3^\circ\text{C}$$

Or in Kelvin:

$$391\text{ K} > 351\text{ K}$$

Because ethanoic acid has a significantly higher numerical boiling point value, it is the correct choice.

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify hydrogen bonding
steps.content:
First, we look at the functional groups. Both ethanol and ethanoic acid contain the hydroxyl group ($-OH$). In chemistry, when hydrogen is directly bonded to a highly electronegative atom like oxygen, it can form **hydrogen bonds**, which are the strongest type of intermolecular force (IMF).

This concept is a key part of **Topic S2.2: The covalent model**.

highlights.text: Identify that both ethanol and ethanoic acid form hydrogen bonds

steps.title: Analyze ethanoic acid dimerization
steps.content:
The reason ethanoic acid has a much higher boiling point than ethanol is due to its ability to form **dimers**. Because ethanoic acid has both a carbonyl group ($C=O$) and a hydroxyl group ($-OH$), two molecules can pair up to form two hydrogen bonds between them.

This can be represented as:

$$\begin{matrix} \text{CH}_3-\text{C} & = & \text{O} & \cdots & \text{H}-\text{O} \\ & & | & & | \\ \text{O}-\text{H} & \cdots & \text{O} & = & \text{C}-\text{CH}_3 \end{matrix}$$

While ethanol typically forms one hydrogen bond per molecule, ethanoic acid forms a stable dimer with **two hydrogen bonds** per pair.

highlights.text: Describe how ethanoic acid can form dimers via two hydrogen bonds per molecule

steps.title: Link to boiling point
steps.content:
Finally, we connect the strength of these forces to the physical property. The formation of dimers creates much stronger overall intermolecular attractions compared to the single hydrogen bonds in ethanol.

In the IB curriculum (Topic S2.2.9), we learn that **stronger intermolecular forces require more energy to overcome**. Therefore, more heat energy is needed to separate the ethanoic acid molecules into the gas phase, resulting in a higher boiling point.

highlights.text: Explain that stronger intermolecular forces require more energy to overcome $\rightarrow$ higher boiling point

steps.title: Identify the primary force
steps.content:
First, we need to recognize the types of intermolecular forces at play. Both ethanol ($CH_3CH_2OH$) and ethanoic acid ($CH_3COOH$) contain a hydrogen atom bonded directly to a highly electronegative oxygen atom. This tells us that both molecules can form **hydrogen bonds**, which are the strongest type of intermolecular force. 

This concept is covered in **Topic S2.2.8: Intermolecular forces**.

highlights.text: Identify that both ethanol and ethanoic acid form hydrogen bonds

steps.title: Compare hydrogen bonding sites
steps.content:
To understand the difference in boiling points, we look at the functional groups:

*   **Ethanol** has one hydroxyl ($-OH$) group, providing one primary site for hydrogen bonding per molecule.
*   **Ethanoic acid** has a carboxyl ($-COOH$) group. This contains both a hydroxyl ($-OH$) group and a carbonyl ($C=O$) group.

The presence of the electron-rich carbonyl oxygen provides **additional sites** for hydrogen bonding that ethanol lacks.

steps.title: Explain dimer formation
steps.content:
Because ethanoic acid has these extra sites, two molecules can align perfectly to form two hydrogen bonds between them. This structure is known as a **dimer**. 

$$\text{Ethanoic Acid Dimer: } (CH_3COOH)_2$$

In contrast, ethanol molecules typically associate through only one hydrogen bond at a time. The ability to form two bonds per pair of molecules significantly increases the total attractive force between ethanoic acid molecules.

highlights.text: Describe how ethanoic acid can form dimers via two hydrogen bonds per molecule

steps.title: Relate to boiling point
steps.content:
In chemistry, the boiling point is a measure of how much thermal energy is required to overcome intermolecular attractions. 

Since the dimer structure in ethanoic acid creates a much stronger network of attraction than the single bonds in ethanol, **more energy** is required to separate the molecules and transition them into the gas phase. This results in the higher boiling point observed for ethanoic acid. 

This relationship is explained in **Topic S2.2.9: Intermolecular forces and physical properties**.

highlights.text: Explain that stronger intermolecular forces require more energy to overcome

parts.content: Describe how increasing the carbon chain length of a carboxylic acid affects its boiling point, and explain why.
parts.markscheme:
- Describe how longer carbon chains have a greater surface area <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain that stronger London dispersion forces result in an increased boiling point <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the trend
steps.content: In a homologous series like carboxylic acids, as the number of carbon atoms in the chain increases, the **boiling point also increases**. This is a consistent trend seen as molecules become larger.

steps.title: Analyze surface area
steps.content: As the carbon chain grows longer, the overall size of the molecule increases. This results in a **greater surface area** over which the molecules can interact with one another. Additionally, more atoms mean a higher total number of electrons in the molecule.

highlights.text: longer carbon chains have a greater surface area

steps.title: Evaluate London dispersion forces
steps.content: The increased surface area and higher number of electrons lead to more frequent and stronger temporary dipoles. This results in **stronger London dispersion forces** (a type of van der Waals force) between the carboxylic acid molecules.

highlights.text: stronger London dispersion forces result in an increased boiling point

steps.title: Relate to boiling point
steps.content: Because the intermolecular forces (London dispersion forces) are stronger in longer chains, more thermal energy is required to overcome these attractions and separate the molecules. Consequently, the temperature at which the liquid boils (the boiling point) increases.

questions.specification:
The table below shows the condensed structural formulas of four different organic compounds:

| Compound | Formula |
| :--- | :--- |
| A | $\mathrm{CH_3CH_2OH}$ |
| B | $\mathrm{CH_3COOH}$ |
| C | $\mathrm{CH_3CH_2Cl}$ |
| D | $\mathrm{CH_3OCH_3}$ |

parts.content: Identify the functional group present in each compound.
parts.markscheme:
A: alcohol

B: carboxylic acid

C: halogenoalkane

D: ether
<marks>2</marks>

steps.title: Identify the task
steps.content: To solve this problem, we will use the **Direct Recognition** method. We look at the condensed structural formulas and pick out specific atoms or groups of atoms that serve as "fingerprints" for their organic class. This topic is covered in **Topic 3.2: Functional groups**.

steps.title: Analyze Compound A
steps.content:
For Compound A, the formula is $\mathrm{CH_3CH_2OH}$. 

We can immediately spot the **$-\mathrm{OH}$** group at the end of the chain. This represents the hydroxyl group, which tells us the compound belongs to the **alcohol** class.

steps.title: Analyze Compound B
steps.content:
For Compound B, the formula is $\mathrm{CH_3COOH}$. 

The grouping **$-\mathrm{COOH}$** is the distinct signature for a carboxyl group. When you see this specific arrangement of atoms in a condensed formula, it always identifies the compound as a **carboxylic acid**.

steps.title: Analyze Compound C
steps.content:
For Compound C, the formula is $\mathrm{CH_3CH_2Cl}$. 

Here, we see a chlorine atom (**$-\mathrm{Cl}$**) attached to the ethyl group. Any alkane chain with a halogen atom (like Cl, Br, or I) substituted for a hydrogen atom is classified as a **halogenoalkane**.

steps.title: Analyze Compound D
steps.content:
For Compound D, the formula is $\mathrm{CH_3OCH_3}$. 

Notice the oxygen atom (**$-\mathrm{O}-$**) sandwiched directly between two methyl groups. This structure ($R-\mathrm{O}-R'$) is the defining characteristic of an **ether**.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Based on our recognition of the functional groups, the classification for each compound is:

*   **A:** alcohol
*   **B:** carboxylic acid
*   **C:** halogenoalkane
*   **D:** ether

In an IB exam, identifying all four correctly would earn you the full 2 marks.

steps.title: Analyze the task
steps.content: To identify the functional groups correctly, we will use the **Structural Formula Expansion** approach. This involves drawing the full structure of each condensed formula to see exactly how the atoms are bonded. This helps us distinguish between similar-looking groups, like alcohols and carboxylic acids.

steps.title: Expand Compound A
steps.content:
For compound A, $\mathrm{CH_3CH_2OH}$, let's expand the bonding:

$$\begin{aligned} &\quad H \quad H \\ &\quad | \quad | \\ H - &C - C - O - H \\ &\quad | \quad | \\ &\quad H \quad H \end{aligned}$$

We see a hydroxyl group ($-\mathrm{OH}$) attached to a saturated carbon chain. This identifies the compound as an **alcohol**.

steps.title: Expand Compound B
steps.content:
For compound B, $\mathrm{CH_3COOH}$, the expansion reveals a specific arrangement at the end of the chain:

$$\begin{aligned} &\quad H \quad O \\ &\quad | \quad // \\ H - &C - C \\ &\quad | \quad \backslash \\ &\quad H \quad O - H \end{aligned}$$

This contains the carboxyl group ($-\mathrm{COOH}$), where one carbon is double-bonded to an oxygen and single-bonded to a hydroxyl group. This identifies the compound as a **carboxylic acid**.

steps.title: Expand Compound C
steps.content:
For compound C, $\mathrm{CH_3CH_2Cl}$, we expand it to see the halogen atom:

$$\begin{aligned} &\quad H \quad H \\ &\quad | \quad | \\ H - &C - C - Cl \\ &\quad | \quad | \\ &\quad H \quad H \end{aligned}$$

Since a chlorine atom (a halogen) is bonded to an alkyl group, the functional group classification is a **halogenoalkane**.

steps.title: Expand Compound D
steps.content:
For compound D, $\mathrm{CH_3OCH_3}$, we see an oxygen atom bridging two carbon groups:

$$\begin{aligned} &\quad H \qquad H \\ &\quad | \qquad | \\ H - &C - O - C - H \\ &\quad | \qquad | \\ &\quad H \qquad H \end{aligned}$$

When an oxygen atom is bonded between two alkyl groups ($R-O-R'$), the compound is an **ether**.

steps.title: Final Comparison
steps.content:
By expanding the formulas, we have clearly identified the characteristic bonding for each compound:

- **A:** Alcohol (Hydroxyl group)
- **B:** Carboxylic acid (Carboxyl group)
- **C:** Halogenoalkane (Halogen atom)
- **D:** Ether (Ether link)

This matches the requirements for the 2 marks available in this question.

parts.content: State whether each compound is saturated or unsaturated, and justify your answer.
parts.markscheme:
- All four compounds are saturated
<marks>1</marks>
- Each contains only single bonds between carbon atoms (no $\mathrm{C=C}$ or $\mathrm{C\equiv C}$)
<marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Define Saturation
steps.content:
In organic chemistry, we classify a molecule based on the types of bonds between its carbon atoms. 

*   **Saturated** compounds contain only **single bonds** between carbon atoms ($C-C$).
*   **Unsaturated** compounds contain at least one **double bond** ($C=C$) or **triple bond** ($C\equiv C$) between carbon atoms.

To determine the status of our four compounds, we will draw their full structural formulas to see the carbon-carbon bonding clearly.

steps.title: Draw Compounds A and B
steps.content:
Let's look at the first two compounds:

**Compound A: Ethanol ($\mathrm{CH_3CH_2OH}$)**
$$\begin{array}{cc} \text{H} & \text{H} \\ | & | \\ \text{H}-\text{C}-\text{C}-\text{O}-\text{H} \\ | & | \\ \text{H} & \text{H} \end{array}$$
Both carbons are connected by a single bond.

**Compound B: Ethanoic acid ($\mathrm{CH_3COOH}$)**
$$\begin{array}{cc} \text{H} & \text{O} \\ | & // \\ \text{H}-\text{C}-\text{C} \\ | & \backslash \\ \text{H} & \text{O}-\text{H} \end{array}$$
Even though there is a double bond between carbon and oxygen ($C=O$), the bond between the two **carbon atoms** is a single bond.

steps.title: Draw Compounds C and D
steps.content:
Now we draw the remaining two compounds:

**Compound C: Chloroethane ($\mathrm{CH_3CH_2Cl}$)**
$$\begin{array}{cc} \text{H} & \text{H} \\ | & | \\ \text{H}-\text{C}-\text{C}-\text{Cl} \\ | & | \\ \text{H} & \text{H} \end{array}$$
The carbon atoms are linked by a single bond.

**Compound D: Methoxymethane ($\mathrm{CH_3OCH_3}$)**
$$\begin{array}{ccc} \text{H} & & \text{H} \\ | & & | \\ \text{H}-\text{C} & -\text{O}- & \text{C}-\text{H} \\ | & & | \\ \text{H} & & \text{H} \end{array}$$
In this ether, the carbon atoms are not even directly bonded to each other; they are both bonded to an oxygen atom via single bonds.

steps.title: State Final Answer
steps.content:
Based on our structural drawings, we can conclude:

*   **State:** All four compounds (A, B, C, and D) are **saturated**.
*   **Justification:** Each molecule contains only **single bonds** between the carbon atoms. There are no carbon-carbon double ($C=C$) or triple ($C\equiv C$) bonds present.

Note: In IB Chemistry, the term "unsaturated" specifically refers to the presence of multiple bonds between carbon atoms, not bonds between carbon and other elements like oxygen.

highlights.text: Each contains only single bonds between carbon atoms (no $\mathrm{C=C}$ or $\mathrm{C\equiv C}$)

steps.title: Define saturation in IB Chemistry
steps.content:
In organic chemistry, the terms **saturated** and **unsaturated** refer specifically to the types of bonds found between carbon atoms. 

*   **Saturated:** A molecule containing only single bonds between carbon atoms.
*   **Unsaturated:** A molecule containing at least one double ($C=C$) or triple ($C\equiv C$) bond between carbon atoms.

Note: Double bonds between a carbon and another element (like the $C=O$ in a carboxylic acid) do **not** make a compound unsaturated by this definition.

steps.title: Identify homologous series
steps.content:
To determine the bonding, we first identify the functional groups and homologous series for each compound based on their condensed formulas:

*   **Compound A ($"CH_3CH_2OH"$):** Contains the $-OH$ group; it is an **alkanol** (ethanol).
*   **Compound B ($"CH_3COOH"$):** Contains the $-COOH$ group; it is an **alkanoic acid** (ethanoic acid).
*   **Compound C ($"CH_3CH_2Cl"$):** Contains a halogen atom; it is a **halogenoalkane** (chloroethane).
*   **Compound D ($"CH_3OCH_3"$):** Contains an ether linkage ($-O-$); it is an **ether** (methoxymethane).

steps.title: Analyze carbon-carbon bonds
steps.content:
Now we evaluate the carbon-to-carbon bonding for each:

1.  **A:** $CH_3-CH_2-OH$. Only a single $C-C$ bond exists.
2.  **B:** $CH_3-COOH$. Although there is a $C=O$ double bond in the acid group, the bond between the two carbon atoms is a single $C-C$ bond.
3.  **C:** $CH_3-CH_2-Cl$. Only a single $C-C$ bond exists.
4.  **D:** $CH_3-O-CH_3$. The carbons are not even bonded to each other; they are connected via an oxygen. There are no $C-C$ multiple bonds.

Since none of these compounds contain $C=C$ or $C\equiv C$ bonds, they are all classified as saturated.

steps.title: Final justification
steps.content:
To earn full marks, you must state the classification for all compounds and provide the correct reasoning.

*   **State:** All four compounds are saturated.
*   **Justify:** They contain only single bonds between carbon atoms (no $C=C$ or $C\equiv C$ bonds are present).

This aligns with the requirements found in **Topic S3.2 Functional groups** of your syllabus.

highlights.text: Each contains only single bonds between carbon atoms (no C=C or C≡C)

parts.content: Identify the homologous series to which ethyl butanoate belongs.
parts.markscheme: Esters <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Analyze the molecular structure
steps.content:
To identify the homologous series, we first need to look at the functional group present in the provided structure of ethyl butanoate. This is a key skill covered in **S3.2.2: Functional groups**.

Looking at the center of the molecule, we see a carbon atom double-bonded to one oxygen atom ($C=O$) and single-bonded to another oxygen atom ($-O-$), which is then connected to a second carbon chain ($R'$).

The general structure looks like this:
$$R-C(=O)-O-R'$$

steps.title: Identify the functional group
steps.content:
The specific arrangement where a carbonyl group ($C=O$) is directly adjacent to an ether linkage ($-O-$) is known as the **ester** functional group, often written as **-COO-**.

In this specific molecule:
- The group on the left ($R$) is a propyl group ($-C_3H_7$).
- The group on the right ($R'$) is an ethyl group ($-C_2H_5$).
- The central link is the ester group.

highlights.text: Identify the homologous series to which ethyl butanoate belongs.

steps.title: Determine the homologous series
steps.content:
Any organic compound containing the $-COO-$ functional group belongs to the homologous series called **esters**. 

You can also recognize this from the name "ethyl butano**ate**". The suffix **-oate** is a standard IUPAC convention for naming members of the ester series.

steps.title: Analyze the IUPAC name
steps.content:
To identify the homologous series, we examine the IUPAC name provided: **ethyl butanoate**. 

In organic chemistry, names are constructed using specific prefixes and suffixes that correspond to different functional groups. This is a core concept covered in **Topic S3.2: Functional groups**.

steps.title: Identify the characteristic suffix
steps.content:
Looking at the ending of the name "butanoate", we identify the suffix **-oate**.

According to IUPAC nomenclature rules, the **-oate** suffix is the standard designation for the **ester** homologous series. In these compounds, the name consists of two parts:
1. The alkyl group attached to the oxygen (ethyl).
2. The carboxylate group derived from the parent acid (butanoate).

steps.title: State the homologous series
steps.content:
Since the suffix **-oate** is unique to this functional group, we can conclude that the compound belongs to the **esters**.

Award 1 mark for identifying the correct series as per the markscheme.

parts.content: State the functional group present in ethyl butanoate.

steps.title: Identify the task
steps.content: The question asks us to identify the specific functional group found in the molecule **ethyl butanoate**, based on the provided skeletal structure. In organic chemistry, functional groups are specific atoms or groups of atoms that determine the chemical properties of a molecule.

highlights.text: State the functional group present in ethyl butanoate.

steps.title: Perform structural analysis
steps.content:
Looking at the skeletal structure, we focus on the central part of the molecule where the oxygen atoms are located. We can see a **carbonyl group** ($C=O$) directly bonded to an **oxygen atom** which is then bonded to another carbon chain ($R-COO-R'$).

This specific arrangement, where a carbonyl is adjacent to an ether-like oxygen link, is the defining feature of a specific functional group.

steps.title: Identify the functional group
steps.content:
Based on **Topic S3.2.2: Functional groups**, the structure containing the $-\text{COO}-$ link between two carbon chains is known as an **ester**. 

In this molecule, the 'butanoate' part comes from the four-carbon chain attached to the carbonyl, and the 'ethyl' part comes from the two-carbon chain attached to the single-bonded oxygen.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The functional group present is the **ester** group.

To earn the mark, you can simply state "ester" or show the group formula as $-\text{COO}-$.

steps.title: Analyze the compound name
steps.content: To determine the functional group using nomenclature analysis, we first look closely at the IUPAC name provided in the question: **ethyl butanoate**.

steps.title: Identify the suffix
steps.content:
In IUPAC organic chemistry nomenclature, the suffix (the ending of the name) tells us which functional group is the most important in the molecule. 

Looking at "butanoate", we can see the suffix is **-oate**.

steps.title: Map suffix to group
steps.content:
According to the rules covered in **Topic S3.2: Functional groups**, the suffix **-oate** is specifically used for **esters**. 

An ester is formed when a carboxylic acid reacts with an alcohol, and its characteristic functional group is the carboxylate group: 

$$-\text{COO}-$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Based on our nomenclature analysis of the **-oate** suffix, the functional group present is the **ester** group.

To get full marks, you can write the name "ester" or provide the structural formula.

**Final Answer:** Ester group ($-\text{COO}-$)

parts.content: Name the two organic compounds used to prepare ethyl butanoate.
parts.markscheme:
Butanoic acid <marks>1 mark</marks>

Ethanol <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the ester
steps.content:
The compound given is **ethyl butanoate**, which belongs to the **ester** functional group. Esters are formed through a condensation reaction (specifically esterification) between an alcohol and a carboxylic acid.

To identify the reactants, we can deconstruct the IUPAC name using nomenclature analysis. This concept is covered in **Topic S3.2: Functional groups**.

steps.title: Analyze the alcohol component
steps.content:
In the IUPAC name of an ester, the first word describes the alkyl group derived from the **alcohol**. 

The prefix **ethyl** indicates a two-carbon chain ($C_2$). Therefore, the alcohol used in this reaction is **ethanol**.

steps.title: Analyze the acid component
steps.content:
The second part of the name, ending in **-oate**, describes the part of the molecule derived from the **carboxylic acid**. 

The term **butanoate** stems from "butane," indicating a four-carbon chain ($C_4$). Therefore, the carboxylic acid used is **butanoic acid**.

steps.title: State final answers
steps.content:
Based on our nomenclature analysis, the two organic compounds used to prepare ethyl butanoate are:

1.  **Ethanol** (1 mark)
2.  **Butanoic acid** (1 mark)

steps.title: Identify the ester group
steps.content:
First, we look at the structural formula provided. We can identify the **ester functional group**, which is represented by the $-COO-$ linkage in the middle of the molecule. 

Esters are formed through a condensation reaction between a **carboxylic acid** and an **alcohol**. To find the starting materials, we need to analyze which part of the molecule came from which reactant.

steps.title: Cleave the ester bond
steps.content:
Using the **Structural Analysis** approach, we 'cleave' (cut) the molecule at the specific bond formed during esterification. This bond is always located between the **carbonyl carbon** ($C=O$) and the **single-bonded oxygen** ($-O-$).

$$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{C}(=\text{O}) \quad \bm{\text{|}} \quad \text{O}\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_3$$

Breaking the molecule here allows us to see the two precursor 'skeletons'.

steps.title: Identify the acid part
steps.content:
The part of the molecule containing the carbonyl group ($C=O$) comes from the **carboxylic acid**. 

Counting the carbons in this section:
1. Carbon 1 (the carbonyl carbon)
2. Carbon 2 (next $CH_2$)
3. Carbon 3 (next $CH_2$)
4. Carbon 4 (terminal $CH_3$)

A 4-carbon carboxylic acid is called **butanoic acid**. This corresponds to the 'butanoate' part of the name **ethyl butanoate**.

steps.title: Identify the alcohol part
steps.content:
The part of the molecule attached to the single-bonded oxygen comes from the **alcohol**. 

Counting the carbons in this section:
1. Carbon 1 ($CH_2$)
2. Carbon 2 ($CH_3$)

A 2-carbon alcohol is called **ethanol**. This corresponds to the 'ethyl' part of the name **ethyl butanoate**.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
Based on our analysis of the carbon chains on either side of the ester linkage, the two organic compounds used are:

1. **Butanoic acid**
2. **Ethanol**

According to the markscheme, you receive 1 mark for each correct name.

parts.content: Write an equation for the formation of ethyl butanoate from these compounds, including structural formulas.
parts.markscheme:
$$\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2COOH} + \mathrm{CH_3CH_2OH} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2COOCH_2CH_3} + \mathrm{H_2O}$$

<marks>1 mark</marks> for correct reactants, <marks>1 mark</marks> for correct product

steps.title: Analyze the ester components
steps.content:
To write the equation for the formation of an ester, we first need to identify the two components that make it up: the **carboxylic acid** and the **alcohol**. 

In the name **ethyl butanoate**:
*   The prefix **'butanoate'** indicates the ester was derived from **butanoic acid** (a 4-carbon carboxylic acid).
*   The prefix **'ethyl'** indicates the ester was derived from **ethanol** (a 2-carbon alcohol).

steps.title: Identify the carboxylic acid
steps.content:
Butanoic acid is a straight-chain carboxylic acid with four carbon atoms. Using the **condensed structural approach**, we represent the alkyl chain followed by the carboxyl functional group ($-COOH$).

Its formula is:
$$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{COOH}$$

steps.title: Identify the alcohol
steps.content:
Ethanol is a two-carbon alcohol. We write its condensed structural formula showing the ethyl group attached to the hydroxyl group ($-OH$).

Its formula is:
$$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$$

steps.title: Construct the balanced equation
steps.content:
Esterification is a **condensation reaction** that typically occurs in the presence of an acid catalyst. The carboxylic acid and alcohol react to form the ester and **water** ($H_2O$) as a byproduct. This reaction is reversible, so we use the equilibrium arrow ($\rightleftharpoons$).

Connecting the fragments, the final equation is:
$$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{COOH} + \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH} \rightleftharpoons \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{COOCH}_2\text{CH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

In the IB markscheme, you earn **1 mark** for the correct reactants and **1 mark** for the correct products (the ester and water).

steps.title: Analyze the ester structure
steps.content:
To write the formation equation, we first need to identify the two components of the ester: the **carboxylic acid** and the **alcohol**. 

Looking at the name **ethyl butanoate**:
- The prefix **ethyl** comes from the alcohol (ethanol, 2 carbons).
- The suffix **butanoate** comes from the carboxylic acid (butanoic acid, 4 carbons).

You can also see this in the provided skeletal structure: the part of the molecule containing the $C=O$ group has a 4-carbon chain, while the part attached to the single oxygen atom has a 2-carbon chain.

steps.title: Identify the carboxylic acid
steps.content:
The "butanoate" portion corresponds to **butanoic acid**. In a skeletal structure, this is represented by a 4-carbon chain ending in a carboxyl group ($\text{-COOH}$).

Converting this skeletal visual into a condensed structural formula, we get:
$$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{COOH}$$

steps.title: Identify the alcohol
steps.content:
The "ethyl" portion corresponds to **ethanol**. Visually, this is a 2-carbon skeletal chain with a hydroxyl group ($\text{-OH}$) attached.

Converting this to a condensed structural formula, we get:
$$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$$

steps.title: Write the condensation equation
steps.content:
In an esterification reaction, the carboxylic acid and alcohol react to form an ester and water. This is a reversible condensation reaction, so we use the equilibrium symbol ($\rightleftharpoons$).

Combine the reactants and products to form the final equation:

$$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{COOH} + \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH} \rightleftharpoons \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{COOCH}_2\text{CH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

As per the markscheme, you earn 1 mark for the correct reactants and 1 mark for the correct products (including the water molecule!).

parts.content: Outline the type of reaction.
parts.markscheme: Condensation reaction <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the functional group
steps.content:
To determine the reaction type, we first need to identify the functional group present in the molecule. Looking at the structural formula, we see a carbonyl group ($C=O$) directly bonded to an oxygen atom that is part of a carbon chain. 

This specific arrangement, $-COO-$, identifies the compound as an **ester**. Identifying functional groups is a key skill covered in **Topic S3.2: Functional groups**.

highlights.text: The compound below is commonly used in artificial fruit flavouring.

steps.title: Determine the reaction type
steps.content:
Esters are synthesized through the reaction of a carboxylic acid and an alcohol. During this process, the two molecules join together, and a small molecule (typically water, $H_2O$) is eliminated.

Any reaction where two molecules combine to form a larger molecule with the loss of a small molecule is classified as a **condensation reaction**. In this specific case, it is also known as esterification, but the broad category required by the IB syllabus for this process is condensation.

steps.title: Identify the functional group
steps.content: First, let's look at the structure provided. The molecule contains the functional group $-COO-$, which identifies it as an **ester**. In IB Chemistry, you'll learn that esters are typically formed from the reaction between a carboxylic acid and an alcohol.

steps.title: Identify the precursors
steps.content:
To understand how this molecule was formed, we can 'break' the ester bond (the $C-O$ single bond) to see the starting materials:

*   The part with the carbonyl group ($C=O$) comes from **ethanoic acid**: $CH_3COOH$
*   The part with the alkyl chain comes from **propan-1-ol**: $HOCH_2CH_2CH_3$

steps.title: Analyze the elimination
steps.content:
When these two precursors join together to form the ester linkage, a small molecule is 'squeezed out' or eliminated. 

Specifically, the $-OH$ group from the ethanoic acid and the $-H$ atom from the alcohol's hydroxyl group combine:

$$OH + H \rightarrow H_2O$$

This loss of a small molecule allows the remaining fragments to bond together to form the artificial flavoring.

steps.title: Classify the reaction
steps.content:
Because this reaction involves the joining of two molecules with the simultaneous **elimination of a small molecule** (water), it is classified as a **condensation reaction**.

In the context of polymers (which you can review in **Topic S2.4.6**), you will see this same logic applied to create long chains!

parts.content: Suggest why esters like ethyl butanoate are often used in perfumes and flavourings.
parts.markscheme: They are volatile and have pleasant fruity smells. <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the functional group
steps.content:
The compound shown in the image is an **ester**. In organic chemistry, esters are represented by the general formula $R-COOR'$. This specific ester is ethyl butanoate, which is formed from the reaction between ethanol and butanoic acid.

This topic is covered in **S3.2 Functional groups**, where you learn to recognize different classes of organic compounds.

steps.title: Analyze sensory properties
steps.content:
A key physical characteristic of esters is their **aroma**. Unlike many organic compounds that have pungent or unpleasant odors, esters typically have **pleasant, fruity, or sweet smells**. 

For example, ethyl butanoate specifically provides a scent similar to pineapple, which is why it is used in artificial fruit flavorings.

steps.title: Evaluate volatility
steps.content:
To be used in perfumes or as a flavoring, a compound must be **volatile**. Volatility is the ability of a substance to evaporate easily into a gas. 

Because esters have relatively weak intermolecular forces (compared to carboxylic acids or alcohols of similar mass), they evaporate at low temperatures. This allows the molecules to enter the air and reach the olfactory receptors in our nose, making the scent detectable.

steps.title: Synthesize the final answer
steps.content: To earn the mark, you must mention both the physical state and the sensory effect. Esters are used because they are **volatile** (they vaporize easily) and have **pleasant/fruity smells**.

highlights.text: They are volatile and have pleasant fruity smells.

parts.content: Predict the products of the complete hydrolysis of ethyl butanoate under acidic conditions.
parts.markscheme:
Butanoic acid ($\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2COOH}$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

Ethanol ($\mathrm{CH_3CH_2OH}$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Understand ester hydrolysis
steps.content:
In IB Chemistry, it is essential to remember that hydrolysis is the chemical breakdown of a compound due to reaction with water. For an ester like **ethyl butanoate**, hydrolysis is the reverse of esterification. Under acidic conditions, the ester reacts with water to reform the original **carboxylic acid** and **alcohol** used to make it.

$$\text{Ester} + \text{Water} \xrightarrow{\text{H}^+} \text{Carboxylic Acid} + \text{Alcohol}$$

highlights.text: complete hydrolysis of ethyl butanoate under acidic conditions

steps.title: Analyze the 'ethyl' prefix
steps.content:
Using **Nomenclature Analysis**, we look at the first part of the IUPAC name: **ethyl**. 

In ester naming, the first word always identifies the alkyl group that came from the **alcohol**. Since the prefix is 'ethyl' (representing a 2-carbon chain), the parent alcohol must be **ethanol**.

$$\text{Ethyl group} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH} \text{ (Ethanol)}$$

steps.title: Analyze the 'butanoate' suffix
steps.content:
Next, we look at the second part of the name: **butanoate**. 

The suffix **-oate** indicates the part of the molecule derived from the **carboxylic acid**. The root 'butan-' tells us there is a 4-carbon chain. Therefore, the parent carboxylic acid is **butanoic acid**.

$$\text{Butanoate group} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{COOH} \text{ (Butanoic acid)}$$

steps.title: Identify the final products
steps.content:
Under acidic conditions, these two components are released in their neutral forms. Unlike basic hydrolysis (saponification), which would produce a carboxylate salt, acidic hydrolysis yields the actual acid.

The two products are:
1.  **Butanoic acid** (1 mark)
2.  **Ethanol** (1 mark)

To review how these functional groups are named, you can check **Topic S3.2: Functional groups** in your syllabus.

steps.title: Identify the ester linkage
steps.content:
To predict the products of hydrolysis, we first identify the ester functional group in ethyl butanoate. An ester has the general structure $\text{R-COO-R'}$. In this specific molecule, the 'butanoate' part comes from a 4-carbon chain (including the carbonyl carbon), and the 'ethyl' part comes from a 2-carbon chain attached to the oxygen.

This is covered in **Structure 3.2: Functional groups**, where we learn to classify and name organic compounds.

highlights.text: complete hydrolysis of ethyl butanoate under acidic conditions

steps.title: Locate the cleavage site
steps.content:
Using the structural cleavage approach, we locate the single bond between the carbonyl carbon ($\text{C=O}$) and the alkoxy oxygen ($\text{-O-}$). During hydrolysis, this is the bond that breaks (cleaves).

$$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{C}(=\text{O}) \boldsymbol{-} \text{OCH}_2\text{CH}_3$$

steps.title: Add water components
steps.content:
Hydrolysis literally means "splitting with water." We add the components of a water molecule ($\text{H-OH}$) across the bond we just broke:

1. Add the hydroxyl group ($-\text{OH}$) to the **carbonyl group** (the side that originally came from the acid).
2. Add the hydrogen atom ($-\text{H}$) to the **alkoxy group** (the side that originally came from the alcohol).

$$\begin{aligned} \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CO-} &+ \text{-OH} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{COOH} \\ \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{O-} &+ \text{-H} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Name the final products
steps.content:
The two products formed are:
- **Butanoic acid**: $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{COOH}$
- **Ethanol**: $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$

Note: Under acidic conditions, the carboxylic acid remains in its protonated form rather than forming a carboxylate salt.

parts.content: Describe how infrared (IR) spectroscopy could be used to distinguish ethyl butanoate from the carboxylic acid used to make it.
parts.markscheme:
IR spectrum of ethyl butanoate shows strong absorption around $1750 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ ($\mathrm{C=O}$ in ester). <marks>1 mark</marks>

Butanoic acid shows broad O–H stretch around $2500\text{--}3300 \text{ cm}^{-1}$. <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the functional groups
steps.content:
To distinguish between these two compounds using IR spectroscopy, we first need to identify the functional groups present in each:

*   **Ethyl butanoate:** This is an ester. It contains a carbonyl group ($C=O$).
*   **Butanoic acid:** This is a carboxylic acid (the acid used to make the ester). It contains both a carbonyl group ($C=O$) and a hydroxyl group ($O-H$).

This question is related to **S3.2.9: Infrared spectra**, where we learn that different functional groups absorb specific frequencies of infrared radiation.

highlights.text: distinguish ethyl butanoate from the carboxylic acid

steps.title: Identify the O-H signal
steps.content:
The most significant difference is the presence of the hydroxyl group in the acid. 

Butanoic acid will show a very characteristic **broad** absorption band due to the $O-H$ stretch. According to the markscheme and standard IB data, this occurs in the range:

$$2500\text{--}3300 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$

This broad peak will be absent in the IR spectrum of the ester (ethyl butanoate).

steps.title: Identify the C=O signal
steps.content:
Both molecules contain a carbonyl group, but they appear at slightly different wavenumbers. 

The IR spectrum of **ethyl butanoate** will show a strong, sharp absorption band for the $C=O$ bond at approximately:

$$1750 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$

While the carboxylic acid also has a $C=O$ peak (usually around $1700\text{--}1725 \text{ cm}^{-1}$), the combination of the specific ester $C=O$ peak and the absence of the $O-H$ peak allows us to confirm the identity of the ester.

steps.title: Summary for marks
steps.content:
To earn both marks, ensure you state both specific features:

1.  **Butanoic acid:** Look for the broad $O-H$ absorption at $2500\text{--}3300 \text{ cm}^{-1}$.
2.  **Ethyl butanoate:** Look for the strong $C=O$ absorption around $1750 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ (and the absence of the broad $O-H$ peak).

steps.title: Identify the molecules
steps.content: To distinguish between the two substances, we first identify their functional groups. **Ethyl butanoate** is an ester, while **butanoic acid** is a carboxylic acid. IR spectroscopy identifies these based on how their bonds vibrate when absorbing infrared radiation.

steps.title: Analyze the Fingerprint Region
steps.content: One definitive way to identify a compound is by looking at the **fingerprint region**, which is the complex area of the spectrum typically below $1500\text{ cm}^{-1}$. Each organic molecule has a unique, complex pattern of absorption peaks in this region, much like a human fingerprint.

steps.title: Compare with Reference Standards
steps.content: To distinguish the ester from the acid using this method, the IR spectrum of the unknown sample is compared against a **database of known reference standards**. If the peaks below $1500\text{ cm}^{-1}$ match the reference spectrum for ethyl butanoate exactly, the identity is confirmed.

steps.title: Identify Diagnostic Peaks
steps.content:
While the fingerprint region is unique, IB markschemes usually require you to identify the major functional group peaks in the diagnostic region (above $1500\text{ cm}^{-1}$):

1.  **Ethyl butanoate (Ester):** Shows a strong, sharp absorption for the $\text{C=O}$ bond around $1750\text{ cm}^{-1}$.
2.  **Butanoic acid (Carboxylic Acid):** Shows a very broad $\text{O-H}$ absorption stretch between $2500$ and $3300\text{ cm}^{-1}$ (often overlapping with $\text{C-H}$ peaks).

ti84.instructions: Add the upper and lower limits of the range and divide by 2.

desmos.instructions: Find the midpoint of the O-H stretch range.

calculator.description: Calculate the center of the broad O-H absorption range for carboxylic acids.

steps.title: Summarize for the Markscheme
steps.content:
To secure both marks, state that:
- The ester spectrum has a strong $\text{C=O}$ peak at $\approx 1750\text{ cm}^{-1}$.
- The acid spectrum has a broad $\text{O-H}$ peak at $\approx 2500\text{--}3300\text{ cm}^{-1}$.

You can further note that these compounds are definitively distinguished by comparing their unique patterns in the **fingerprint region** ($< 1500\text{ cm}^{-1}$) to reference spectra.

parts.content: Define structural isomerism.
parts.markscheme: Compounds with the same molecular formula but different structural arrangements of atoms [1]

steps.title: Identify the core requirement
steps.content: In organic chemistry, specifically in **Topic S3.2.6**, we study how the same set of atoms can be put together in different ways. The question asks for the standard definition of this phenomenon, known as **structural isomerism**.

steps.title: Break down the definition
steps.content:
To get the mark for this definition, you must mention two specific criteria:

1.  **Same Molecular Formula**: The isomers must contain the exact same number and type of atoms (e.g., both must be $C_4H_{10}O$).
2.  **Different Structural Arrangement**: The sequence in which these atoms are bonded together must be different (leading to a different structural formula).

steps.title: Final Definition
steps.content:
Structural isomerism refers to compounds that have the **same molecular formula** but **different structural arrangements** of their atoms.

In the context of the question, while both alcohols share the formula $C_4H_{10}O$, they might differ by the position of the $-OH$ group or the branching of the carbon chain.

parts.content: Name and draw the displayed structures of one straight-chain primary alcohol and one tertiary alcohol with the molecular formula $\mathrm{C_4H_{10}O}$.
parts.markscheme:
Straight-chain primary alcohol: Butan-1-ol (name) and correct displayed structure [1]
Tertiary alcohol: 2-methylpropan-2-ol (name) and correct displayed structure [1]

Note: Both name and structure are required for each mark.

steps.title: Analyze the formula and requirements
steps.content:
The molecular formula is $\mathrm{C_4H_{10}O}$. Since it follows the general formula $\mathrm{C_nH_{2n+2}O}$, we are looking for saturated alcohols. We need to derive two specific isomers based on their classification:

1.  **Straight-chain primary alcohol**: The carbon chain must be unbranched, and the $-\text{OH}$ group must be attached to a carbon that is bonded to only one other carbon.
2.  **Tertiary alcohol**: The $-\text{OH}$ group must be attached to a carbon that is bonded to three other carbons.

steps.title: Derive the straight-chain primary alcohol
steps.content:
For a **straight-chain** alcohol with 4 carbons, the skeleton is a simple line: $\text{C}-\text{C}-\text{C}-\text{C}$. 

To make it **primary**, we must place the hydroxyl ($-\text{OH}$) group on an end carbon (Carbon 1). 

**Name:** Butan-1-ol

**Displayed Structure:**
$$\begin{array}{ccccc} & \text{H} & \text{H} & \text{H} & \text{H} \\ & | & | & | & | \\ \text{H} & -\text{C} & -\text{C} & -\text{C} & -\text{C} & -\text{O} - \text{H} \\ & | & | & | & | \\ & \text{H} & \text{H} & \text{H} & \text{H} \end{array}$$

steps.title: Derive the tertiary alcohol
steps.content:
For a **tertiary** alcohol with 4 carbons, the carbon bonded to the $-\text{OH}$ group must be bonded to 3 other carbons. This forces a branched skeleton where a central carbon is surrounded by three methyl groups. 

According to IUPAC rules, the longest chain is 3 carbons long, with a methyl group and a hydroxyl group both on the second carbon.

**Name:** 2-methylpropan-2-ol

**Displayed Structure:**
$$\begin{array}{ccccccc} & & \text{H} & & \\ & & | & & \\ & \text{H} & -\text{C} & -\text{H} & \\ & & | & & \\ \text{H} & & \text{C} & & \text{H} \\ | & & | & & | \\ \text{H} - \text{C} & - & \text{C} & - & \text{C} - \text{H} \\ | & & | & & | \\ \text{H} & & \text{O} & & \text{H} \\ & & | & & \\ & & \text{H} & & \end{array}$$

steps.title: Final Review
steps.content:
This question tests your knowledge of **Topic S3.2 Functional groups**. To earn full marks, ensure every single bond (including $\text{O}-\text{H}$ and $\text{C}-\text{H}$) is shown in your displayed drawing and that the IUPAC names correctly identify the positions of functional groups.

- **Straight-chain primary:** Butan-1-ol
- **Tertiary:** 2-methylpropan-2-ol

steps.title: Define the classification criteria
steps.content:
To solve this using the **Isomer Identification and Filtering** approach, we first need to define the categories mentioned in the question:

1.  **Straight-chain:** Carbon atoms are connected in a single continuous line without any branches.
2.  **Primary ($1^{\circ}$) alcohol:** The carbon atom bonded to the $-OH$ group is attached to only **one** other carbon atom.
3.  **Tertiary ($3^{\circ}$) alcohol:** The carbon atom bonded to the $-OH$ group is attached to **three** other carbon atoms.

This knowledge comes from **Topic S3.2: Functional groups**, which covers the classification of organic compounds.

steps.title: Filter for the primary alcohol
steps.content:
Let's evaluate the possible isomers of $\mathrm{C_4H_{10}O}$ to find the **straight-chain primary** alcohol:

*   **Butan-1-ol:** A 4-carbon straight chain with the $-OH$ on the end (C1). This is a **straight-chain primary alcohol**. 
*   **Butan-2-ol:** A 4-carbon straight chain, but the $-OH$ is on C2. This makes it a secondary alcohol. 
*   **2-methylpropan-1-ol:** This has a branched chain, so it fails the "straight-chain" criteria.

The correct match is **butan-1-ol**.

steps.title: Draw Butan-1-ol
steps.content:
In a **displayed structure**, you must show every single atom and every single bond, including the $O-H$ bond. 

**Name:** Butan-1-ol

**Structure:**
$$\begin{array}{ccccccc} & H & H & H & H & \\ & | & | & | & | & \\ H - & C & - C & - C & - C & - O - H \\ & | & | & | & | & \\ & H & H & H & H & \end{array}$$

steps.title: Filter for the tertiary alcohol
steps.content:
Now we filter the isomers to find the **tertiary** alcohol. A tertiary alcohol requires the $-OH$ carbon to be bonded to three other carbons. This structure must be branched to accommodate this:

*   **2-methylpropan-2-ol:** The central carbon is bonded to three methyl ($-CH_3$) groups and one $-OH$ group. This fits the definition of a tertiary alcohol perfectly.

The correct match is **2-methylpropan-2-ol**.

steps.title: Draw 2-methylpropan-2-ol
steps.content:
Again, ensure every bond is visible. The central carbon is the "hub" for the three branches and the hydroxyl group.

**Name:** 2-methylpropan-2-ol

**Structure:**
$$\begin{array}{ccccc} & & H & & \\ & & | & & \\ & H & - C - & H & \\ & & | & & \\ H & H & C & H & H \\ | & | & | & | & | \\ H - C - & C & - & C - H \\ | & | & | & | & | \\ H & & O & & H \\ & & | & & \\ & & H & & \end{array}$$

*Note: In the exam, you earn 1 mark for each correctly named and drawn pair.*

parts.content: Describe one physical **and** one chemical difference between these two isomers, and explain the reason for the difference in their boiling points.
parts.markscheme:
Physical: Butan-1-ol has a higher boiling point than 2-methylpropan-2-ol due to stronger London (dispersion) forces / less branching allows for greater surface area contact [1]
Chemical: Butan-1-ol can be oxidized (by acidified $\mathrm{K_2Cr_2O_7}$ / $\mathrm{KMnO_4}$), whereas 2-methylpropan-2-ol resists oxidation under the same conditions [1]

steps.title: Identify the Isomer Structures
steps.content:
To compare these two isomers, we first look at their structural classifications:

1.  **Butan-1-ol**: A **primary alcohol**. It has a straight-chain structure where the carbon attached to the hydroxyl group ($-\text{OH}$) is bonded to only one other carbon.
2.  **2-methylpropan-2-ol**: A **tertiary alcohol**. It is highly branched, with the carbon attached to the $-\text{OH}$ group bonded to three other carbons.

This difference in "branching" is the key to understanding both their physical and chemical behaviors. These concepts are covered in **Topic S3.2 Functional groups**.

steps.title: Physical Difference: Boiling Points
steps.content:
The physical difference lies in their **boiling points**. Although both isomers can form hydrogen bonds, they differ in their **London (dispersion) forces**.

*   **Butan-1-ol** has a long, straight-chain shape. This provides a **greater surface area** for contact between molecules, resulting in stronger London dispersion forces.
*   **2-methylpropan-2-ol** is branched and more spherical. This compact shape **decreases the surface area** available for contact, leading to weaker London dispersion forces.

Because butan-1-ol has stronger overall intermolecular forces, it requires more energy to boil, giving it a **higher boiling point**.

highlights.text: Butan-1-ol has a higher boiling point than 2-methylpropan-2-ol due to stronger London (dispersion) forces / less branching allows for greater surface area contact

steps.title: Chemical Difference: Oxidation Susceptibility
steps.content:
The chemical difference involves their reaction with oxidizing agents like acidified $\text{K}_2\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7$ or $\text{KMnO}_4$:

*   **Butan-1-ol (Primary)**: It has two hydrogen atoms attached to the carbon atom that holds the hydroxyl group (the $\alpha$-hydrogens). This allows it to be **oxidized** first to an aldehyde (butanal) and then to a carboxylic acid (butanoic acid).
*   **2-methylpropan-2-ol (Tertiary)**: The carbon holding the $-\text{OH}$ group has no $\alpha$-hydrogens. Breaking a $\text{C}-\text{C}$ bond is much harder than breaking a $\text{C}-\text{H}$ bond, so it **resists oxidation** under standard conditions.

You can review these redox reactions in **Topic R3.2.9 Oxidation of organic compounds**.

highlights.text: Butan-1-ol can be oxidized (by acidified K2Cr2O7 / KMnO4), whereas 2-methylpropan-2-ol resists oxidation under the same conditions
