parts.content: Outline why the compounds in the table are known as hydrocarbons.
parts.markscheme: They are composed of carbon and hydrogen only. <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Interpret the term
steps.content: To understand why these are called **hydrocarbons**, we can start by looking at the name itself. It is a compound word formed from **hydrogen** and **carbon**. This gives us a direct clue about the chemical composition of the molecules listed in the table.

steps.title: Analyze chemical composition
steps.content:
Using the **Chemical Composition Analysis** approach, we examine the molecular formulas provided in the data table:

- Ethane: $C_2H_6$
- Ethene: $C_2H_4$
- Butane: $C_4H_{10}$
- Hexene: $C_6H_{12}$

Notice that every single formula in the list contains only the symbols $C$ (carbon) and $H$ (hydrogen). There are no other elements, such as oxygen or nitrogen, present in any of these structures.

steps.title: State the definition
steps.content:
In IB Chemistry, specifically in **Topic S3.2: Functional groups**, we define a hydrocarbon based on this observation. A hydrocarbon is a compound that is composed of **carbon and hydrogen atoms only**.

According to the markscheme, the key requirement for this mark is to mention that these compounds contain *only* these two specific elements.

parts.content: State the structural feature of the alkenes ethene, butene and hexene.
parts.markscheme: Carbon-carbon double bond / $\text{C}=\text{C}$ <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the naming pattern
steps.content:
To solve this, we first look at the names of the specific compounds mentioned in the question: **ethene**, **butene**, and **hexene**. 

In organic chemistry, the suffix (the ending of the name) tells us which **homologous series** the compound belongs to. All three of these compounds end in **-ene**.

steps.title: Recognize the homologous series
steps.content:
As covered in **Topic S3.2: Functional groups**, the suffix **-ene** is the identifying marker for the **alkene** homologous series. 

Members of the same homologous series share the same general formula and, most importantly, the same **functional group** or structural feature.

steps.title: Identify the structural feature
steps.content:
The defining structural feature of all alkenes is the presence of at least one **carbon-carbon double bond**. 

In your IB exams, you can write this out in words or show it as a chemical symbol:

$$\text{C}=\text{C}$$

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
Based on the markscheme, the correct answer is simply stating the feature that makes these molecules alkenes.

**Answer:**
Carbon-carbon double bond (or $C=C$)

steps.title: Compare molecular formulas
steps.content:
To find the shared structural feature, let's compare the molecular formulas of the alkanes and alkenes provided in the table for a given number of carbon atoms:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Ethane } (C_2H_6) &\rightarrow \text{Ethene } (C_2H_4) \\
\text{Butane } (C_4H_{10}) &\rightarrow \text{Butene } (C_4H_8) \\
\text{Hexane } (C_6H_{14}) &\rightarrow \text{Hexene } (C_6H_{12})
\end{aligned}$$

Notice that in every case, the alkene has exactly two fewer hydrogen atoms than the corresponding alkane.

steps.title: Analyze the H:C ratio
steps.content:
Alkanes are **saturated** hydrocarbons with the general formula $C_nH_{2n+2}$. 

The alkenes in the table follow the general formula $C_nH_{2n}$. This lower hydrogen-to-carbon ratio indicates a **degree of unsaturation**. For every two hydrogens "missing" compared to the saturated alkane, the molecule must contain a structural feature that allows carbon to maintain its four required bonds without those extra hydrogens.

steps.title: Identify the structural feature
steps.content:
To satisfy the valency of carbon (which must form 4 bonds), the "missing" hydrogen atoms are compensated for by the presence of a **carbon-carbon double bond** ($C=C$). 

This is the defining functional group for the alkene homologous series, as covered in **Topic S3.2: Functional groups**.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content: The structural feature common to ethene, butene, and hexene is the **carbon-carbon double bond** (or $C=C$).

parts.content: Explain why hexane is a liquid at $20^\circ\text{C}$ and ethane is a gas.
parts.markscheme:
Hexane has a higher molar mass <marks>1</marks>

and stronger London dispersion forces than ethane <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify intermolecular forces
steps.content:
To understand why hexane and ethane have different states of matter, we first need to look at their structure. Both hexane ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_{14}$) and ethane ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_6$) are non-polar hydrocarbons. 

Because they are non-polar, the only intermolecular forces acting between their molecules are **London dispersion forces (LDF)**. This is a key concept from **Topic S2.2.8: Intermolecular forces**.

steps.title: Compare molar masses
steps.content:
The strength of London dispersion forces depends heavily on the size of the molecule. We can quantify this by comparing their molar masses using the periodic table values ($C = 12.01, H = 1.01$):

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Molar mass of ethane } (\text{C}_2\text{H}_6) &= 2(12.01) + 6(1.01) = 30.08 \text{ g mol}^{-1} \\ \text{Molar mass of hexane } (\text{C}_6\text{H}_{14}) &= 6(12.01) + 14(1.01) = 86.20 \text{ g mol}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

Hexane has a significantly **higher molar mass** than ethane.

gdc.instructions: Enter the atomic mass sums: 2*12.01 + 6*1.01 for ethane and 6*12.01 + 14*1.01 for hexane.

ti84.instructions: Enter the atomic mass sums: 2*12.01 + 6*1.01 for ethane and 6*12.01 + 14*1.01 for hexane.

desmos.instructions: Enter the atomic mass sums: 2*12.01 + 6*1.01 for ethane and 6*12.01 + 14*1.01 for hexane.

calculator.description: Verify the molar mass calculations.

steps.title: Relate size to LDF strength
steps.content:
Since hexane is a larger molecule with more electrons, it has a larger electron cloud. This makes the molecule more polarizable, which leads to **stronger London dispersion forces** compared to those in ethane.

In the IB markscheme, you earn the first mark for identifying the higher molar mass and the second mark for connecting it to stronger LDF.

steps.title: Connect to state of matter
steps.content:
Stronger intermolecular forces require more energy to overcome. Because hexane's LDF are much stronger than ethane's, its boiling point ($69^\circ\text{C}$) is much higher than room temperature ($20^\circ\text{C}$), meaning it stays as a **liquid**. 

In contrast, ethane's weak LDF result in a boiling point of $-89^\circ\text{C}$, so it has already boiled and exists as a **gas** at room temperature.

steps.title: Compare chain length
steps.content:
To understand why these substances have different states of matter, we first look at their molecular structures. Hexane ($C_6H_{14}$) has a much longer carbon chain than ethane ($C_2H_6$). 

Because hexane has more atoms, it also has a significantly higher number of electrons and a larger molar mass. This is a key concept covered in **Topic S3.2.4: Physical trends in homologous series**.

steps.title: Surface area and polarizability
steps.content:
The longer chain in hexane provides a larger **surface area** for contact between molecules. Combined with the higher number of electrons, this makes the electron cloud in hexane more **polarizable** (easier to distort). 

Greater polarizability leads to more frequent and stronger temporary dipoles forming between molecules.

steps.title: Identify intermolecular forces
steps.content:
Both molecules are non-polar hydrocarbons, so they only experience **London dispersion forces** (LDFs). 

Because hexane has a larger surface area and more electrons, its London dispersion forces are significantly stronger than those in ethane. This aligns with **Topic S2.2.8: Intermolecular forces** in your syllabus.

steps.title: Link to boiling point
steps.content:
Stronger intermolecular forces require more energy to overcome. 

As seen in the table, hexane's boiling point ($+69^\circ\text{C}$) is much higher than room temperature ($20^\circ\text{C}$), so it remains a **liquid**. Ethane's boiling point ($-89^\circ\text{C}$) is far below room temperature, so it has already turned into a **gas**.

parts.content: Deduce the molecular and empirical formula of propene.
parts.markscheme:
Empirical formula: $\text{CH}_2$ <marks>1</marks>

Molecular formula: $\text{C}_3\text{H}_6$ <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the homologous series
steps.content: First, we look at the name **propene**. The suffix **-ene** tells us that this compound belongs to the **alkene** homologous series. Alkenes are hydrocarbons that contain at least one carbon-carbon double bond.

steps.title: Determine the carbon count
steps.content: Next, we use IUPAC nomenclature rules (which you can review in **Topic S3.2.5**) to identify the number of carbon atoms from the prefix. The prefix **prop-** indicates that there are exactly **3 carbon atoms** in the molecule ($n = 3$).

steps.title: Find the molecular formula
steps.content:
The general formula for any alkene is $$C_nH_{2n}$$. Since we know $n = 3$ for propene, we can substitute this value into the formula:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Number of C atoms} &= 3 \\ \text{Number of H atoms} &= 2 \times 3 = 6 \end{aligned}$$

This gives us the **molecular formula**: $$\text{C}_3\text{H}_6$$

ti84.instructions: Multiply 2 by 3

calculator.description: Calculate the number of hydrogen atoms for n=3

steps.title: Simplify for empirical formula
steps.content:
The **empirical formula** is the simplest whole-number ratio of the atoms in a compound (as covered in **Topic S1.4.4**). 

For $\text{C}_3\text{H}_6$, the ratio of $C:H$ is $3:6$. We can simplify this by dividing both numbers by their greatest common divisor, which is 3:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{C} &= \frac{3}{3} = 1 \\ \text{H} &= \frac{6}{3} = 2 \end{aligned}$$

Therefore, the **empirical formula** is: $$\text{CH}_2$$

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content: The molecular formula is $$\text{C}_3\text{H}_6$$ and the empirical formula is $$\text{CH}_2$$. You earn 1 mark for each correct formula.

steps.title: Interpret the name "propene"
steps.content:
To deduce the formula, we first look at the name of the hydrocarbon. In IUPAC nomenclature (which you can review in **Topic S3.2.5**):
- The prefix **"prop-"** indicates that the molecule contains exactly **three carbon atoms**.
- The suffix **"-ene"** indicates that it belongs to the alkene homologous series, meaning it contains at least one **carbon-carbon double bond** ($C=C$).

steps.title: Sketch the structural diagram
steps.content:
We can now sketch the structure. Carbon atoms must always form four bonds to be stable. We place three carbons in a chain with one double bond:

1. Start with the carbon chain: $C=C-C$
2. Add hydrogen atoms so each Carbon has 4 bonds total:
   - The first carbon is double-bonded, so it needs **2 hydrogens**.
   - The middle carbon has a double and a single bond (3 bonds), so it needs **1 hydrogen**.
   - The last carbon is single-bonded, so it needs **3 hydrogens**.

The structure looks like this: 

$$CH_2=CH-CH_3$$

steps.title: Determine the molecular formula
steps.content:
The **molecular formula** shows the actual number of atoms of each element in the molecule. By counting the atoms in our structural sketch:
- Total Carbon (C) atoms = $1 + 1 + 1 = 3$
- Total Hydrogen (H) atoms = $2 + 1 + 3 = 6$

Therefore, the molecular formula is:

$$C_3H_6$$

steps.title: Determine the empirical formula
steps.content:
The **empirical formula** is the simplest whole-number ratio of atoms of each element in a compound. We take the subscripts from the molecular formula ($C_3H_6$) and divide them by their greatest common divisor, which is 3.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Ratio of C} &= \frac{3}{3} = 1 \\ \text{Ratio of H} &= \frac{6}{3} = 2 \end{aligned}$$

This gives us a $1:2$ ratio, so the empirical formula is:

$$CH_2$$

gdc.instructions: Divide 6 by 3 to simplify the ratio.

ti84.instructions: Divide the number of hydrogens by the number of carbons to find the ratio.

calculator.description: Simplify the atom ratio by dividing by the greatest common divisor (3).

steps.title: Identify the pattern
steps.content:
First, let's look at the compounds in the table that end in **-ene**. By looking at their molecular formulas, we can see a clear relationship between the number of carbon ($C$) and hydrogen ($H$) atoms:

*   **Ethene**: $C_2H_4$ ($2 \times 2 = 4$)
*   **Butene**: $C_4H_8$ ($4 \times 2 = 8$)
*   **Hexene**: $C_6H_{12}$ ($6 \times 2 = 12$)

In every case, the number of hydrogen atoms is exactly **double** the number of carbon atoms. This gives us a ratio of $1:2$.

steps.title: Determine carbon count
steps.content:
Next, we need to determine how many carbon atoms are in **propene**. 

Following the naming convention (IUPAC nomenclature) shown in the table:
*   **Eth-** means 2 carbons
*   **But-** means 4 carbons
*   **Hex-** means 6 carbons

The prefix **Prop-** represents the 3-carbon intermediate in this sequence. Therefore, propene has **3 carbon atoms**.

steps.title: Deduce molecular formula
steps.content:
Now we apply the $1:2$ ratio we identified in Step 0 to the 3 carbon atoms:

$$\text{Number of H atoms} = 3 \times 2 = 6$$

So, the **molecular formula** for propene is **$C_3H_6$**.

ti84.instructions: Enter the number of carbons and multiply by 2.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of hydrogen atoms using the 1:2 ratio for 3 carbons.

steps.title: Find empirical formula
steps.content:
The **empirical formula** is the simplest whole-number ratio of atoms in a compound. 

For $C_3H_6$, we can divide both subscripts by their greatest common divisor, which is 3:

$$\frac{C_3}{3} \rightarrow C_1$$
$$\frac{H_6}{3} \rightarrow H_2$$

This gives us an **empirical formula** of **$CH_2$**.

parts.content: Predict the physical state of propene at $20^\circ\text{C}$, giving a reason for your answer.
parts.markscheme: Gas because butene, with four carbon atoms in the longest chain, is also a gas. <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify propene in the series
steps.content:
First, let's identify what propene is. Propene ($C_3H_6$) is an alkene with 3 carbon atoms. In the provided table, we can see data for other alkenes: ethene ($C_2H_4$) and butene ($C_4H_8$).

This question is related to **Topic S3.2.4: Physical trends in homologous series**, where we study how properties like boiling point change as the carbon chain gets longer.

steps.title: Analyze the surrounding data
steps.content:
To predict the state of propene, we can look at the alkenes that are slightly smaller and slightly larger than it:

*   **Ethene ($C_2H_4$):** 2 carbons, Boiling Point = $-104^\circ\text{C}$ (Gas at $20^\circ\text{C}$)
*   **Butene ($C_4H_8$):** 4 carbons, Boiling Point = $-6^\circ\text{C}$ (Gas at $20^\circ\text{C}$)

Propene ($C_3H_6$) sits directly between these two in the homologous series.

steps.title: Interpolate the physical state
steps.content:
Interpolation involves estimating a value that falls between two known values. 

Since ethene (2 carbons) is a gas and butene (4 carbons) is also a gas at $20^\circ\text{C}$, we can logically conclude that the 3-carbon alkene between them must also be a gas at that same temperature.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The physical state of propene at $20^\circ\text{C}$ is **gas**.

**Reason:** Propene contains 3 carbons, which is between ethene (2 carbons) and butene (4 carbons). Since both of these reference points are gases at room temperature, propene must also be a gas.

steps.title: Identify the trend
steps.content: To predict the state of propene ($C_3H_6$), we look at the relationship between the number of carbon atoms and the boiling point in a homologous series. As discussed in **Topic S2.2.9: Intermolecular forces and physical properties**, the boiling point of hydrocarbons increases as the carbon chain length increases.

steps.title: Explain London dispersion forces
steps.content:
Hydrocarbons are non-polar, so the only intermolecular forces acting between them are **London dispersion forces**. 

As the number of carbon atoms increases, the molar mass and the size of the electron cloud increase. This leads to stronger London dispersion forces, which require more energy to overcome, resulting in a higher boiling point.

steps.title: Compare propene to butene
steps.content:
Let's compare propene ($C_3H_6$) to the next alkene in the series provided in the table: butene ($C_4H_8$).

* Butene has 4 carbons and a boiling point of $-6^\circ\text{C}$.
* Propene has only 3 carbons.

Because propene has a shorter carbon chain, it has weaker London dispersion forces than butene. Therefore, its boiling point must be lower than $-6^\circ\text{C}$.

steps.title: Determine the physical state
steps.content:
We are asked for the state at room temperature ($20^\circ\text{C}$). 

If butene is already a gas at $20^\circ\text{C}$ (because its boiling point of $-6^\circ\text{C}$ is much lower than room temperature), and propene has an even lower boiling point, then propene must also be a gas.

steps.title: Final prediction
steps.content:
The physical state of propene at $20^\circ\text{C}$ is **gas**.

**Reasoning:** Propene has a shorter carbon chain than butene, resulting in weaker London dispersion forces and a lower boiling point. Since butene is a gas at room temperature, propene must also be a gas.

parts.content: Deduce equations for the complete and incomplete combustion of ethene.
parts.markscheme:
Complete combustion: $\text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + 3\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ <marks>1</marks>

Incomplete combustion: $\text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify reactants and products
steps.content:
To solve this, we use the **Systematic Stoichiometric Balancing** method. First, we identify the chemical formula for ethene ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_4$) from the table. 

In IB Chemistry, combustion reactions follow specific conventions:
*   **Complete combustion**: Occurs in excess oxygen, producing carbon dioxide ($\text{CO}_2$) and water ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$).
*   **Incomplete combustion**: Occurs in limited oxygen, producing carbon monoxide ($\text{CO}$) and water ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$) (or sometimes carbon/soot, but we follow the markscheme's preference for $\text{CO}$ here).

These concepts are covered in **Topic R1.3: Energy from fuels**.

steps.title: Balance complete combustion
steps.content:
We start with the skeleton equation for complete combustion:
$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Following the systematic approach, we balance atoms in this order: Carbon (C), then Hydrogen (H), then Oxygen (O).

1.  **Carbon**: 2 carbons on the left, so we need 2 carbons on the right.
    $$\text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
2.  **Hydrogen**: 4 hydrogens on the left, so we need 4 on the right ($2 \times 2 = 4$).
    $$\text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$
3.  **Oxygen**: Count the oxygens on the right side:
    $$2(2) + 2(1) = 6$$
    To get 6 oxygens on the left, we need $3\text{O}_2$.

gdc.instructions: Sum the oxygen atoms from the products.

ti84.instructions: Calculate the total oxygen atoms in 2CO2 and 2H2O.

desmos.instructions: Evaluate the expression for total oxygen atoms.

calculator.description: Verify oxygen atom count for complete combustion products.

steps.title: Final complete combustion equation
steps.content:
The balanced equation for the complete combustion of ethene is:

$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_4(\text{g}) + 3\text{O}_2(\text{g}) \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2(\text{g}) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(\text{l})$$

This earns the first mark.

steps.title: Balance incomplete combustion
steps.content:
Now we follow the same steps for incomplete combustion, where $\text{CO}$ is produced instead of $\text{CO}_2$:

1.  **Carbon**: 2 carbons on the left, so we need 2 on the right.
    $$\text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
2.  **Hydrogen**: 4 hydrogens on the left, so we need 4 on the right.
    $$\text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$
3.  **Oxygen**: Count the oxygens on the right side:
    $$2(1) + 2(1) = 4$$
    To get 4 oxygens on the left, we need $2\text{O}_2$.

gdc.instructions: Sum the oxygen atoms from the products.

ti84.instructions: Calculate the total oxygen atoms in 2CO and 2H2O.

desmos.instructions: Evaluate the expression for total oxygen atoms.

calculator.description: Verify oxygen atom count for incomplete combustion products.

steps.title: Final incomplete combustion equation
steps.content:
The balanced equation for the incomplete combustion of ethene is:

$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_4(\text{g}) + 2\text{O}_2(\text{g}) \rightarrow 2\text{CO}(\text{g}) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(\text{l})$$

This earns the second mark. Note that state symbols are usually not required in these specific IB questions unless stated, but they are good practice!

parts.content: Outline the difference between the two types of combustion in terms of the amount of energy released.
parts.markscheme: Complete combustion releases more energy per mole than incomplete combustion. <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the task
steps.content: The question asks for the difference in energy release between complete and incomplete combustion. This is a fundamental concept in **Topic R1.3: Energy from fuels**, specifically looking at how the availability of oxygen affects the energy output of a reaction.

steps.title: Analyze oxidation states
steps.content:
In chemistry, the amount of energy released from a fuel is related to the extent of its oxidation. 

*   **Complete combustion** occurs when there is an excess of oxygen, leading to the **full oxidation** of the hydrocarbon into $\text{CO}_2$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$.
*   **Incomplete combustion** occurs with limited oxygen, resulting in **partial oxidation** (producing carbon monoxide, $\text{CO}$, or carbon soot, $\text{C}$).

steps.title: Compare energy per mole
steps.content: Because the hydrocarbon undergoes full oxidation during complete combustion, the maximum possible chemical potential energy is released. Consequently, complete combustion releases a greater amount of energy per mole than incomplete combustion.

highlights.text: Complete combustion releases more energy per mole than incomplete combustion.

Aldehyde <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Do not accept "carbonyl" as this is the name of the functional group, not the class of compound.</note>

parts.content: Compound A and Compound B are structural isomers. With reference to their structures, distinguish between the classification of Compound A and Compound B as alcohols.
parts.markscheme:
- Compound A is a primary ($1^{\circ}$) alcohol because the carbon atom bonded to the hydroxyl ($-\mathrm{OH}$) group is attached to only one other carbon atom. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Compound B is a secondary ($2^{\circ}$) alcohol because the carbon atom bonded to the hydroxyl ($-\mathrm{OH}$) group is attached to two other carbon atoms. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award [1 max] if the student correctly identifies the classifications but provides no structural reasoning.</note>

parts.content: Using the data in the table, compare the boiling points of Compound C and Compound D. Explain this difference by referring to the types of intermolecular forces present in each compound.
parts.markscheme:
- Compound D has a significantly higher boiling point than Compound C ($163.5\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ vs $74.8\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$) because Compound D (a carboxylic acid) can form hydrogen bonds between its molecules. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Compound C (an aldehyde) lacks the $-\mathrm{OH}$ group and primarily experiences weaker dipole-dipole attractions (and London dispersion forces), which require less energy to overcome. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Accept mentions of "dimerization" for Compound D as a more specific form of hydrogen bonding.</note>

parts.content: A sample containing $0.850\,\mathrm{mol}$ of Compound B is subjected to complete combustion. Calculate the mass of water, in grams, produced in this reaction. (Molar mass of $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O} = 18.02\,\mathrm{g\,mol}^{-1}$)
parts.markscheme:
Step 1: Determine the molecular formula of 

Compound B and the stoichiometry. Compound B (butan-2-ol) is $\mathrm{C}_4\mathrm{H}_{10}\mathrm{O}$. One mole of B produces 5 moles of $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ upon combustion:


$$\mathrm{C}_4\mathrm{H}_{10}\mathrm{O} + 6\mathrm{O}_2 \rightarrow 4\mathrm{CO}_2 + 5\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$$
$$\text{Moles of } \mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O} = 0.850 \times 5 = 4.25\,\mathrm{mol}$$
<marks>1 mark</marks>

Step 2: Calculate the mass of water:

$$\text{Mass} = 4.25\,\mathrm{mol} \times 18.02\,\mathrm{g\,mol}^{-1} = 76.6\,\mathrm{g}$$
<marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Allow ECF if the student incorrectly identifies the number of hydrogen atoms in the molecular formula but performs the calculation correctly.</note>

parts.content:
The measured $Cl–C–Cl$ bond angle in phosgene is $111.8^\circ$.

Suggest why this angle is smaller than the ideal $120^\circ$ predicted for a trigonal planar arrangement, referring to electron domain repulsion.
parts.markscheme:
- **State** that the double bond ($C=O$) has a higher electron density than the single bonds ($C–Cl$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
The molar mass of phosgene is $98.91 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$.

parts.markscheme:
- **Calculate** the total mass of chlorine in 1 mole: $2 \times 35.45 = 70.90 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Calculate** the percentage: $\frac{70.90}{98.91} \times 100 = 71.68\%$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

options.markscheme: This is a secondary alcohol. Oxidation of a secondary alcohol using acidified potassium dichromate(VI) produces a ketone (hexan-2-one), which cannot be further oxidized to a carboxylic acid under these conditions.

This is a tertiary alcohol. Tertiary alcohols are resistant to oxidation by acidified dichromate(VI) because the carbon atom attached to the hydroxyl group does not have any hydrogen atoms available for removal.

This is a primary alcohol. Primary alcohols are oxidized first to aldehydes and then to carboxylic acids (in this case, 3-methylbutanoic acid) when heated under reflux with an excess of the oxidizing agent.
This is a cyclic secondary alcohol. Like other secondary alcohols, it is oxidized to a ketone (cyclopentanone) rather than a carboxylic acid.

parts.content: Draw the Lewis structure for the nitrite ion ($NO_2^-$), ensuring that all valence electrons and the overall charge are clearly indicated.
parts.markscheme:
- Central $N$ atom with one lone pair and bonded to two $O$ atoms (one single bond, one double bond). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- All valence electrons (18 total) correctly placed on oxygen atoms (3 lone pairs on single-bonded $O$, 2 lone pairs on double-bonded $O$) and square brackets with a negative sign.<marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the formal charge of each atom in one of the resonance structures of the $NO_2^-$ ion. Show your working.
parts.markscheme:
- Nitrogen: $5 - 2 - \frac{1}{2}(6) = 0$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Double-bonded Oxygen: $6 - 4 - \frac{1}{2}(4) = 0$ AND Single-bonded Oxygen: $6 - 6 - \frac{1}{2}(2) = -1$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using VSEPR theory, identify the molecular geometry of the species shown in model (i) and suggest a value for its O-N-O bond angle.
parts.markscheme:
- Molecular geometry: Bent / V-shaped <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Bond angle: $115^\circ$ (accept any value $110^\circ \le x < 120^\circ$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain why the nitrogen-oxygen bonds in the nitrite ion are of equal length, referring to the concept of resonance.
parts.markscheme:
- Resonance means the electrons are delocalized between the two $N-O$ positions. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The actual structure is an average/hybrid of the resonance forms, resulting in a bond order of 1.5 for both bonds. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Species (ii) represents the nitryl cation ($NO_2^+$). Compare the $N-O$ bond length in $NO_2^+$ with that in $NO_2^-$. Justify your answer using bond order.
parts.markscheme:
- $NO_2^+$ has a bond order of 2 (it is linear with two double bonds). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- $NO_2^-$ has a bond order of 1.5. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- $NO_2^+$ has shorter $N-O$ bonds because higher bond order correlates with shorter bond length. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Nitryl chloride ($NO_2Cl$) is a related molecule where the nitrogen atom is the central atom bonded to two oxygen atoms and one chlorine atom. Predict whether $NO_2Cl$ is a polar or non-polar molecule and justify your conclusion by considering its shape and bond polarities.
parts.markscheme:
- Polar <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The molecular geometry is trigonal planar (based on 3 electron domains and no lone pairs on $N$). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The molecule is asymmetrical / the $N-Cl$ and $N-O$ bond dipoles differ in magnitude and do not cancel out. <marks>1 mark</marks>

options.markscheme: Butan-1-ol is a primary alcohol. Under oxidation with acidified potassium dichromate(VI), it forms butanal (an aldehyde) and eventually butanoic acid (a carboxylic acid), rather than a ketone.

2-methylpropan-2-ol is a tertiary alcohol. Tertiary alcohols are resistant to oxidation by acidified potassium dichromate(VI) because there is no hydrogen atom attached to the carbon atom that carries the hydroxyl group.
Butanoic acid is a carboxylic acid. It is already in a high oxidation state and cannot be further oxidized by acidified potassium dichromate(VI).
Butan-2-ol is a secondary alcohol. The oxidation of secondary alcohols using acidified potassium dichromate(VI) yields ketones (in this case, butanone).

parts.content: Identify the functional group present in the compound based on the IR data.
parts.markscheme:
- Identifies the presence of a carbonyl group, $C=O$, from an absorption around $1740\ \text{cm}^{-1}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identifies this carbonyl as consistent with an ester (rather than, e.g., a ketone/aldehyde) based on the position in the $\sim 1735$–$1750\ \text{cm}^{-1}$ region <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_

- States the functional group is an ester from the strong sharp absorption at $1740\ \text{cm}^{-1}$ (ester $C=O$ stretch) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Deduce the full structure of the compound consistent with the molecular formula and NMR data.
parts.markscheme:
- Deduces the structure as methyl propanoate, $CH_3CH_2COOCH_3$ (name or displayed/condensed structure) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Uses the $3.7\ \text{ppm}$ singlet integrating to $3$ H to assign an $OCH_3$ (methoxy) group <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Uses the $2.3\ \text{ppm}$ quartet integrating to $2$ H to assign $CH_2$ adjacent to $C=O$ (in $CH_3CH_2C(=O)-$) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Uses the $1.2\ \text{ppm}$ triplet integrating to $3$ H to assign a terminal $CH_3$ next to $CH_2$ (ethyl fragment) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Confirms the proposed structure matches $C_4H_8O_2$ overall (atom count consistent) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Justify your proposed structure by explaining how the IR and NMR data support it.

- Explains that the singlet at $3.7\ \text{ppm}$ (3 H) corresponds to $OCH_3$ (methyl attached to oxygen) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains that the quartet at $2.3\ \text{ppm}$ (2 H) corresponds to $CH_2$ next to $C=O$ and split by an adjacent $CH_3$ (ethyl fragment) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains that the triplet at $1.2\ \text{ppm}$ (3 H) corresponds to a $CH_3$ split by an adjacent $CH_2$ (ethyl fragment) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Predict and explain the splitting pattern you would expect for the hydrogens on the $-CH_2-$ group in the $CH_3CH_2C(=O)-$ fragment (i.e., adjacent to the carbonyl group on the alkyl side) in the $^1$H NMR spectrum.
parts.markscheme:
- Predicts the $-CH_2-$ hydrogens adjacent to the carbonyl (in $CH_3CH_2C(=O)-$) appear as a quartet <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Applies the $n+1$ rule with $n=3$ neighbouring equivalent hydrogens on the adjacent $CH_3$ group, giving $3+1=4$ peaks <marks>1 mark</marks>
- States the coupling is to the adjacent methyl group only (no neighbouring H on the carbonyl carbon), consistent with a quartet rather than more complex splitting <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
State the functional group present in:

i) ethanol

ii) dimethyl ether
parts.markscheme:
- i) hydroxyl AND ii) ether <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Analyze the visual structures
steps.content: To identify the functional groups, we need to look at the specific arrangements of atoms in the displayed structures provided. Both molecules have the molecular formula $\text{C}_2\text{H}_6\text{O}$, but their atoms are connected differently, making them structural isomers.

steps.title: Identify ethanol's group
steps.content: By visually inspecting the structure of ethanol, we see an oxygen atom bonded to a hydrogen atom, forming an $-\text{OH}$ group at the end of the carbon chain. In organic chemistry, this specific group is known as the **hydroxyl** group.

steps.title: Identify dimethyl ether's group
steps.content: Now, looking at the structure of dimethyl ether, we see an oxygen atom positioned between two carbon atoms, creating a $\text{C}-\text{O}-\text{C}$ linkage. This structural arrangement is the defining feature of an **ether** group.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Based on our visual identification:

i) ethanol contains a **hydroxyl** group.

ii) dimethyl ether contains an **ether** group.

Following the markscheme, both must be identified correctly to earn the mark.

steps.title: Analyze the name 'ethanol'
steps.content:
To identify the functional group in **ethanol**, we look at its IUPAC name. In organic chemistry, the suffix (ending) of a name tells us the primary functional group present. 

*   The prefix **'eth-'** indicates a two-carbon chain.
*   The suffix **'-ol'** is used specifically for the alcohol homologous series.

According to **Topic S3.2.5 (IUPAC nomenclature)**, the characteristic functional group of alcohols is the **hydroxyl** group ($-\text{OH}$).

steps.title: Analyze 'dimethyl ether'
steps.content:
Next, let's look at the name **dimethyl ether**. Unlike ethanol, where the group is hidden in a suffix, the name here is very explicit.

*   The term **'ether'** in the name directly identifies the class of the compound.
*   The **'dimethyl'** part tells us there are two methyl groups ($-\text{CH}_3$) attached to the central oxygen.

In the IB syllabus (**Topic S3.2.2**), the functional group where an oxygen atom is bonded between two carbon atoms ($-\text{O}-$) is simply called an **ether** group.

steps.title: Final answer summary
steps.content:
By analyzing the nomenclature, we can conclude:

i) **Ethanol** contains a **hydroxyl** group.

ii) **Dimethyl ether** contains an **ether** group.

This matches the requirement for the 1-mark award in the markscheme.

steps.title: Identify the objective
steps.content: The goal is to identify the specific **functional groups** in the two molecules provided. To do this, we will look for specific patterns of atoms (like oxygen and hydrogen) and compare them to the standard list in your **IB Chemistry Data Booklet**.

steps.title: Analyze ethanol's structure
steps.content:
Looking at the displayed structure of ethanol, we see an $-\text{OH}$ group attached to a carbon atom. 

In **Section 19** of the Data Booklet, the group consisting of an oxygen atom bonded to a hydrogen atom ($-\text{OH}$) is identified as the **hydroxyl** group. 

*Note: While the "class" of this compound is an alcohol, the specific name for the group itself is the hydroxyl group.*

steps.title: Analyze dimethyl ether's structure
steps.content:
Now look at dimethyl ether. The structure shows an oxygen atom "sandwiched" between two carbon atoms ($-\text{C}-\text{O}-\text{C}-$). 

Referring back to **Section 19** of the Data Booklet, a molecule with an oxygen atom connected to two alkyl groups ($R-\text{O}-R'$) belongs to the **ether** group.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Based on the Data Booklet definitions:

i) ethanol contains a **hydroxyl** group.

ii) dimethyl ether contains an **ether** group.

parts.content: Explain why ethanol and dimethyl ether are classified as functional group isomers.
parts.markscheme:
- State that they have the same molecular formula, $\text{C}_2\text{H}_6\text{O}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain that they contain different functional groups (alcohol vs. ether) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define functional group isomerism
steps.content:
To answer this question using the definition-based approach, we first need to recall what **functional group isomers** are. These are molecules that satisfy two conditions:
1. They share the same **molecular formula**.
2. They belong to different **functional group** classes.

steps.title: Verify the molecular formula
steps.content:
First, we confirm that both molecules have the same number of atoms. By counting the atoms in the structures provided:
- **Ethanol**: 2 carbons, 6 hydrogens, and 1 oxygen ($C_2H_6O$)
- **Dimethyl ether**: 2 carbons, 6 hydrogens, and 1 oxygen ($C_2H_6O$)

Both molecules have the same molecular formula, which is the first requirement for isomerism.

steps.title: Identify different functional groups
steps.content:
Next, we look at how the atoms are arranged into functional groups. 

- In **ethanol**, we see a hydroxyl group ($-OH$) attached to a carbon chain, which classifies it as an **alcohol**.
- In **dimethyl ether**, we see an oxygen atom bonded between two carbon atoms ($C-O-C$), which classifies it as an **ether**.

Since they belong to different chemical families (alcohol vs. ether), they are functional group isomers.

steps.title: Final Summary
steps.content:
To secure both marks, your explanation should be concise:

Ethanol and dimethyl ether are functional group isomers because they have the **same molecular formula** ($C_2H_6O$) but contain **different functional groups** (an alcohol group in ethanol and an ether group in dimethyl ether).

steps.title: Compare molecular formulas
steps.content:
The first requirement for any type of isomerism is that the molecules must share the same molecular formula. By counting the atoms in the displayed structures:

*   **Ethanol**: 2 Carbon atoms, 6 Hydrogen atoms, and 1 Oxygen atom.
*   **Dimethyl ether**: 2 Carbon atoms, 6 Hydrogen atoms, and 1 Oxygen atom.

Both molecules have the same molecular formula:

$$C_2H_6O$$

steps.title: Analyze structural connectivity
steps.content:
Next, we look at how these atoms are connected. The "Structural Analysis Approach" highlights that even though the atom counts are identical, the arrangement of the oxygen atom is fundamentally different:

*   In **ethanol**, the oxygen is bonded to a hydrogen atom, forming a **hydroxyl group** ($-OH$). This makes ethanol part of the **alcohol** homologous series.
*   In **dimethyl ether**, the oxygen atom is bonded between two carbon atoms ($C-O-C$), forming an **alkoxy linkage**. This makes it an **ether**.

steps.title: Classify the isomerism
steps.content:
To earn full marks, we combine these two observations. These molecules are classified as **functional group isomers** because they:

1.  Possess the same molecular formula ($C_2H_6O$).
2.  Contain different functional groups (an alcohol vs. an ether).

This specific type of structural isomerism occurs when atoms are rearranged to create entirely different chemical families.

parts.content: Predict which compound has a higher boiling point and justify your answer in terms of intermolecular forces.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify that ethanol has hydrogen bonding between its molecules due to the $\text{-OH}$ group <marks>M1</marks>
- State that ethanol has a higher boiling point (or dimethyl ether has a lower boiling point due to weaker dipole-dipole/London forces) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze molecular structures
steps.content:
To predict the boiling point, we first look at the functional groups present in each molecule. This tells us which **intermolecular forces (IMFs)** are acting between the molecules. This concept is covered in **Topic S2.2.8: Intermolecular forces**.

*   **Ethanol** ($CH_3CH_2OH$) contains a hydroxyl group ($\text{-OH}$).
*   **Dimethyl ether** ($CH_3OCH_3$) contains an ether group ($\text{C-O-C}$).

Both molecules have the same molecular formula, so their **London dispersion forces** (based on molar mass) will be very similar. We must look at the specific dipole interactions to find the difference.

steps.title: Identify hydrogen bonding
steps.content:
In ethanol, the hydrogen atom is directly bonded to a highly electronegative oxygen atom. This creates a significant partial positive charge on the hydrogen and a partial negative charge on the oxygen. 

Because of this $\text{-OH}$ group, ethanol molecules can form **hydrogen bonds** with one another. Hydrogen bonding is the strongest type of intermolecular force for molecules of this size.

steps.title: Evaluate dimethyl ether forces
steps.content:
In dimethyl ether, the oxygen atom is bonded only to carbon atoms. There are no hydrogen atoms bonded to nitrogen, oxygen, or fluorine, so it **cannot** form hydrogen bonds.

Dimethyl ether is a polar molecule, so it exhibits **dipole-dipole forces** and **London dispersion forces**, but these are much weaker than the hydrogen bonds found in ethanol.

steps.title: Predict the boiling point
steps.content:
The boiling point is the temperature at which molecules have enough kinetic energy to overcome the attractive forces between them. Since ethanol has stronger intermolecular forces (hydrogen bonding), it requires more energy to separate the molecules.

**Prediction:** **Ethanol** has the higher boiling point.

In an IB exam, you earn marks by:
1. Identifying the specific $\text{-OH}$ group and the resulting **hydrogen bonding** in ethanol.
2. Concluding that ethanol has the **higher boiling point** because its IMFs are stronger than the dipole-dipole forces in dimethyl ether.

parts.content: Ethanol is soluble in water. Suggest why dimethyl ether is less soluble in water, even though both contain oxygen atoms.
parts.markscheme:
- State that ethanol can form hydrogen bonds with water molecules <marks>M1</marks>
- Explain that dimethyl ether is less soluble because it cannot form hydrogen bonds with water as effectively (it can accept but not donate H-bonds) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze ethanol's bonding
steps.content:
To understand solubility, we first look at how the molecule interacts with water. Ethanol contains a hydroxyl ($-\text{OH}$) group. Because the hydrogen is bonded to a highly electronegative oxygen, ethanol can form strong **hydrogen bonds** with water molecules.

This concept is covered in **Topic S2.2.8: Intermolecular forces**. Being able to form these bonds makes ethanol very compatible with water's own hydrogen-bonded structure, leading to high solubility.

steps.title: Examine dimethyl ether's structure
steps.content:
Now let's look at dimethyl ether ($(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{O}$). Although it has an oxygen atom, the oxygen is bonded only to carbon atoms. There are no hydrogen atoms directly bonded to the oxygen.

For a molecule to **donate** a hydrogen bond, it must have an $\text{H}$ atom bonded to $\text{F}$, $\text{O}$, or $\text{N}$. Since dimethyl ether lacks this, it cannot donate hydrogen bonds to water.

steps.title: Compare solubility effectiveness
steps.content:
The difference in solubility comes down to the "effectiveness" of the hydrogen bonding:

- **Ethanol**: Can both **accept** and **donate** hydrogen bonds with water.
- **Dimethyl ether**: Can **accept** hydrogen bonds (via the lone pairs on its oxygen) but **cannot donate** them.

Because dimethyl ether cannot form hydrogen bonds with water as effectively as ethanol, it is less soluble. This relationship between bonding and physical properties is a key part of **Topic S2.2.9**.

highlights.text: Suggest why dimethyl ether is less soluble in water

Identify the two functional groups present in the molecule shown below:

![2-aminoethanol](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-cc74c498-8d46-4a53-a8ae-f8684540763e-1771937587718/7f5b348e-9622-4d4e-8741-00eca8ab1e90.jpeg)

parts.content: Calculate the percentage by mass of nitrogen in 2-aminoethanol, $C_2H_7NO$.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the molar mass of $C_2H_7NO$:
$$ M_r = (2 \times 12.01) + (7 \times 1.01) + 14.01 + 16.00 = 61.10\text{ g mol}^{-1} $$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Calculate the percentage of nitrogen:
$$ \frac{14.01}{61.10} \times 100 = 22.93\% $$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
The boiling points of two compounds with similar molar masses are shown in the table below:

| Compound | Molar Mass / $\text{g mol}^{-1}$ | Boiling Point / $\text{°C}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 2-aminoethanol | 61.1 | 170 |
| Propan-1-amine | 59.1 | 48 |

Explain why the boiling point of 2-aminoethanol is significantly higher than that of propan-1-amine.
parts.markscheme:
- Both molecules exhibit hydrogen bonding between their molecules <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Oxygen is more electronegative than nitrogen, so the O–H hydrogen bonds in 2-aminoethanol are stronger than the N–H hydrogen bonds in propan-1-amine <marks>1 mark</marks>
- 2-aminoethanol has more sites available for hydrogen bonding (contains both an –OH and an –NH$_2$ group) <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Do not accept "stronger intermolecular forces" without specifying the reason (electronegativity or additional bonding sites).</note>

questions.specification:
An analytical chemist is investigating an unknown organic compound, **Y**, which has the molecular formula $C_3H_6O_2$. The relative intensities of the most significant peaks in its mass spectrum are recorded in the table below:

| $m/z$ | Relative Intensity (%) |
| :--- | :--- |
| 29 | 45.2 |
| 45 | 100.0 |
| 57 | 15.1 |
| 74 | 82.0 |
| 75 | 2.7 |

parts.content: Identify the molecular ion peak in the table and use the relative intensities of the $M$ and $M+1$ peaks to calculate the number of carbon atoms present in a molecule of **Y**.
parts.markscheme:
- The molecular ion ($M^+$) peak is at $m/z = 74$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>
- $n = \frac{100 \times I(M+1)}{1.1 \times I(M)} = \frac{100 \times 2.7}{1.1 \times 82} = \frac{270}{90.2} \approx 3$ carbon atoms <marks> 1 mark </marks>

parts.content: The peak at $m/z = 45$ is the base peak of the spectrum. Deduce the formula of the ion responsible for this peak and identify the specific functional group it characterizes.
parts.markscheme:
- Fragment ion = $COOH^+$ (accept $[C(O)OH]^+$) <marks> 1 mark </marks>
- This fragment is characteristic of a carboxylic acid <marks> 1 mark </marks>

parts.content: Deduce the formula of the fragment ion at $m/z = 57$ and identify the neutral species lost from the molecular ion to produce it.
parts.markscheme:
- Fragment ion = $CH_3CH_2CO^+$ (accept $[C_3H_5O]^+$) <marks> 1 mark </marks>
- Formed by the loss of a hydroxyl group ($\cdot OH$ or a fragment of mass 17) <marks> 1 mark </marks>

parts.content: Compound **Y** is propanoic acid ($CH_3CH_2COOH$). Explain how the mass spectrum of its structural isomer, methyl ethanoate ($CH_3COOCH_3$), would differ significantly regarding the peaks at $m/z = 15$ and $m/z = 59$.
parts.markscheme:
- Methyl ethanoate would show a significant peak at $m/z = 15$ corresponding to the methyl cation, $[CH_3]^+$, which is directly bonded to the carbonyl or oxygen <marks> 1 mark </marks>
- It would show a prominent peak at $m/z = 59$ corresponding to the loss of a methyl group ($[M - 15]^+$ or $[COOCH_3]^+$) <marks> 1 mark </marks>
- Propanoic acid's fragmentation is dominated by the loss of the ethyl group ($m/z = 45$) or the hydroxyl group ($m/z = 57$), and does not readily form these specific fragments <marks> 1 mark </marks>

questions.specification:
Identify the types of hybridization shown by the carbon atoms in the compound $$CH_3CN$$.

I. $$sp$$   
II. $$sp^2$$   
III. $$sp^3$$

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Structure 2. Models of bonding and structure > S2.2 The covalent model > S2.2.16 Hybridization and Bonding (Higher Level Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: S2.2.16 Hybridization and Bonding (Higher Level Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks to identify the hybridization states (sp, sp2, sp3) of the carbon atoms in CH3CN, which directly tests hybridization assignment in covalent bonding.

options.markscheme: Incorrect. Ethanenitrile ($$CH_3CN$$) does not contain any carbon atoms with $$sp^2$$ hybridization as there are no carbon-carbon or carbon-oxygen double bonds present.
Incorrect. Ethanenitrile ($$CH_3CN$$) does not contain any carbon atoms with $$sp^2$$ hybridization as there are no carbon-carbon or carbon-oxygen double bonds present.

- The methyl carbon ($$CH_3$$) is bonded to four atoms (three hydrogen atoms and one carbon atom) via four $$\sigma$$ bonds, indicating it is $$sp^3$$ hybridized.
- The nitrile carbon ($$CN$$) is bonded to two atoms (one carbon atom and one nitrogen atom) via one $$\sigma$$ bond and two $$\pi$$ bonds, indicating it is $$sp$$ hybridized.

Incorrect. The nitrile carbon in $$CH_3CN$$ is $$sp$$ hybridized, not $$sp^2$$. Saturated carbons are correctly identified as $$sp^3$$, but the presence of the triple bond makes $$sp$$ the second type present.

parts.content: Deduce which of the two isomers shown in the diagram (cis or trans) has a net molecular dipole moment of zero.
parts.markscheme:
- **Deduce** the isomer: Trans-1,2-dichloroethene <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>In the trans isomer, the bond dipoles of the $C-Cl$ bonds are oriented in exactly opposite directions, causing them to cancel each other out.</note>

parts.content: Compare the boiling points of the cis and trans isomers of 1,2-dichloroethene and justify the difference in terms of intermolecular forces.
parts.markscheme:
- **Compare** the boiling points: The cis-isomer has a higher boiling point than the trans-isomer <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Justify** using polarity: The cis-isomer is a polar molecule (has a net dipole), whereas the trans-isomer is non-polar <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Justify** using forces: The cis-isomer experiences dipole-dipole attractions (in addition to London dispersion forces), which require more energy to overcome than the London forces alone in the trans-isomer <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
Use the data in the table to determine the empirical formula of oxolane.

| Element | % Composition |
| :--- | :--- |
| C | 66.7% |
| H | 11.1% |
| O | 22.2% |
parts.markscheme:
Calculation of moles:
$n(C) = \frac{66.7}{12.01} = 5.55 \text{ mol}$
$n(H) = \frac{11.1}{1.01} = 10.99 \text{ mol}$
$n(O) = \frac{22.2}{16.00} = 1.39 \text{ mol}$ <marks>M1</marks>

Simplest ratio:
$C = \frac{5.55}{1.39} \approx 4$
$H = \frac{10.99}{1.39} \approx 8$
$O = \frac{1.39}{1.39} = 1$ <marks>M1</marks>

Empirical formula: $C_4H_8O$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Assume 100g sample
steps.content:
To determine the empirical formula, we start by assuming we have a $100\text{ g}$ sample of oxolane. This allows us to directly convert the given percentages into masses in grams.

From the table:
- Mass of Carbon ($C$) = $66.7\text{ g}$
- Mass of Hydrogen ($H$) = $11.1\text{ g}$
- Mass of Oxygen ($O$) = $22.2\text{ g}$

This is a standard first step for problems involving **Topic S1.4.4: Empirical and molecular formulas**.

steps.title: Calculate number of moles
steps.content:
Next, we convert these masses into moles using the formula for amount ($n$) from the Data Booklet:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

We use the relative atomic masses ($A_r$) from the periodic table: $C = 12.01$, $H = 1.01$, and $O = 16.00$.

desmos.instructions: Calculate values for n_C, n_H, and n_O.

calculator.description: Calculate moles for each element by dividing mass by molar mass.

steps.title: Find the molar values
steps.content:
Using the calculations from the previous step, we get:

$$\begin{aligned} n(C) &= \frac{66.7}{12.01} = 5.55\text{ mol} \\ n(H) &= \frac{11.1}{1.01} = 10.99\text{ mol} \\ n(O) &= \frac{22.2}{16.00} = 1.39\text{ mol} \end{aligned}$$

Recording these values correctly earns you the first mark (**M1**).

steps.title: Determine simplest molar ratio
steps.content:
To find the simplest whole-number ratio, divide each mole value by the smallest value calculated ($1.39\text{ mol}$ for Oxygen):

$$\begin{aligned} C &= \frac{5.55}{1.39} \approx 4 \\ H &= \frac{10.99}{1.39} \approx 7.9 \approx 8 \\ O &= \frac{1.39}{1.39} = 1 \end{aligned}$$

Showing this division step earns you the second mark (**M2**).

steps.title: State the empirical formula
steps.content:
The simplest ratio is $C:H:O = 4:8:1$. 

Therefore, the empirical formula of oxolane is:

$$C_4H_8O$$

Providing the correct final formula earns you the final mark (**A1**).

steps.title: Analyze the skeletal structure
steps.content:
To determine the empirical formula using structural analysis, we first look at the skeletal structure of oxolane. In a skeletal structure:
- Each vertex (corner) and the end of each line represents a **carbon atom**.
- Hydrogen atoms are not shown, but they are assumed to be present to satisfy carbon's valency of 4 bonds.

Looking at the 5-membered ring, we see **4 carbon atoms** and **1 oxygen atom**.

steps.title: Count the hydrogen atoms
steps.content:
Next, we determine how many hydrogen atoms are needed. Each of the 4 carbon atoms in the ring is currently shown with 2 bonds to adjacent ring atoms (either C-C or C-O).

Since carbon must have 4 bonds, each carbon atom must be bonded to **2 hydrogen atoms**. 

$$\text{Total Hydrogens} = 4 \text{ carbons} \times 2 \text{ hydrogens/carbon} = 8 \text{ hydrogens}$$

steps.title: Identify the molecular formula
steps.content:
Combining our counts, we find the molecular formula for oxolane is:
$$C_4H_8O$$

steps.title: Verify with composition data
steps.content:
We can verify this formula by calculating its theoretical percentage composition and comparing it to the table provided in the question. 

First, calculate the molar mass of $C_4H_8O$:
- $M(C) = 12.01 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$
- $M(H) = 1.01 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$
- $M(O) = 16.00 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$

$$\text{Molar Mass} = (4 \times 12.01) + (8 \times 1.01) + 16.00 = 72.12 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the mass of carbon (48.04) divided by total mass (72.12) and multiply by 100.

ti84.instructions: Calculate the molar mass and then the % by mass for Carbon: (4 * 12.01) / 72.12 * 100

desmos.instructions: Calculate percentage composition for C, H, and O to verify against table data.

calculator.description: Calculate theoretical percentage composition for verification.

steps.title: Compare results
steps.content:
The theoretical percentages are:
- $\%C = \frac{48.04}{72.12} \times 100 \approx 66.6\%$
- $\%H = \frac{8.08}{72.12} \times 100 \approx 11.2\%$
- $\%O = \frac{16.00}{72.12} \times 100 \approx 22.2\%$

These values closely match the experimental data in the table, confirming our structural analysis.

steps.title: Determine the empirical formula
steps.content:
The empirical formula is the simplest whole-number ratio of atoms. For $C_4H_8O$, the ratio is **4:8:1**.

Since this ratio cannot be simplified any further (because of the single oxygen atom), the empirical formula is the same as the molecular formula.

**Empirical Formula:** $C_4H_8O$

parts.content: Determine the molecular formula of oxolane given that its $M_r$ is $72$.
parts.markscheme:
$M_r(C_4H_8O) = 4(12.01) + 8(1.01) + 16.00 = 72.12$
$\frac{72}{72.12} \approx 1$

Molecular formula $C_4H_8O$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Analyze the skeletal structure
steps.content:
In a skeletal formula, vertices (corners) and ends of lines represent carbon atoms, unless labeled otherwise. Looking at the five-membered ring of oxolane:

*   One vertex is explicitly labeled with an **Oxygen (O)** atom.
*   The other four vertices are unlabeled, meaning they are **Carbon (C)** atoms.

So far, we have a base formula of $C_4O$.

steps.title: Determine the hydrogen count
steps.content:
Carbon must always form four bonds to be stable (valency of 4). We look at each carbon in the ring to see how many hydrogen atoms are "hidden":

1.  Each carbon atom is currently shown with **two single bonds** (connecting to the neighboring atoms in the ring).
2.  To reach a total of four bonds, each carbon must be bonded to **two hydrogen atoms**.

Since there are 4 carbon atoms, each with 2 hydrogens, we have:
$$4 \times 2 = 8 \text{ hydrogen atoms}$$

steps.title: Verify with molar mass
steps.content:
Based on our analysis, the formula is $C_4H_8O$. Let's verify this using the relative atomic masses from the periodic table ($A_r: C=12.01, H=1.01, O=16.00$):

$$\begin{aligned} M_r(C_4H_8O) &= 4(12.01) + 8(1.01) + 16.00 \\ &= 48.04 + 8.08 + 16.00 \\ &= 72.12 \end{aligned}$$

The question provides an $M_r$ of 72. Since $72.12 \approx 72$, the molecular formula $C_4H_8O$ is correct.

ti84.instructions: Enter 4*12.01 + 8*1.01 + 16.00 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the total relative molecular mass of C4H8O.

steps.title: State the final formula
steps.content:
The molecular formula of oxolane is **$C_4H_8O$**. 

This identifies all atoms present in one molecule of the compound as determined by the skeletal structure and confirmed by the mass.

steps.title: Identify the General Formula
steps.content:
To solve this using the General Formula Approach, we first analyze the structure of oxolane. It is a **saturated cyclic compound** containing one oxygen atom. 

Normally, a saturated chain (alkane) has the formula $C_nH_{2n+2}$. Forming a ring removes two hydrogen atoms, and adding one oxygen for an ether linkage results in the general formula:

$$C_nH_{2n}O$$

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.content:
We are given that the relative molecular mass ($M_r$) is 72. We can use the relative atomic masses from the periodic table ($C = 12.01, H = 1.01, O = 16.00$) to set up an equation based on our general formula:

$$n(12.01) + 2n(1.01) + 16.00 = 72$$

steps.title: Solve for n
steps.content:
Now, we simplify the equation to find the value of $n$:

$$\begin{aligned} 12.01n + 2.02n + 16.00 &= 72 \\ 14.03n &= 72 - 16 \\ 14.03n &= 56 \\ n &= \frac{56}{14.03} \end{aligned}$$

Using a calculator, we find $n \approx 4$.

gdc.instructions: In the run-matrix menu, input (72 - 16) / 14.03 and execute.

ti84.instructions: Enter (72 - 16) / (12.01 + 2 * 1.01) and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the value of n by dividing the mass of the hydrocarbon part by the mass of one CH2 unit.

steps.title: Determine molecular formula
steps.content:
Since $n = 4$, we substitute this back into our general formula $C_nH_{2n}O$:

$$C_4H_{(2 \times 4)}O \rightarrow C_4H_8O$$ 

This matches the $M_r$ of approximately 72 ($4 \times 12.01 + 8 \times 1.01 + 16.00 = 72.12$).

parts.content: The boiling point of oxolane is $66\,^\circ\text{C}$. Suggest the strongest intermolecular forces between molecules of oxolane.
parts.markscheme: Dipole-dipole forces <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Analyze bond polarity
steps.content:
To determine the intermolecular forces, we first look at the bonds within the oxolane molecule. Oxygen is significantly more electronegative than carbon. This difference in electronegativity creates polar covalent bonds between the carbon and oxygen atoms ($C-O$). 

In these bonds, electron density is pulled towards the oxygen, resulting in partial charges:
- Oxygen atom: $\delta^-$
- Adjacent carbon atoms: $\delta^+$

steps.title: Evaluate molecular symmetry
steps.content:
Next, we consider the shape of the molecule. Oxolane is a five-membered ring containing one oxygen atom and four carbon atoms. Because the oxygen atom is different from the carbon atoms, the molecule is **unsymmetrical**.

In chemistry, if a molecule is unsymmetrical and contains polar bonds, the individual bond dipoles do not cancel each other out. This results in a permanent **net molecular dipole**.

steps.title: Check for hydrogen bonding
steps.content:
We should also check if hydrogen bonding is possible. For hydrogen bonding to occur, a hydrogen atom must be directly bonded to a highly electronegative atom ($N$, $O$, or $F$). 

In oxolane, all hydrogen atoms are bonded to carbon atoms ($C-H$). Since there are no $O-H$ bonds, hydrogen bonding cannot occur between oxolane molecules.

steps.title: Identify the strongest force
steps.content:
Since the molecule has a permanent net dipole but cannot form hydrogen bonds, the strongest interactions between the molecules are the attractions between the positive end of one molecule and the negative end of another. 

These are known as **dipole-dipole forces** (or dipole-dipole interactions).

steps.title: Identify the functional group
steps.content:
First, let's look at the provided structure of oxolane. We see a five-membered ring consisting of four carbon atoms and one oxygen atom. This arrangement identifies the molecule as a **cyclic ether**.

In IB Chemistry, identifying the functional group is the first step in predicting physical properties like boiling point. You can review functional groups in **Topic S3.2: Functional groups**.

steps.title: Rule out hydrogen bonding
steps.content:
To determine the strongest intermolecular force, we first check for the strongest possible type: hydrogen bonding. For hydrogen bonding to occur *between* molecules, a hydrogen atom must be covalently bonded to a highly electronegative atom ($N$, $O$, or $F$).

In oxolane:
- The oxygen atom is bonded only to two carbon atoms ($C-O-C$).
- All hydrogen atoms are bonded to carbon atoms ($C-H$).

Since there is no $H$ bonded directly to $O$, oxolane molecules **cannot** form hydrogen bonds with each other.

steps.title: Evaluate molecular polarity
steps.content:
Next, we look at bond polarity. Oxygen is more electronegative than carbon, making the $C-O$ bonds polar. Because the molecule is a cyclic ether, the distribution of these polar bonds is asymmetrical, resulting in a net molecular dipole.

This makes oxolane significantly more polar than a pure hydrocarbon (which only has very weak London dispersion forces). This polarity is consistent with its boiling point of $66\,^\circ\text{C}$, which is higher than similar-sized alkanes but lower than alcohols of the same mass.

steps.title: Identify strongest force
steps.content:
Since the molecule is polar but does not exhibit hydrogen bonding, the strongest intermolecular forces acting between oxolane molecules are **dipole-dipole forces**.

London (dispersion) forces are also present (as they are in all molecules), but dipole-dipole forces are the stronger type of attraction here.

parts.content: Outline the details of one experimental technique that could be used to separate a mixture of oxolane and water.
parts.markscheme:
(Fractional) distillation <marks>A1</marks>

Heat the mixture until the oxolane boils (at approximately $66\,^\circ\text{C}$) and then condense the vapor/distillate into a separate container. <marks>M1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the mixture type
steps.content:
To separate a mixture of oxolane and water, we first recognize that they are miscible liquids with different boiling points. Oxolane boils at approximately $66\,^\circ\text{C}$, while water boils at $100\,^\circ\text{C}$. 

This difference in physical properties is the key to choosing the correct separation method, which is covered in **Topic S1.1.1: Elements, Compounds and Mixtures**.

highlights.text: separate a mixture of oxolane and water

steps.title: Choose fractional distillation
steps.content:
The most effective technique for separating miscible liquids with different boiling points is **fractional distillation**. 

While simple distillation can work, a fractionating column provides a much more efficient separation by allowing for multiple cycles of evaporation and condensation within the column.

steps.title: Apply heat and boil
steps.content:
In the lab, you would place the mixture in a round-bottom flask and heat it. As the temperature reaches $66\,^\circ\text{C}$, the oxolane begins to boil and its vapor rises through the fractionating column.

$$\text{Boiling Point (Oxolane)} \approx 66\,^\circ\text{C}$$
$$\text{Boiling Point (Water)} = 100\,^\circ\text{C}$$

highlights.text: Heat the mixture until the oxolane boils (at approximately 66 °C)

steps.title: Condense and collect
steps.content: The oxolane vapor then enters a condenser (usually a Liebig condenser), where it is cooled by a jacket of cold water and turns back into a liquid. This liquid, called the **distillate**, is then collected in a separate container, leaving the water behind in the original flask.

highlights.text: condense the vapor/distillate into a separate container

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
To earn the 2 marks for this question:
1.  **A1**: Correctly identify the technique as **fractional distillation**.
2.  **M1**: Describe heating the mixture to the lower boiling point (oxolane) and **condensing** the vapor into a separate container.

steps.title: Identify the technique
steps.content:
To separate a mixture of two liquids like oxolane and water, we use **distillation**. This technique relies on the difference in boiling points between the components of the mixture. 

Since oxolane is more volatile (meaning it has a lower boiling point) than water, it will evaporate first when the mixture is heated.

steps.title: Heat the mixture
steps.content: Place the mixture into a distillation flask and heat it. You should monitor the temperature using a thermometer. When the temperature reaches approximately $66\,^\circ\text{C}$, the oxolane will begin to boil and turn into vapor, while the water remains in the flask.

highlights.text: Heat the mixture until the oxolane boils (at approximately $66\,^\circ\text{C}$)

steps.title: Condense and collect
steps.content: The oxolane vapor passes into a **Liebig condenser**, which is cooled by a constant flow of cold water. This cooling causes the vapor to condense back into a liquid. Finally, you collect this liquid (the distillate) in a separate container.

highlights.text: condense the vapor/distillate into a separate container.

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
In an IB exam, you earn marks by:
1.  Naming the technique as **distillation** (or fractional distillation).
2.  Describing the process of **heating** the mixture until the oxolane boils and then **condensing** the resulting vapor into a separate container.

steps.title: Identify Gas Chromatography (GC)
steps.content:
Gas Chromatography (GC) is a powerful analytical technique used to separate and analyze the components of a volatile mixture, such as oxolane and water. It is particularly useful when the components are present in small quantities or when high precision is required for identification.

This technique relates to **Topic S2.2.10: Chromatography** in your syllabus, which explores how differences in physical properties allow for the separation of mixtures.

highlights.text: separate a mixture of oxolane and water

steps.title: Establish the Mobile Phase
steps.content:
In Gas Chromatography, the mixture is first vaporized and then carried through the system by a **mobile phase**. This phase consists of an **inert carrier gas**, such as Helium ($He$) or Nitrogen ($N_2$). 

The role of the carrier gas is to transport the vaporized oxolane and water molecules through the separation column without reacting with them.

steps.title: Separation in the Column
steps.content:
The separation occurs inside a long, narrow column containing the **stationary phase** (usually a high-boiling liquid or polymer coated on an inert solid support). 

Components separate based on their different **partition coefficients** or affinities for the stationary phase:
- **Oxolane**, being less polar than water, will interact differently with the stationary phase.
- **Water** will have its own specific level of attraction to the column lining.

Components with a higher affinity for the stationary phase move more slowly, while those with a higher affinity for the mobile gas phase move faster.

steps.title: Detecting Retention Times
steps.content:
As the carrier gas pushes the substances through the column, they emerge at different times, known as **retention times**. 

By measuring these times, we can successfully separate the oxolane from the water. In a laboratory setting, a detector at the end of the column identifies when each substance exits, allowing for the quantitative analysis of the contamination.

steps.title: Analyzing the Markscheme
steps.content:
While **Gas Chromatography** is a valid scientific method for separating these substances for analysis, it is important to note the expected answer in the context of this specific IB exam paper. 

The official markscheme focuses on **fractional distillation** as the primary method for separating two liquids with different boiling points in a bulk mixture. 

- **Distillation:** Separates based on boiling point ($66\,^\circ\text{C}$ for oxolane vs. $100\,^\circ\text{C}$ for water).
- **GC:** Separates based on partition coefficients between gas and liquid phases.

If you are asked to "outline details," always ensure you describe the specific phases (mobile and stationary) and the basis of separation (affinity/partitioning).

highlights.text: Heat the mixture until the oxolane boils (at approximately $66\,^\circ\text{C}$) and then condense the vapor/distillate into a separate container.

parts.content:
A sample of contaminated tap water was found to have a concentration of oxolane of $1.40 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$. 

Determine the mass of oxolane in a $250 \text{ cm}^3$ sample of contaminated tap water.
parts.markscheme:
$n = \frac{250}{1000} \text{ dm}^3 \times 1.40 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mol dm}^{-3} = 3.50 \times 10^{-8} \text{ mol}$ <marks>M1</marks>

$m = 3.50 \times 10^{-8} \text{ mol} \times 72.0 \text{ g mol}^{-1} = 2.52 \times 10^{-6} \text{ g}$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Calculate the chemical amount
steps.content:
First, we need to find the number of moles ($n$) of oxolane in the sample. Since we are given the volume in $\text{cm}^3$, we must convert it to $\text{dm}^3$ to match the concentration units.

Using the formula from the data booklet:
$$n = CV$$

$$\begin{aligned} n &= (1.40 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}) \times \left(\frac{250}{1000} \text{ dm}^3\right) \\ n &= 1.40 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.250 \\ n &= 3.50 \times 10^{-8} \text{ mol} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 1.40E-7 * (250 / 1000) and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of moles by multiplying concentration by volume in dm³.

highlights.text: concentration of oxolane of $1.40 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$

steps.title: Determine the molar mass
steps.content:
Next, we determine the molar mass ($M$) of oxolane from its structural formula. Oxolane is a five-membered ring containing four carbon atoms, eight hydrogen atoms, and one oxygen atom ($C_4H_8O$).

Using relative atomic masses from the periodic table:
$$\begin{aligned} M(C_4H_8O) &= (4 \times 12.01) + (8 \times 1.01) + 16.00 \\ &= 48.04 + 8.08 + 16.00 \\ &= 72.12 \text{ g mol}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures as used in the markscheme, we get $72.0 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$.

steps.title: Convert moles to mass
steps.content:
Finally, we convert the chemical amount in moles to mass ($m$) using the rearranged molar mass formula from the data booklet:

$$m = n \times M$$

$$\begin{aligned} m &= (3.50 \times 10^{-8} \text{ mol}) \times (72.0 \text{ g mol}^{-1}) \\ m &= 2.52 \times 10^{-6} \text{ g} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 3.50E-8 * 72.0 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the mass by multiplying moles by molar mass.

steps.title: Determine oxolane's molar mass
steps.content:
To start, we need to find the molar mass ($M$) of oxolane from its structure. Looking at the diagram, oxolane is a cyclic compound with 4 carbon atoms, 8 hydrogen atoms, and 1 oxygen atom, giving it the molecular formula $\text{C}_4\text{H}_8\text{O}$.

Using relative atomic masses from the periodic table:
- Carbon ($C$): $12.01 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$
- Hydrogen ($H$): $1.01 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$
- Oxygen ($O$): $16.00 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$

$$M = (4 \times 12.01) + (8 \times 1.01) + 16.00 = 72.12 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$$

Rounding to one decimal place as seen in the markscheme, we use **$72.0 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$**. This concept is covered in **Structure 1.4: Counting particles by mass: The mole**.

ti84.instructions: Enter 4*12.01 + 8*1.01 + 16.00 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the molar mass of oxolane (C4H8O).

steps.title: Calculate mass concentration
steps.content:
Now, we convert the molar concentration ($C$) into mass concentration (in $\text{g dm}^{-3}$). This is done by multiplying the molarity by the molar mass we just found.

$$\text{Mass Concentration} = C \times M$$

Substituting the given concentration:
$$\text{Mass Concentration} = (1.40 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}) \times (72.0 \text{ g mol}^{-1})$$
$$\text{Mass Concentration} = 1.008 \times 10^{-5} \text{ g dm}^{-3}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter (1.40*10^-7) * 72.0 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Multiply the molarity by the molar mass.

steps.title: Calculate the final mass
steps.content:
Finally, we multiply the mass concentration by the volume of the sample ($V$). 

First, convert the volume from $\text{cm}^3$ to $\text{dm}^3$ to match our units:
$$V = 250 \text{ cm}^3 = 0.250 \text{ dm}^3$$

Now, calculate the total mass ($m$):
$$m = \text{Mass Concentration} \times V$$
$$m = (1.008 \times 10^{-5} \text{ g dm}^{-3}) \times (0.250 \text{ dm}^3)$$
$$m = 2.52 \times 10^{-6} \text{ g}$$

This calculation combines the formulas for concentration and moles found in the **Data Booklet**.

ti84.instructions: Enter 1.008e-5 * 0.250 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the final mass by multiplying mass concentration by volume.

steps.title: Identify the molecular formula
steps.content:
To find the mass, we first need the molar mass of oxolane. From the skeletal structure provided, we can see a five-membered ring containing one oxygen atom and four carbon atoms. 

Each carbon atom in the skeletal structure is bonded to two other atoms in the ring, meaning each carbon must also be bonded to two hydrogen atoms to satisfy the octet rule. 

The molecular formula is therefore:
$$C_4H_8O$$

steps.title: Calculate the molar mass
steps.content:
Using the atomic masses from the periodic table ($C = 12.01$, $H = 1.01$, $O = 16.00$):

$$\begin{aligned} M(C_4H_8O) &= (4 \times 12.01) + (8 \times 1.01) + 16.00 \\ &= 72.12 \text{ g mol}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

(The markscheme uses $72.0 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$ for simplicity, but $72.12$ is more precise for IB calculations).

highlights.sectionName: Structure 1.4 Counting particles by mass: The mole

steps.title: Set up dimensional analysis
steps.content:
We can determine the mass in one continuous calculation by converting the volume to $\text{dm}^3$, then to moles using the concentration, and finally to grams using the molar mass.

$$\text{Mass (g)} = 250 \text{ cm}^3 \times \left( \frac{1 \text{ dm}^3}{1000 \text{ cm}^3} \right) \times \left( \frac{1.40 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mol}}{1 \text{ dm}^3} \right) \times \left( \frac{72.12 \text{ g}}{1 \text{ mol}} \right)$$ 

This method ensures all units cancel out correctly except for grams.

highlights.text: concentration of oxolane of $1.40 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$

steps.title: Calculate the final mass
steps.content:
Now, perform the calculation:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Mass} &= 0.250 \text{ dm}^3 \times (1.40 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}) \times 72.12 \text{ g mol}^{-1} \\ &= 2.5242 \times 10^{-6} \text{ g} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures (based on the concentration provided), we get:
$$\text{Mass} = 2.52 \times 10^{-6} \text{ g}$$

Water is safe to drink. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the safety threshold
steps.content:
First, we identify the safety limit provided in the question. Oxolane becomes harmful only if its concentration exceeds a specific value.

Safety threshold = $1.00 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$

highlights.text: harmful in concentrations exceeding 1.00 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}

steps.title: Compare concentration to threshold
steps.content:
From the previous steps, we calculated the concentration of oxolane in the water sample to be $1.40 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$. 

Now, we compare the sample concentration to the safety threshold. Since $10^{-7}$ is a much smaller number than $10^{-4}$, the sample concentration is significantly lower than the limit:

$$1.40 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mol dm}^{-3} < 1.00 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$$

steps.content:
Because the concentration of oxolane in the sample is less than the safety threshold, the water is safe to drink.

**Final Conclusion:** The water is safe.

questions.specification:
Ozone $\left(\mathrm{O}_3\right)$ in the stratosphere is formed and destroyed in a dynamic equilibrium primarily involving UV radiation. Anthropogenic emissions of chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) disrupt this equilibrium by catalyzing the breakdown of ozone. The Montreal Protocol was implemented to reduce CFC emissions and protect the ozone layer.

Table 1: Concentration of stratospheric ozone and chlorine compounds (ppb) in the atmosphere.

| Year | O₃ concentration | Cl₂O₂ (chlorine peroxide) | CFCl₃ (CFC-11) | CF₂Cl₂ (CFC-12) |
|------|------------------|---------------------------|----------------|-----------------|
| 2000 | 300              | 2.0                       | 260            | 540             |
| 2005 | 290              | 2.2                       | 220            | 480             |
| 2010 | 280              | 2.1                       | 180            | 420             |
| 2015 | 295              | 1.8                       | 140            | 360             |
| 2020 | 310              | 1.5                       | 100            | 300             |
