parts.content: Define the role of UV radiation in the formation and breakdown of ozone in the stratosphere.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1</marks> for describing that UV radiation initiates both the formation and destruction of ozone:

- High energy UV breaks oxygen molecules ($\mathrm{O}_2$) to form oxygen radicals/ozone AND lower energy UV breaks ozone (to reform $\mathrm{O}_2$ / maintain equilibrium).

steps.title: Identify UV's dual role
steps.content: In the stratosphere, UV radiation acts as the energy source that drives the **Chapman Cycle**, a natural dynamic equilibrium. Its primary role is to initiate the photochemical dissociation (breaking bonds using light) of both oxygen and ozone molecules.

steps.title: Formation: High-energy UV
steps.content:
To **form** ozone, high-energy (short-wavelength) UV radiation is absorbed by oxygen molecules ($O_2$). This energy is strong enough to break the $O=O$ double bond, creating highly reactive oxygen radicals:

$$O_2(g) \xrightarrow{\text{UV high energy}} 2O\bullet(g)$$

These radicals then react with other $O_2$ molecules to produce ozone ($O_3$). This process is a key part of **Topic R1.2 (Bond Enthalpy)**, as it requires specific energy to overcome the bond strength of oxygen.

steps.title: Breakdown: Lower-energy UV
steps.content:
To **break down** ozone, lower-energy (longer-wavelength) UV radiation is absorbed by ozone molecules ($O_3$). Because the bonds in ozone are weaker than the double bond in $O_2$, less energy is required to dissociate it back into an oxygen molecule and a radical:

$$O_3(g) \xrightarrow{\text{UV lower energy}} O_2(g) + O\bullet(g)$$

This continuous cycle of formation and destruction absorbs harmful UV radiation before it reaches the Earth's surface.

steps.title: Final Definition
steps.content:
To earn the mark, your definition must highlight that UV radiation initiates **both** processes using different energy levels:

1.  **High-energy UV** dissociates $O_2$ to initiate ozone formation.
2.  **Lower-energy UV** dissociates $O_3$ to facilitate its breakdown and maintain equilibrium.

steps.title: Introduction to the Chapman Cycle
steps.content: In the stratosphere, ozone levels are maintained through a natural set of reactions known as the **Chapman Cycle**. UV radiation is the driving force for this cycle, providing the necessary energy to break chemical bonds (a process called photolysis).

steps.title: Ozone Formation Equations
steps.content:
Ozone formation begins when high-energy (short wavelength) UV radiation breaks the strong double bond in an oxygen molecule ($O_2$) to create two oxygen radicals. These radicals then react with other oxygen molecules to form ozone:

$$\begin{aligned} &O_2(g) \xrightarrow{UV \text{ (high energy)}} 2O\cdot(g) \\ &O\cdot(g) + O_2(g) \rightarrow O_3(g) \end{aligned}$$

This process is essential for shielding the Earth, as it absorbs the most dangerous high-energy UV rays.

highlights.text: High energy UV breaks oxygen molecules (O2) to form oxygen radicals/ozone

steps.title: Ozone Destruction Equations
steps.content:
UV radiation also facilitates the natural breakdown of ozone. Because the bonds in ozone ($O_3$) are weaker than those in $O_2$, lower-energy (longer wavelength) UV radiation is sufficient to break them, regenerating oxygen molecules and radicals:

$$O_3(g) \xrightarrow{UV \text{ (lower energy)}} O_2(g) + O\cdot(g)$$

This step is crucial because it allows the atmosphere to absorb a wider spectrum of UV radiation while maintaining a steady-state concentration of ozone.

highlights.text: lower energy UV breaks ozone (to reform O2 / maintain equilibrium)

steps.title: Summary of the Role
steps.content:
To answer the question: UV radiation acts as the **energy source** that initiates both the formation and destruction of ozone. 

1. It causes the **photolysis of $O_2$** using high-energy radiation to start ozone production. 
2. It causes the **photolysis of $O_3$** using lower-energy radiation to drive its natural breakdown, creating a dynamic equilibrium.

parts.content: Analyze the data in the table to describe the trends in stratospheric ozone and chlorine compounds from 2000 to 2020.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1</marks> for each of the following, up to <marks>2</marks>:

- Ozone concentration decreases (from 300 ppb to 280 ppb) between 2000 and 2010, then increases (to 310 ppb by 2020).

- CFC concentrations ($\mathrm{CFCl}_3$ and $\mathrm{CF}_2\mathrm{Cl}_2$) decrease steadily, while $\mathrm{Cl}_2\mathrm{O}_2$ concentration peaks in 2005 before decreasing.

parts.content: State the products of the breakdown of $\mathrm{Cl}_2\mathrm{O}_2$ and write the chemical equations for the catalytic depletion of ozone involving oxygen radicals ($\mathrm{O}^\bullet$), including the overall equation.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1</marks> for identifying that $\mathrm{Cl}_2\mathrm{O}_2$ breaks down into chlorine radicals and stating the propagation steps:

- $\mathrm{Cl}_2\mathrm{O}_2$ breaks down (via UV photolysis) into chlorine radicals ($\mathrm{Cl}^\bullet$) and the propagation steps are:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{Cl}^\bullet+\mathrm{O}_3 & \rightarrow \mathrm{ClO}^\bullet+\mathrm{O}_2 \\
\mathrm{ClO}^\bullet+\mathrm{O}^\bullet & \rightarrow \mathrm{Cl}^\bullet+\mathrm{O}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Award <marks>1</marks> for the overall reaction:

- The overall reaction:
$$
\mathrm{O}_3+\mathrm{O}^\bullet \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{O}_2
$$

steps.title: Breakdown of chlorine peroxide
steps.content:
In the stratosphere, chlorine peroxide ($\text{Cl}_2\text{O}_2$) undergoes photolysis, usually due to UV radiation. This process breaks the molecule down into chlorine radicals.

Product: **chlorine radicals** ($\text{Cl}^\bullet$)

This is a crucial concept in **Topic R3.3 Radicals**, which explores how species with unpaired electrons initiate chain reactions.

steps.title: First propagation step
steps.content:
The chlorine radical is highly reactive and attacks an ozone molecule ($\text{O}_3$). This forms a chlorine monoxide radical and molecular oxygen:

$$\text{Cl}^\bullet + \text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{ClO}^\bullet + \text{O}_2$$

steps.title: Second propagation step
steps.content:
To act as a catalyst, the chlorine radical must be regenerated. The chlorine monoxide radical ($\text{ClO}^\bullet$) reacts with an oxygen radical ($\text{O}^\bullet$) present in the atmosphere:

$$\text{ClO}^\bullet + \text{O}^\bullet \rightarrow \text{Cl}^\bullet + \text{O}_2$$

Notice that the $\text{Cl}^\bullet$ used in the first step is produced again here, allowing it to destroy thousands more ozone molecules.

steps.title: The overall reaction
steps.content:
To find the overall reaction, we add the two propagation steps together and cancel out the species that appear on both sides (the intermediates $\text{Cl}^\bullet$ and $\text{ClO}^\bullet$):

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Cl}^\bullet + \text{O}_3 &\rightarrow \text{ClO}^\bullet + \text{O}_2 \\ \text{ClO}^\bullet + \text{O}^\bullet &\rightarrow \text{Cl}^\bullet + \text{O}_2 \\ \hline \text{O}_3 + \text{O}^\bullet &\rightarrow 2\text{O}_2 \end{aligned}$$

According to the markscheme, stating the breakdown products and both propagation steps earns **1 mark**, while the correct overall equation earns the **2nd mark**.

parts.content: Explain the relationship between the trends in CFC concentrations and ozone levels, based on the data provided.
parts.markscheme:
- The decrease in CFC concentrations ($\mathrm{CFCl}_3$ and $\mathrm{CF}_2\mathrm{Cl}_2$) from 2000 to 2020 correlates with a reduction in $\mathrm{Cl}_2\mathrm{O}_2$ levels, as CFCs are the source of chlorine radicals. <marks>1</marks>

- As chlorine radicals decrease, the rate of ozone depletion slows, allowing ozone concentrations to recover. <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Analyze CFC concentration trends
steps.content:
First, we look at the data for the two chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) provided in the table: $\mathrm{CFCl}_3$ and $\mathrm{CF}_2\mathrm{Cl}_2$. 

From the year 2000 to 2020, we can see a continuous and significant decrease in the concentrations of both compounds:
- $\mathrm{CFCl}_3$ dropped from $260\text{ ppb}$ to $100\text{ ppb}$.
- $\mathrm{CF}_2\mathrm{Cl}_2$ dropped from $540\text{ ppb}$ to $300\text{ ppb}$.

This trend is the result of international agreements like the Montreal Protocol, which aimed to phase out these substances.

steps.title: Correlate CFCs with chlorine compounds
steps.content:
Next, we observe how these CFC trends affect other chlorine-containing species, such as chlorine peroxide ($\mathrm{Cl}_2\mathrm{O}_2$). 

CFCs are the primary source of chlorine radicals in the stratosphere. As the concentration of CFCs decreases, the amount of available chlorine to form $\mathrm{Cl}_2\mathrm{O}_2$ also eventually decreases. In the table, we see that after a slight peak in 2005, the concentration of $\mathrm{Cl}_2\mathrm{O}_2$ falls steadily from $2.2\text{ ppb}$ to $1.5\text{ ppb}$ by 2020.

This shows a direct correlation: lower CFC levels lead to lower levels of reactive chlorine species.

steps.title: Link chlorine reduction to ozone recovery
steps.content:
Finally, we examine the ozone ($\mathrm{O}_3$) levels. Between 2000 and 2010, ozone levels were still declining (from $300$ to $280\text{ ppb}$), likely due to the lag time of older CFCs remaining in the atmosphere.

However, after 2010, as the concentrations of $\mathrm{Cl}_2\mathrm{O}_2$ and CFCs continued to fall, we see a clear **recovery** in ozone levels:
- 2010: $280\text{ ppb}$
- 2015: $295\text{ ppb}$
- 2020: $310\text{ ppb}$

This recovery happens because there are fewer chlorine radicals available to catalyze the breakdown of $\mathrm{O}_3$, allowing the natural dynamic equilibrium of the ozone layer to shift back toward higher concentrations.

steps.title: Summarize the inverse relationship
steps.content:
In summary, there is an **inverse relationship** between the trends. The continuous decrease in CFCs and the subsequent reduction in chlorine peroxide concentrations correlate with the eventual increase and recovery of ozone levels after 2010.

To earn full marks on this question, ensure you mention both the correlation between CFCs and chlorine radicals and how the reduction in these radicals allows the ozone concentration to recover.

steps.title: Analyze the CFC and chlorine peroxide trends
steps.content:
First, we look at the raw data in the table. From 2000 to 2020, both CFC concentrations ($\mathrm{CFCl}_3$ and $\mathrm{CF}_2\mathrm{Cl}_2$) show a consistent decrease. Specifically, from 2010 to 2020, the concentration of the intermediate compound $\mathrm{Cl}_2\mathrm{O}_2$ (chlorine peroxide) also drops from $2.1$ ppb to $1.5$ ppb.

This correlation suggests that the reduction in CFCs is directly linked to the amount of chlorine-containing intermediates in the stratosphere.

steps.title: Connect CFCs to chlorine radicals
steps.content:
CFCs act as the source of chlorine radicals ($\mathrm{Cl}^{\bullet}$) in the stratosphere. When CFCs are exposed to high-energy UV radiation, they undergo **homolytic fission**, a process covered in **Topic R3.3: Electron sharing reactions**.

As the concentrations of $\mathrm{CFCl}_3$ and $\mathrm{CF}_2\mathrm{Cl}_2$ decrease due to the Montreal Protocol, there are fewer molecules available to produce these highly reactive chlorine radicals.

steps.title: Interpret chlorine peroxide as evidence
steps.content:
The molecule $\mathrm{Cl}_2\mathrm{O}_2$ is a critical intermediate in the catalytic cycle that destroys ozone. Its formation depends on the presence of $\mathrm{Cl}^{\bullet}$ radicals. 

The decrease in $\mathrm{Cl}_2\mathrm{O}_2$ levels from 2010 onwards provides direct evidence that the abundance of chlorine radicals has declined. With fewer radicals available to drive the catalytic cycle, the mechanism of ozone destruction is effectively slowed down.

steps.title: Link to ozone recovery
steps.content:
Finally, we observe the trend in ozone ($\mathrm{O}_3$). As the rate of catalytic destruction decreases, the natural formation of ozone (from oxygen) begins to outpace its breakdown. 

From the data, we see $\mathrm{O}_3$ levels recover from a low of $280$ ppb in 2010 to $310$ ppb in 2020. This confirms that reduced CFC emissions have successfully allowed the ozone layer to start replenishing itself.

options.markscheme: Pentan-3-ol is a secondary alcohol. The hydroxyl ($-\text{OH}$) group is attached to the third carbon atom of a five-carbon chain, which is bonded to two other carbon atoms (C2 and C4).

2-methylbutan-2-ol is a tertiary alcohol. The carbon atom bonded to the hydroxyl group (C2) is also bonded to three other carbon atoms: the C1 methyl group, the C3 methylene group, and a separate methyl branch at the C2 position.
3-methylbutan-1-ol is a primary alcohol. Although there is branching further down the chain, the carbon atom bonded to the hydroxyl group (C1) is only bonded to one other carbon atom (C2).

2,2-dimethylpropan-1-ol is a primary alcohol. Even though the molecule contains a quaternary carbon at the second position, the carbon atom carrying the functional group (C1) is only attached to one other carbon (C2).

options.markscheme: The reaction follows Markovnikov's rule, where the electrophile ($\text{H}^+$) adds to the carbon of the double bond with the most hydrogen atoms. In 2-methylpent-2-ene ($\text{(CH}_3\text{)}_2\text{C=CHCH}_2\text{CH}_3$), C3 has one hydrogen atom while C2 has none. Addition of $\text{H}^+$ to C3 creates a tertiary carbocation at C2, which is more stable than the secondary carbocation that would form at C3. Subsequent nucleophilic attack by $\text{Cl}^-$ at C2 yields 2-chloro-2-methylpentane as the major product.

parts.content: Draw the skeletal formula of 2-methylbutane.
parts.markscheme:
- Draw a correct skeletal structure showing a four-carbon chain with a methyl group at the second carbon position. <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

parts.content:
Convert the following skeletal formula into a full structural formula showing all atoms and bonds.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/0bb2017d-f958-4ae9-b0cd-7d7461434c8d.jpeg)
parts.markscheme:
- Correct identification of the structure as butanoic acid (or showing a 4-carbon chain with a carboxyl group), $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{COOH}$. <marks>A1</marks>
- Correct full structural formula showing all atoms and bonds, including the $\text{C}=\text{O}$ double bond and the $\text{O}-\text{H}$ single bond. <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the carbon skeleton
steps.content:
To start our **Direct Skeletal Decoding**, we look at every vertex (corner) and every line end in the skeletal drawing. Each of these represents a carbon atom.

Looking at the image, we can count:
1. The left end of the line.
2. The first vertex (corner).
3. The second vertex (corner).
4. The carbon atom attached to the oxygen atoms on the right.

This gives us a total of **4 carbon atoms** in a straight chain. This is typical for a 'but-' prefix molecule.

steps.title: Expand the functional group
steps.content:
The right end of the molecule contains the functional group. In skeletal form, it shows a double bond to an oxygen and a single bond to an $OH$ group. 

To convert this to a **full structural formula**, we must show every single bond explicitly, including the bond between the oxygen and the hydrogen. This group is the carboxylic acid group:

$$\text{C=O}$$ and $$\text{C-O-H}$$

According to the markscheme, showing this specific detail is essential for the second mark.

highlights.text: full structural formula showing all atoms and bonds

steps.title: Apply the valency rule
steps.content:
Now we add the hydrogen atoms. Each carbon atom must have exactly **4 bonds** (the four-bond valency rule).

*   **Carbon 1 (right):** Already has 4 bonds ($2$ to $=O$, $1$ to $-OH$, and $1$ to the next Carbon). No H atoms needed.
*   **Carbon 2:** Has 2 bonds to adjacent carbons. It needs **2** H atoms to reach 4 bonds.
*   **Carbon 3:** Has 2 bonds to adjacent carbons. It needs **2** H atoms to reach 4 bonds.
*   **Carbon 4 (left end):** Has only 1 bond to Carbon 3. It needs **3** H atoms to reach 4 bonds.

steps.title: Draw the final formula
steps.content:
Combine the 4-carbon chain, the expanded carboxyl group, and the calculated hydrogen atoms into one drawing. Ensure every bond is shown as a line.

$$\begin{matrix} \text{H} & & \text{H} & & \text{H} & & \text{O} \\ | & & | & & | & & // \\ \text{H} - \text{C} & - & \text{C} & - & \text{C} & - & \text{C} \\ | & & | & & | & & \backslash \\ \text{H} & & \text{H} & & \text{H} & & \text{O} - \text{H} \end{matrix}$$

This represents **butanoic acid**. This complete drawing satisfies both requirements of the markscheme: the correct 4-carbon chain and the fully expanded functional group.

highlights.text: Correct full structural formula showing all atoms and bonds

steps.title: Analyze the carbon skeleton
steps.content:
To start our **IUPAC Identification and Reconstruction**, we first need to count the carbons in the skeletal formula. In a skeletal drawing, every end of a line and every intersection (bend) represents a carbon atom.

Looking at the provided image:
1. The far left end is the first carbon.
2. The first downward bend is the second carbon.
3. The next upward bend is the third carbon.
4. The carbon at the far right is the fourth carbon (which is double-bonded to an oxygen).

This gives us a total of **4 carbons** in the main chain. This falls under **Topic S3.2: Functional groups** in your syllabus, where we learn how to represent and name organic compounds.

steps.title: Identify the IUPAC name
steps.content:
Next, we identify the functional group to determine the IUPAC name:
- The rightmost carbon is part of a $-\text{COOH}$ group (a carboxyl group), evidenced by the double bond to oxygen ($=\text{O}$) and the single bond to a hydroxyl group ($-\text{OH}$).
- For a 4-carbon chain, the prefix is **but-**.
- For a carboxylic acid, the suffix is **-anoic acid**.

Combining these, the IUPAC name is **butanoic acid**. Identifying this structure correctly earns you the first mark.

highlights.text: Correct identification of the structure as butanoic acid

steps.title: Reconstruct the structural formula
steps.content:
Now we reconstruct the molecule from the name **butanoic acid** into a full structural formula. This means we must show **every single atom and every single bond**.

1. Draw the 4-carbon backbone: $\text{C}-\text{C}-\text{C}-\text{C}$.
2. On the first carbon, add the carboxyl group. Ensure you show the double bond to the oxygen and the single bond to the oxygen of the hydroxyl group.
3. **CRITICAL:** You must also show the single bond between the oxygen and the hydrogen ($-\text{O}-\text{H}$). Leaving this as "OH" is a common mistake that loses marks in IB exams!

highlights.text: showing all atoms and bonds, including the C=O double bond and the O-H single bond

steps.title: Complete with hydrogen atoms
steps.content:
Finally, fill in the remaining valencies for the other carbon atoms with hydrogen atoms. Remember that each carbon must have a total of **4 bonds**.

- The terminal methyl carbon needs 3 hydrogens.
- The two middle carbons (methylene groups) each need 2 hydrogens.

The final full structural formula is:

$$\begin{array}{ccccccc} & H & H & H & O & \\ & | & | & | & // & \\ H & - C & - C & - C & - C & \\ & | & | & | & \backslash & \\ & H & H & H & & O - H \end{array}$$

This complete drawing fulfills the requirements for the second mark.

parts.content: Evaluate the use of skeletal formulae in organic chemistry by discussing one advantage and one disadvantage.
parts.markscheme:
- **State** one advantage: simplifies representation of large or complex molecules OR is quicker and clearer for experienced chemists. <marks>R1</marks>
- **State** one disadvantage: omits $\text{C}$ and $\text{H}$ atoms explicitly OR can be confusing for beginners when identifying specific atoms. <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Introduction to Skeletal Representation
steps.content: In organic chemistry, **Topic S3.2.1** introduces various ways to represent molecules. Skeletal formulae are a specific style where carbon-carbon bonds are shown as lines, and carbon atoms are assumed to be at every vertex and end of a line. Unlike full structural formulas, most hydrogen atoms are not shown at all.

steps.title: Advantage: Enhanced Structural Clarity
steps.content: The main advantage of this layout is that the **simplification of the carbon backbone improves structural clarity**. In large or complex molecules, drawing every single atom creates a "cluttered" image. By using lines to show the skeleton, the overall shape and the position of important functional groups become much easier to see at a glance.

highlights.text: simplifies representation of large or complex molecules

steps.title: Disadvantage: Reduced Explicit Detail
steps.content: The disadvantage lies in the fact that the **omission of specific $\text{C}$ and $\text{H}$ atoms reduces explicit detail**. Because these atoms are only implied, a chemist must mentally calculate the number of hydrogens by remembering that each carbon must have four bonds. This lack of explicit labeling can be confusing for beginners or when trying to identify specific atoms quickly in a reaction mechanism.

highlights.text: omits C and H atoms explicitly

steps.title: Final Summary for Marks
steps.content:
To earn the 2 marks, ensure you clearly state one of each:

*   **Advantage:** It simplifies the representation of large/complex molecules, making them clearer to read.
*   **Disadvantage:** It omits $\text{C}$ and $\text{H}$ atoms explicitly, which can lead to confusion for those identifying specific atoms.

steps.title: Identify the Representation Style
steps.content: In organic chemistry, specifically within **Topic S3.2.1: Representation of organic compounds**, skeletal formulae are used to show the structure of a molecule without drawing every single atom. Carbon atoms are represented as the ends of lines or vertices (corners), and hydrogen atoms attached to carbons are usually omitted. To evaluate this method properly, we need to look at it through the eyes of different users.

steps.title: Advantage: Efficiency for Experts
steps.content:
From the perspective of an **experienced chemist**, the primary advantage is **efficiency and clarity**. 

Because skeletal formulae omit the repetitive $C$ and $H$ atoms, the "backbone" and functional groups of large, complex molecules (like those found in **Topic S2.4.4: Polymers**) become much easier to see quickly. It is significantly faster to draw and provides a less cluttered view of the molecule's overall shape.

highlights.text: simplifies representation of large or complex molecules OR is quicker and clearer for experienced chemists.

steps.title: Disadvantage: Confusion for Beginners
steps.content:
From the perspective of a **beginner**, the main disadvantage is the **risk of confusion**. 

Since carbon and hydrogen atoms are not explicitly shown, a novice might struggle to identify the correct number of atoms or understand the tetravalent nature of carbon. For example, remembering that a vertex represents a carbon with enough hidden hydrogens to total four bonds is a common hurdle when first learning **Topic S3.2: Functional groups**.

highlights.text: omits C and H atoms explicitly OR can be confusing for beginners when identifying specific atoms.

steps.title: Final Answer Summary
steps.content:
To earn both marks, you must state one clear advantage and one clear disadvantage based on the user experience:

*   **Advantage:** It simplifies the representation of large molecules and is much faster/clearer for experienced chemists to interpret.
*   **Disadvantage:** It omits $C$ and $H$ atoms explicitly, which can cause confusion for beginners trying to count atoms or identify the structure.

questions.specification:
Compounds utilized as fuels in internal combustion engines and portable heaters are often evaluated based on their physical properties and energy content. The selection of an appropriate fuel depends on factors such as its state at room temperature, volatility, and the energy released upon combustion.

Data for several alkanes and alcohols, which are common components or alternatives for transport fuels, are provided in the table below.

| Category | Compound | Formula | Molar Mass / $\text{g mol}^{-1}$ | Boiling Point / $^\circ\text{C}$ | $\Delta H_c^\ominus / \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Alkane | Ethane | $\text{C}_2\text{H}_6$ | 30.08 | -88.6 | -1560 |
| Alcohol | Methanol | $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$ | 32.05 | 64.7 | -726 |
| Alkane | Propane | $\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$ | 44.11 | -42.1 | -2220 |
| Alcohol | Ethanol | $\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}$ | 46.08 | 78.4 | -1367 |
| Alkane | Butane | $\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}$ | 58.14 | -0.5 | -2877 |
| Alcohol | Propan-1-ol | $\text{C}_3\text{H}_7\text{OH}$ | 60.11 | 97.2 | -2021 |

questions.specification:
Which of the following alcohols will cause *warm, acidified* potassium manganate(VII) solution to be decolourised (purple to colourless) when the oxidant is not in excess?

I. $\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2CH_2CH_2OH}$

II. $\mathrm{CH_3CH_2C(CH_3)_2OH}$

III. $\mathrm{(CH_3)_2CHCH(OH)CH_3}$

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 3. What are the mechanisms of chemical change? > R3.2 Electron transfer reactions > R3.2.9 Oxidation of organic compounds
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R3.2.9 Oxidation of organic compounds
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question tests which alcohols are oxidized by acidified potassium manganate(VII) (primary/secondary vs tertiary), i.e., oxidation behavior of organic functional groups.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 3. What are the mechanisms of chemical change? > R3.2 Electron transfer reactions > R3.2.1 Oxidation and reduction definitions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R3.2.1 Oxidation and reduction definitions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses redox chemistry via a color change of MnO4− in acidic solution, requiring understanding of oxidation/reduction in this reaction.

options.markscheme:
I is a primary alcohol and is oxidised by warm, acidified $\mathrm{KMnO_4}$, decolourising the solution.
II is a tertiary alcohol and is not readily oxidised under these conditions, so it does not decolourise the solution.

I is a primary alcohol and III is a secondary alcohol; both are oxidised by warm, acidified $\mathrm{KMnO_4}$, reducing $\mathrm{MnO_4^-}$ to $\mathrm{Mn^{2+}}$ (colourless), so the solution is decolourised.
II is a tertiary alcohol and is not oxidised under these conditions.
III is a secondary alcohol and is oxidised by warm, acidified $\mathrm{KMnO_4}$, but II is a tertiary alcohol and is not readily oxidised under these conditions, so this combination is not correct.
II is a tertiary alcohol and is not readily oxidised by warm, acidified $\mathrm{KMnO_4}$, so not all three alcohols will decolourise the solution.

options.markscheme: The molecule contains a carbonyl group (C=O) at the end of the carbon chain, which is characteristic of the aldehyde homologous series.
Ketones contain a carbonyl group bonded to two carbon atoms (internal), whereas this group is terminal.
Carboxylic acids require an -OH group bonded to the same carbon as the C=O group.
Alcohols contain a hydroxyl group (-OH) and do not have a double bond between carbon and oxygen.

questions.specification: An organic compound is analyzed to determine its empirical formula. It is composed of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen. Complete combustion of $1.50\text{ g}$ of the compound produces $3.30\text{ g}$ of carbon dioxide ($\text{CO}_2$) and $1.35\text{ g}$ of water ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$).

parts.content: Define the term empirical formula.
parts.markscheme: The empirical formula is the simplest whole-number ratio of atoms of each element in a compound. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Prerequisite Knowledge
steps.content: To answer this question, we look at **Topic S1.4: Counting particles by mass: The mole**, which covers the relationship between empirical and molecular formulas. While a molecular formula shows the actual number of atoms in a molecule, the empirical formula represents the fundamental ratio between them.

steps.title: Define Empirical Formula
steps.content:
The formal definition of an **empirical formula** is:

The simplest whole-number ratio of atoms of each element present in a compound.

This means that if you have a compound like glucose ($C_6H_{12}O_6$), its empirical formula is the reduced version, $CH_2O$.

steps.title: Awarding of Marks
steps.content:
In an IB exam, to earn the mark for this definition, you must include two specific criteria:
1. The ratio must be the **simplest**.
2. The ratio must consist of **whole numbers**.

You can refer to the ratio of "atoms" or "moles" of each element.

highlights.text: The empirical formula is the simplest whole-number ratio of atoms of each element in a compound.

parts.content: Calculate the moles of carbon and hydrogen present in the compound based on the combustion data.
parts.markscheme:
**Step 1: Moles of carbon in $\text{CO}_2$**

$$\text{Moles of } \text{CO}_2 = \frac{3.30\text{ g}}{44.01\text{ g mol}^{-1}} = 0.0750\text{ mol}$$

Since each $\text{CO}_2$ molecule contains one carbon atom:
$$\text{Moles of C} = 0.0750\text{ mol}$$ <marks>A1</marks>

**Step 2: Moles of hydrogen in $\text{H}_2\text{O}$**

$$\text{Moles of } \text{H}_2\text{O} = \frac{1.35\text{ g}}{18.02\text{ g mol}^{-1}} = 0.0749\text{ mol}$$

Since each $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ molecule contains two hydrogen atoms:
$$\text{Moles of H} = 0.0749\text{ mol} \times 2 = 0.150\text{ mol}$$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content:
To find the moles of carbon and hydrogen in the compound, we use the fact that all carbon in the compound ends up in $\text{CO}_2$ and all hydrogen ends up in $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ during complete combustion. We will use the molar mass of these products to work backward to the elements. 

This concept is a key part of **Topic S1.4: Counting particles by mass**, specifically regarding empirical formulas.

steps.title: Calculate moles of Carbon
steps.content:
First, we find the moles of $\text{CO}_2$ produced using the formula from the data booklet:

$$\text{moles } (n) = \frac{\text{mass } (m)}{\text{molar mass } (M)}$$

The molar mass of $\text{CO}_2$ is $44.01\text{ g mol}^{-1}$. Since there is $1$ atom of carbon in every molecule of $\text{CO}_2$, the moles of carbon will be equal to the moles of $\text{CO}_2$.

$$\begin{aligned} n(\text{CO}_2) &= \frac{3.30\text{ g}}{44.01\text{ g mol}^{-1}} \\ n(\text{C}) &= 0.0750\text{ mol} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the mass divided by the molar mass.

calculator.description: Calculate moles of carbon dioxide produced.

steps.title: Calculate moles of Hydrogen
steps.content:
Next, we find the moles of $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ produced. The molar mass of $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ is $18.02\text{ g mol}^{-1}$. 

Crucially, each molecule of $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ contains **two** hydrogen atoms. Therefore, the moles of hydrogen ($n(\text{H})$) is double the moles of water:

$$\begin{aligned} n(\text{H}_2\text{O}) &= \frac{1.35\text{ g}}{18.02\text{ g mol}^{-1}} \approx 0.0749\text{ mol} \\ n(\text{H}) &= 0.0749\text{ mol} \times 2 \\ n(\text{H}) &= 0.150\text{ mol} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Divide the mass by molar mass, then multiply the result by 2.

desmos.instructions: Input (1.35 / 18.02) * 2 to get the total moles of hydrogen.

calculator.description: Calculate the moles of hydrogen atoms by multiplying the moles of water by 2.

steps.title: Final Answer Summary
steps.content:
Based on the Direct Molar Conversion method:
- **Moles of C:** $0.0750\text{ mol}$
- **Moles of H:** $0.150\text{ mol}$

In an IB exam, you earn 1 mark for each correctly calculated molar value, provided you show the 1:1 ratio for Carbon and the 2:1 ratio for Hydrogen.

steps.title: Calculate mass of Carbon
steps.content:
To find the mass of carbon in the compound, we use the mass fraction of carbon in $\text{CO}_2$. Since carbon has an atomic mass of $12.01\text{ g mol}^{-1}$ and $\text{CO}_2$ has a molar mass of $44.01\text{ g mol}^{-1}$, the fraction of mass that is carbon is $\frac{12.01}{44.01}$.

$$\text{mass of C} = 3.30\text{ g} \times \frac{12.01}{44.01} \approx 0.901\text{ g}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 3.30 * (12.01 / 44.01) and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the mass of carbon from the mass of carbon dioxide.

steps.title: Convert Carbon to moles
steps.content:
Now, we convert the mass of carbon into moles using the formula from the data booklet:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

$$\text{moles of C} = \frac{0.901\text{ g}}{12.01\text{ g mol}^{-1}} = 0.0750\text{ mol}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 0.900545 / 12.01 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of moles of carbon.

steps.title: Calculate mass of Hydrogen
steps.content:
Next, we find the mass of hydrogen using its mass fraction in $\text{H}_2\text{O}$. Since there are two hydrogen atoms ($2 \times 1.01 = 2.02\text{ g mol}^{-1}$) in water ($18.02\text{ g mol}^{-1}$), the fraction is $\frac{2.02}{18.02}$.

$$\text{mass of H} = 1.35\text{ g} \times \frac{2.02}{18.02} \approx 0.151\text{ g}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 1.35 * (2.02 / 18.02) and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the mass of hydrogen from the mass of water.

steps.title: Convert Hydrogen to moles
steps.content:
Finally, we convert the mass of hydrogen into moles. Note that we divide by the atomic mass of H ($1.01\text{ g mol}^{-1}$) because the empirical formula is based on the ratio of individual atoms.

$$\text{moles of H} = \frac{0.151\text{ g}}{1.01\text{ g mol}^{-1}} = 0.150\text{ mol}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 0.15133 / 1.01 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of moles of hydrogen.

parts.content: Determine the mass of oxygen in the organic compound.
parts.markscheme:
**Mass of carbon:**
$$\text{Mass of C} = 0.0750\text{ mol} \times 12.01\text{ g mol}^{-1} = 0.901\text{ g}$$

**Mass of hydrogen:**
$$\text{Mass of H} = 0.150\text{ mol} \times 1.008\text{ g mol}^{-1} = 0.151\text{ g}$$

**Mass of oxygen:**
$$\text{Mass of O} = 1.50\text{ g} - (0.901\text{ g} + 0.151\text{ g}) = 0.448\text{ g}$$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Calculate mass of Carbon
steps.content:
First, we need to find the mass of carbon in the original sample. All the carbon from the compound ends up in the $3.30\text{ g}$ of $\text{CO}_2$. We use the formula for moles from the data booklet:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

1. Find moles of $\text{CO}_2$: 
$$n(\text{CO}_2) = \frac{3.30\text{ g}}{44.01\text{ g mol}^{-1}} \approx 0.0750\text{ mol}$$

2. Since each $\text{CO}_2$ molecule has one carbon atom, $n(\text{C}) = 0.0750\text{ mol}$. Now convert back to mass:
$$\text{Mass of C} = 0.0750\text{ mol} \times 12.01\text{ g mol}^{-1} = 0.901\text{ g}$$

ti84.instructions: Divide 3.30 by 44.01 to get moles, then multiply by 12.01.

calculator.description: Calculate mass of carbon from CO2

steps.title: Calculate mass of Hydrogen
steps.content:
Next, we find the mass of hydrogen. All the hydrogen from the compound ends up in the $1.35\text{ g}$ of $\text{H}_2\text{O}$.

1. Find moles of $\text{H}_2\text{O}$:
$$n(\text{H}_2\text{O}) = \frac{1.35\text{ g}}{18.02\text{ g mol}^{-1}} \approx 0.0750\text{ mol}$$

2. Since each water molecule has **two** hydrogen atoms, the moles of H atoms is double:
$$n(\text{H}) = 2 \times 0.0750\text{ mol} = 0.150\text{ mol}$$

3. Convert to mass:
$$\text{Mass of H} = 0.150\text{ mol} \times 1.008\text{ g mol}^{-1} = 0.151\text{ g}$$

ti84.instructions: Divide 1.35 by 18.02, multiply by 2, then multiply by 1.008.

calculator.description: Calculate mass of hydrogen from H2O

steps.title: Subtract to find Oxygen
steps.content:
Since the organic compound is only composed of C, H, and O, we can find the mass of oxygen by subtracting the masses of C and H from the total mass of the compound ($1.50\text{ g}$).

$$\text{Mass of O} = \text{Total mass} - (\text{Mass of C} + \text{Mass of H})$$
$$\text{Mass of O} = 1.50\text{ g} - (0.901\text{ g} + 0.151\text{ g})$$
$$\text{Mass of O} = 0.448\text{ g}$$

This logic follows **Topic S1.4: Counting particles by mass**, where we use mass conservation to identify unknown components.

ti84.instructions: Subtract the sum of 0.901 and 0.151 from 1.50.

calculator.description: Subtract C and H masses from total mass

highlights.text: \text{Mass of O} = 1.50\text{ g} - (0.901\text{ g} + 0.151\text{ g}) = 0.448\text{ g}

steps.title: Calculate mass of carbon
steps.content:
To find the mass of carbon in the original compound, we use the mass fraction of carbon in $\text{CO}_2$. 

The molar mass of $\text{CO}_2$ is $12.01 + 2(16.00) = 44.01\text{ g mol}^{-1}$. 

$$\text{Mass of C} = \text{Mass of } \text{CO}_2 \times \frac{\text{Molar Mass of C}}{\text{Molar Mass of } \text{CO}_2}$$

$$\text{Mass of C} = 3.30\text{ g} \times \frac{12.01}{44.01} = 0.901\text{ g}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 3.30 * (12.01 / 44.01) into the main screen.

calculator.description: Calculate mass of carbon from CO2

steps.title: Calculate mass of hydrogen
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the mass of hydrogen using the mass fraction of hydrogen in $\text{H}_2\text{O}$. 

The molar mass of $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ is $2(1.008) + 16.00 = 18.016\text{ g mol}^{-1}$. Note that there are 2 moles of hydrogen per mole of water.

$$\text{Mass of H} = \text{Mass of } \text{H}_2\text{O} \times \frac{2 \times \text{Molar Mass of H}}{\text{Molar Mass of } \text{H}_2\text{O}}$$

$$\text{Mass of H} = 1.35\text{ g} \times \frac{2.016}{18.016} = 0.151\text{ g}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 1.35 * (2.016 / 18.016) into the main screen.

calculator.description: Calculate mass of hydrogen from H2O

steps.title: Calculate mass of oxygen
steps.content:
Since the compound only contains carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen, the mass of oxygen is the remaining mass of the $1.50\text{ g}$ sample after subtracting the carbon and hydrogen.

$$\text{Mass of O} = \text{Total Mass} - (\text{Mass of C} + \text{Mass of H})$$

$$\text{Mass of O} = 1.50\text{ g} - (0.901\text{ g} + 0.151\text{ g}) = 0.448\text{ g}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 1.50 - (0.901 + 0.151) into the main screen.

calculator.description: Calculate mass of oxygen by subtraction

parts.content: Calculate the empirical formula of the organic compound.
parts.markscheme:
**Step 1: Moles of each element**
*   C: $0.0750\text{ mol}$
*   H: $0.150\text{ mol}$
*   O: $\frac{0.448\text{ g}}{16.00\text{ g mol}^{-1}} = 0.0280\text{ mol}$ <marks>A1</marks>

**Step 2: Divide by the smallest number of moles ($0.0280$)**
*   C: $\frac{0.0750}{0.0280} = 2.68$
*   H: $\frac{0.150}{0.0280} = 5.36$
*   O: $\frac{0.0280}{0.0280} = 1$ <marks>A1</marks>

**Step 3: Convert to whole numbers**
Multiply by 3:
*   C: $2.68 \times 3 \approx 8$
*   H: $5.36 \times 3 \approx 16$
*   O: $1 \times 3 = 3$

Empirical formula: $\text{C}_8\text{H}_{16}\text{O}_3$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Calculate mass of Carbon
steps.content:
Using the mass and mole analysis approach, we first find the mass of carbon from the $3.30\text{ g}$ of $\text{CO}_2$ produced. Since all the carbon in the original sample ends up in the $\text{CO}_2$, we use the mass ratio of C to $\text{CO}_2$:

$$\text{Mass of C} = \text{mass of CO}_2 \times \frac{M_r(\text{C})}{M_r(\text{CO}_2)}$$

$$\text{Mass of C} = 3.30\text{ g} \times \frac{12.01}{44.01} = 0.9006\text{ g}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply the mass of CO2 by the fraction of carbon's molar mass over total CO2 molar mass.

ti84.instructions: Enter the multiplication of the mass by the molar mass ratio.

desmos.instructions: Type the calculation directly to find the mass of carbon.

calculator.description: Calculate the mass of carbon in the CO2 sample.

steps.title: Calculate mass of Hydrogen
steps.content:
Next, find the mass of hydrogen from the $1.35\text{ g}$ of water produced. Note that there are two hydrogen atoms in every water molecule ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$):

$$\text{Mass of H} = \text{mass of H}_2\text{O} \times \frac{2 \times M_r(\text{H})}{M_r(\text{H}_2\text{O})}$$

$$\text{Mass of H} = 1.35\text{ g} \times \frac{2.02}{18.02} = 0.1513\text{ g}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the fraction of hydrogen in H2O and multiply by the total water mass.

ti84.instructions: Multiply the mass of water by the hydrogen mass ratio.

desmos.instructions: Multiply 1.35 by the ratio of (2*1.01) to 18.02.

calculator.description: Calculate the mass of hydrogen in the water sample.

steps.title: Calculate mass of Oxygen
steps.content:
The compound is made of C, H, and O. To find the mass of oxygen, subtract the masses of C and H from the initial sample mass of $1.50\text{ g}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Mass of O} &= \text{Total mass} - (\text{Mass C} + \text{Mass H}) \\ \text{Mass of O} &= 1.50\text{ g} - (0.9006\text{ g} + 0.1513\text{ g}) \\ \text{Mass of O} &= 0.4481\text{ g} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Subtract the found element masses from the sample mass.

desmos.instructions: Evaluate 1.50 minus the sum of the previously calculated C and H masses.

calculator.description: Subtract the masses of C and H from the total mass.

steps.title: Convert masses to moles
steps.content:
Now, convert these masses into moles using the formula from the data booklet:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

$$\begin{aligned} n_C &= \frac{0.9006}{12.01} = 0.0750\text{ mol} \\ n_H &= \frac{0.1513}{1.01} = 0.150\text{ mol} \\ n_O &= \frac{0.4481}{16.00} = 0.0280\text{ mol} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Determine simple molar ratio
steps.content:
Divide each amount by the smallest number of moles ($0.0280$):

$$\begin{aligned} \text{C} &: \frac{0.0750}{0.0280} = 2.68 \\ \text{H} &: \frac{0.150}{0.0280} = 5.36 \\ \text{O} &: \frac{0.0280}{0.0280} = 1.00 \end{aligned}$$

desmos.instructions: Find the ratio of C and H relative to O.

calculator.description: Divide moles of each element by the smallest value.

steps.title: Convert to whole numbers
steps.content:
Since $2.68$ and $5.36$ are not whole numbers, we need to find a multiplier. Notice that $2.68 \approx 2\frac{2}{3} = \frac{8}{3}$. Multiplying by $3$ will give us whole numbers:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{C} &: 2.68 \times 3 \approx 8 \\ \text{H} &: 5.36 \times 3 \approx 16 \\ \text{O} &: 1 \times 3 = 3 \end{aligned}$$

Thus, the empirical formula is $\text{C}_8\text{H}_{16}\text{O}_3$.

gdc.instructions: Scale the ratio to whole numbers.

desmos.instructions: Verify the result of multiplying the ratios by 3.

calculator.description: Multiply ratios by 3 to reach integers.

steps.title: Calculate percentage of Carbon
steps.content:
First, we find the mass of Carbon in the produced $\text{CO}_2$. Since all the Carbon in the original compound ends up in $\text{CO}_2$, we use the molar mass ratio ($M_{\text{C}} = 12.01\text{ g mol}^{-1}$ and $M_{\text{CO}_2} = 44.01\text{ g mol}^{-1}$):

$$\text{Mass of C} = 3.30\text{ g} \times \frac{12.01}{44.01} = 0.9005\text{ g}$$

Now, convert this to a percentage of the original $1.50\text{ g}$ compound:

$$\%\text{C} = \left( \frac{0.9005\text{ g}}{1.50\text{ g}} \right) \times 100 = 60.03\%$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the mass of C using 3.30 * (12.01 / 44.01). Calculate the percentage using (Ans / 1.50) * 100.

ti84.instructions: Enter 3.30 * (12.01 / 44.01) to find the mass of C. Then divide that result by 1.50 and multiply by 100.

desmos.instructions: Input the expression to find the mass percentage directly.

calculator.description: Calculate mass and percentage of Carbon

steps.title: Calculate percentage of Hydrogen
steps.content:
Next, find the mass of Hydrogen in the produced $\text{H}_2\text{O}$. There are 2 Hydrogen atoms per water molecule ($M_{\text{H}} = 1.01\text{ g mol}^{-1}$ and $M_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 18.02\text{ g mol}^{-1}$):

$$\text{Mass of H} = 1.35\text{ g} \times \frac{2 \times 1.01}{18.02} = 0.1513\text{ g}$$

Convert this to a percentage of the original $1.50\text{ g}$ compound:

$$\%\text{H} = \left( \frac{0.1513\text{ g}}{1.50\text{ g}} \right) \times 100 = 10.09\%$$

gdc.instructions: Find the mass of H: 1.35 * (2.02 / 18.02). Then calculate percentage: (Ans / 1.50) * 100.

ti84.instructions: Enter 1.35 * (2.02 / 18.02) to find the mass of H. Divide by 1.50 and multiply by 100.

desmos.instructions: Evaluate the expression for percentage of hydrogen.

calculator.description: Calculate mass and percentage of Hydrogen

steps.title: Find percentage of Oxygen
steps.content:
Since the compound only contains C, H, and O, the remaining percentage must be Oxygen:

$$\%\text{O} = 100\% - \%\text{C} - \%\text{H}$$
$$\%\text{O} = 100 - 60.03 - 10.09 = 29.88\%$$

ti84.instructions: Subtract the previous percentages from 100.

calculator.description: Find the remaining percentage for Oxygen

steps.title: Determine molar ratios
steps.content:
To find the empirical formula, we treat these percentages as masses in a $100\text{ g}$ sample and convert to moles using the formula from the data booklet:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

$$\begin{aligned} n(\text{C}) &= \frac{60.03}{12.01} = 4.998 \text{ mol} \\ n(\text{H}) &= \frac{10.09}{1.01} = 9.990 \text{ mol} \\ n(\text{O}) &= \frac{29.88}{16.00} = 1.868 \text{ mol} \end{aligned}$$

Now, divide each by the smallest number of moles ($1.868$):

$$\begin{aligned} \text{C:} & \frac{4.998}{1.868} \approx 2.68 \\ \text{H:} & \frac{9.990}{1.868} \approx 5.35 \\ \text{O:} & \frac{1.868}{1.868} = 1 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Find the mole values, then divide by the smallest one to find the ratio.

ti84.instructions: Divide each percentage by its molar mass to get moles. Then divide those results by the smallest value (approx 1.868).

desmos.instructions: Divide the calculated moles to find the ratio for C and H.

calculator.description: Calculate molar ratios

steps.title: Scale to whole numbers
steps.content:
The ratios $2.68 : 5.35 : 1$ are not whole numbers. Since $0.68$ is approximately $2/3$, we multiply the entire ratio by $3$ to obtain whole numbers:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{C:} & 2.68 \times 3 \approx 8 \\ \text{H:} & 5.35 \times 3 \approx 16 \\ \text{O:} & 1 \times 3 = 3 \end{aligned}$$

The empirical formula is $\text{C}_8\text{H}_{16}\text{O}_3$.

ti84.instructions: Multiply the ratios by 3 to verify they reach whole numbers.

calculator.description: Final scaling to whole numbers

parts.content: Explain how molecular formula differs from empirical formula, and describe one method to determine the molecular formula from experimental data.
parts.markscheme:
**Definition**
The molecular formula represents the actual number of atoms of each element in a molecule, while the empirical formula gives the simplest whole-number ratio of the elements. <marks>A1</marks>

**Determination method**
*   Measure the compound's molar mass experimentally (e.g., using mass spectrometry). <marks>A1</marks>
*   Divide the experimental molar mass by the molar mass of the empirical formula to find the integer multiplier and multiply the empirical formula by this factor. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Define Formula Types
steps.content:
To start, we need to distinguish between these two fundamental chemistry terms. 

*   **Empirical Formula**: This is the simplest whole-number ratio of the atoms of each element in a compound. 
*   **Molecular Formula**: This represents the actual number of atoms of each element present in one molecule of the compound.

This concept is covered in **Topic S1.4.4: Empirical and molecular formulas**. Think of the empirical formula as the "reduced" version of the molecular formula.

highlights.text: The molecular formula represents the actual number of atoms of each element in a molecule, while the empirical formula gives the simplest whole-number ratio of the elements.

steps.title: Determine Experimental Molar Mass
steps.content:
To find the molecular formula from an empirical one, we must determine the compound's actual molar mass experimentally. 

A standard method for this is **mass spectrometry** (covered in **Topic S3.2.8**). When a sample is analyzed, we look for the **molecular ion peak** ($M^+$), which corresponds to the entire molecule losing one electron. The mass-to-charge ratio ($m/z$) of this peak gives us the relative molecular mass ($M_r$) of the compound.

highlights.text: Measure the compound's molar mass experimentally (e.g., using mass spectrometry).

steps.title: Calculate the Multiplier
steps.content:
Once we have the experimental molar mass and the mass of the empirical formula, we find the integer multiplier ($n$).

We use the formula:

$$n = \frac{\text{Molar Mass (from mass spectrometry)}}{\text{Molar Mass (of empirical formula)}}$$

This $n$ value tells us how many empirical units fit into the actual molecule.

highlights.text: Divide the experimental molar mass by the molar mass of the empirical formula to find the integer multiplier

steps.title: Apply the Multiplier
steps.content:
The final step is to multiply all the subscripts in the empirical formula by the integer $n$ we just calculated. 

$$\text{Molecular Formula} = n \times (\text{Empirical Formula})$$

According to the IB markscheme, clearly stating this relationship and how to find $n$ is essential for earning full marks.

highlights.text: multiply the empirical formula by this factor.

steps.title: Contrast the formulas
steps.content:
The **empirical formula** is the simplest whole-number ratio of atoms of each element in a compound. In contrast, the **molecular formula** represents the actual number of atoms of each element present in one molecule of the compound.

This is a fundamental concept in **Topic S1.4: Counting particles by mass**.

highlights.text: The molecular formula represents the actual number of atoms of each element in a molecule, while the empirical formula gives the simplest whole-number ratio of the elements.

steps.title: Experimental molar mass determination
steps.content:
To find the molecular formula, we first need the experimental molar mass ($M$). Using the **Ideal Gas Law**, we can vaporize a known mass ($m$) of the compound and measure its pressure ($P$), volume ($V$), and temperature ($T$). 

From the data booklet, we use the ideal gas equation:

$$PV = nRT$$

Since the number of moles is defined as $n = \frac{m}{M}$, we can substitute and rearrange to solve for the molar mass:

$$\begin{aligned} PV &= \frac{m}{M}RT \\ M &= \frac{mRT}{PV} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the molecular formula
steps.content:
Once the experimental molar mass is determined, compare it to the molar mass of the empirical formula ($M_{\text{empirical}}$). 

Find the integer multiplier ($k$) using this ratio:

$$k = \frac{M_{\text{experimental}}}{M_{\text{empirical}}}$$

Finally, multiply all subscripts in the empirical formula by $k$ to obtain the molecular formula.

parts.content: Outline two products which would form as a result of incomplete combustion.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>A1</marks> for each of the following (max 2 marks):
*   Carbon monoxide / $\text{CO}$
*   Soot / carbon particles / $\text{C}$

steps.title: Define incomplete combustion
steps.content:
In chemistry, **combustion** is the reaction of a fuel with oxygen. While complete combustion occurs when oxygen is in excess (producing $\text{CO}_2$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$), **incomplete combustion** occurs when the oxygen supply is limited.

This topic is covered in **Reactivity 1.3: Energy from fuels**, specifically section **R1.3.2 Incomplete combustion**.

steps.title: Identify carbon monoxide
steps.content:
When there is not enough oxygen to fully oxidize the carbon atoms to carbon dioxide ($\text{CO}_2$), one characteristic product is **carbon monoxide** ($$CO$$).

Carbon monoxide is a colorless, odorless, and toxic gas. In an IB exam, you can provide either the name or the chemical formula to earn the mark.

steps.title: Identify soot (carbon)
steps.content:
If the oxygen supply is extremely limited, the carbon atoms may not be oxidized at all, resulting in the formation of solid **carbon** ($$C$$), commonly referred to as **soot**.

Soot appears as black smoke or a powdery deposit during the reaction. Just like with carbon monoxide, either the name "soot", "carbon particles", or the symbol "C" is acceptable.

steps.title: Final Answer Summary
steps.content:
To earn the 2 marks available for this question, you should list the two carbon-containing products that differ from those of complete combustion:

1.  **Carbon monoxide** (or $$\text{CO}$$)
2.  **Soot** (or **carbon** / $$\text{C}$$)

Note: While water ($$\text{H}_2\text{O}$$) is also a product of incomplete combustion, examiners typically look for the carbon-based products ($$\text{CO}$$ or $$\text{C}$$) because they are specific to the "incomplete" process.

steps.title: Define incomplete combustion
steps.content:
Incomplete combustion occurs when there is an insufficient supply of oxygen ($\text{O}_2$) to fully oxidize the carbon in the fuel to carbon dioxide ($\text{CO}_2$). This concept is covered in **Reactivity 1.3: Energy from fuels (R1.3.2)**. 

When the oxygen-to-fuel ratio drops, the carbon atoms form partially oxidized species or remain unoxidized.

steps.title: Derive product 1: CO
steps.content:
If we consider a simple carbohydrate-like compound with the empirical formula $\text{CH}_2\text{O}$ (consistent with the masses provided in the problem), we can compare the oxygen requirements.

For **complete combustion**, the ratio is 1:1:
$$\text{CH}_2\text{O} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

If the oxygen supply is limited (e.g., a 1:0.5 ratio), the carbon is only partially oxidized to **carbon monoxide**:
$$\text{CH}_2\text{O} + 0.5\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

steps.title: Derive product 2: C
steps.content:
When the oxygen supply is extremely limited, the oxygen available may only be enough to react with the hydrogen to form water, leaving the carbon unreacted. 

In this scenario, solid **carbon** (often seen as soot) is produced:
$$\text{CH}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Note that even without adding external $\text{O}_2$, the oxygen already present within the organic compound's structure can facilitate the formation of water, leaving elemental carbon behind.

steps.title: Identify final products
steps.content:
Based on the chemical equations derived for limited oxygen conditions, the two products formed are:

1.  **Carbon monoxide** ($\text{CO}$)
2.  **Carbon** ($\text{C}$) / soot / carbon particles

Each correct identification earns 1 mark according to the markscheme.

parts.content: State one major problem during combustion experiments.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>A1</marks> for any of the following:
*   Formation of soot/carbon particles (due to incomplete combustion)
*   Formation of carbon monoxide (toxic gas)
*   Difficulty in ensuring complete combustion
*   Difficulty in collecting all gaseous products / gas leaks

steps.title: Ideal combustion assumption
steps.content: In an ideal combustion analysis experiment, we assume that every single atom of carbon in the original $1.50\text{ g}$ sample reacts fully with oxygen to produce carbon dioxide ($\text{CO}_2$). Similarly, we assume all hydrogen atoms form water ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$). This complete reaction is essential for accurately calculating the empirical formula, as covered in **Topic S1.4.4: Empirical and molecular formulas**.

steps.title: Identifying incomplete combustion
steps.content: In practice, it is often very difficult to ensure that the reaction goes to 100% completion. This is known as **incomplete combustion**, which usually occurs if the oxygen supply is insufficient or the temperature is not high enough. This concept is explored further in **Topic R1.3.2: Incomplete combustion**.

steps.title: Formation of by-products
steps.content:
When incomplete combustion occurs, carbon atoms do not all turn into $\text{CO}_2$. Instead, some may form:

*   **Carbon monoxide ($\text{CO}$)**: A colorless, odorless, and toxic gas.
*   **Soot ($\text{C}$)**: Fine black particles of solid carbon.

Because these products are not measured as $\text{CO}_2$, the amount of carbon in the compound will be underestimated, leading to an incorrect empirical formula.

steps.title: State the problem
steps.content: A major problem in these experiments is the formation of soot or carbon monoxide due to the difficulty in ensuring complete combustion.

highlights.text: Formation of soot/carbon particles (due to incomplete combustion)

steps.title: Analyze the experimental goal
steps.content: In a combustion analysis experiment, we calculate the empirical formula based on the masses of $\text{CO}_2$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ produced. This method relies on the assumption that **all** the carbon in the sample is converted into carbon dioxide and **all** the hydrogen is converted into water. Any physical or technical issue that prevents this will lead to an incorrect result.

highlights.text: State one major problem during combustion experiments.

steps.title: Incomplete combustion limitations
steps.content:
A primary technical difficulty is **incomplete combustion**. This occurs if there is insufficient oxygen or if the temperature isn't high enough. 

Instead of forming only $\text{CO}_2$, some carbon may form:
*   **Carbon monoxide (CO)**: A colorless gas that escapes detection if only $\text{CO}_2$ is being measured.
*   **Soot (C)**: Solid carbon particles that remain in the combustion tube.

If this happens, the measured mass of $\text{CO}_2$ will be lower than the true value, causing an error in the carbon-to-hydrogen ratio. You can review this concept in **Topic R1.3: Energy from fuels**.

steps.title: Physical collection difficulties
steps.content:
Another procedural limitation involves the **collection of gaseous products**. In a laboratory setup, gases like $\text{CO}_2$ and water vapor must be passed through absorption tubes. 

Common physical issues include:
*   **Gas leaks**: Small gaps in the apparatus allow the produced gases to escape before they reach the weighing scale.
*   **Incomplete absorption**: The chemicals used to trap the gases (like anhydrous $\text{CaCl}_2$ or $\text{NaOH}$) might not absorb 100% of the products passing through.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
To earn the mark, you only need to state one of these problems. 

One major problem is the **formation of soot or carbon monoxide due to incomplete combustion**.

questions.specification:
Which row in the table correctly matches the condensed structural formula of an organic compound to its functional group class?

| | Structural Formula | Functional Group Class |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| A | $CH_3CHO$ | Ketone |
| B | $CH_3COOCH_3$ | Ester |
| C | $CH_3OCH_2CH_3$ | Alcohol |
| D | $CH_3CONH_2$ | Amine |

options.markscheme: Row A is incorrect: $CH_3CHO$ is an aldehyde (ethanal), not a ketone.
Row B is correct: $CH_3COOCH_3$ contains the ester functional group ($RCOOR'$).
Row C is incorrect: $CH_3OCH_2CH_3$ is an ether ($R-O-R'$), not an alcohol.
Row D is incorrect: $CH_3CONH_2$ is an amide ($RCONH_2$), not an amine.
